text: To classify if an input is attempting to bypass safety alignments through roleplay or cipher encodings, gateways run {{c1::adversarial jailbreak detection}}.
explanation: Adversarial jailbreaks use complex wrapper prompts (like 'Do Anything Now' - DAN) or base64 encodings to bypass the LLM's safety filters. Jailbreak detectors evaluate the structural syntax of inputs to intercept these patterns.
usage: Jailbreak detection filters run in parallel with the user prompt token parsing step.
spanish: Para clasificar si una entrada está intentando eludir las alineaciones de seguridad a través de juegos de rol o codificaciones cifradas, las pasarelas ejecutan la detección de evasión de restricciones adversarias (adversarial jailbreak detection).

text: To protect costly model endpoints from denial-of-service (DoS) abuse and control costs, firewalls enforce {{c1::rate limiting}} on API tokens.
explanation: Rate limiting restricts the number of API requests or total tokens processed per user within a specific timeframe (e.g. 1000 tokens per minute), throwing a 429 Too Many Requests error if exceeded.
usage: Implemented at the gateway using algorithms like Token Bucket or Leaky Bucket with Redis backends.
spanish: Para proteger los costosos puntos finales del modelo del abuso de denegación de servicio (DoS) y controlar los costos, los cortafuegos aplican limitación de velocidad (rate limiting) en los tokens de la API.

text: Instead of extracting plain text from complex documents, multimodal RAG uses models like {{c1::ColPali}} to generate vector embeddings directly from {{c2::rendered document images}}.
explanation: Traditional RAG struggles with charts, tables, and PDFs because the parsing step loses structural layouts. ColPali represents pages as images and extracts semantic visual-textual patches, embedding them directly into vector spaces for highly accurate visual document retrieval.
usage: Highly recommended for reading financial statements, engineering blueprints, and slides.
spanish: En lugar de extraer texto plano de documentos complejos, el RAG multimodal utiliza modelos como ColPali para generar embeddings vectoriales directamente a partir de imágenes renderizadas de los documentos.

text: Industrial RAG applications implement hybrid search by combining semantic {{c1::dense vector retrieval}} with keyword-based {{c2::sparse BM25 retrieval}} and merging results using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF).
explanation: Dense vectors capture general concept meanings but miss exact keyword match strings (like serial codes or product names). Sparse retrieval (BM25) targets keyword precision. Combining both via RRF yields optimal search recall and accuracy.
usage: Standard production search architecture for enterprise wikis and code search tools.
spanish: Las aplicaciones RAG industriales implementan búsquedas híbridas combinando la recuperación de vectores densos con la recuperación de palabras clave BM25, uniendo los resultados mediante RRF.

text: To capture global, high-level summaries of massive text datasets, {{c1::GraphRAG}} builds knowledge graphs using LLMs and clusters them with algorithms like {{c2::Leiden community detection}}.
explanation: GraphRAG extracts structured entities and relations from texts to construct a Knowledge Graph. It then groups nodes into hierarchical communities. When a user asks a global query (e.g., 'What are the main issues in this repository?'), the model summarizes the clusters instead of performing keyword searches.
usage: Perfect for broad corpus summaries, compliance audits, and architectural mapping.
spanish: Para capturar resúmenes globales de conjuntos de datos masivos, GraphRAG construye gráficos de conocimiento con LLMs y los agrupa mediante algoritmos como la detección de comunidades de Leiden.

text: {{c1::Corrective RAG (CRAG)}} adds a self-correction loop where a evaluator agent assesses search result quality; if quality falls below a threshold, it triggers a fallback search using {{c2::external web APIs}}.
explanation: If retrieved documents are irrelevant, traditional RAG outputs incorrect answers (hallucinations). CRAG inserts a logic gate: if the retrieved context is classified as 'incorrect' or 'irrelevant', the system triggers Tavily or Google Search to fetch verified web documentation.
usage: Critical for dynamic, high-stakes support pipelines where stale documentation causes system failures.
spanish: Corrective RAG (CRAG) añade un ciclo de autoconrección donde un agente evaluador califica los resultados de búsqueda; si la calidad es baja, activa una búsqueda alternativa en APIs web externas.

text: To prevent context loss, {{c1::Semantic Chunking}} splits documents by measuring the {{c2::cosine distance}} of embeddings between consecutive sentences, creating boundaries when semantic flow shifts.
explanation: Traditional chunking splits documents by arbitrary token counts, often cutting sentences or paragraphs in half. Semantic chunking calculates embeddings for every sentence and groups them into a chunk until the semantic similarity between adjacent blocks drops below a threshold.
usage: Essential for preprocessing lengthy technical guidelines, legal contracts, and novels.
spanish: Para evitar la pérdida de contexto, el fragmentado semántico divide los documentos midiendo la distancia de coseno entre oraciones consecutivas, creando límites cuando cambia el flujo semántico.

text: To improve document retrieval recall by generating multiple variations of the user's query, RAG systems run {{c1::Query Expansion}}.
explanation: Query Expansion uses an LLM to rewrite a single query into 3-5 variations or sub-queries. This handles different wordings and synonyms, searching the vector database with all variations to yield a broader set of relevant documents.
usage: Common Query Expansion prompt: Generate 3 alternative search queries for: [query].
spanish: Para mejorar la recuperación de documentos generando múltiples variaciones del prompt del usuario, los sistemas RAG ejecutan la expansión de consultas (Query Expansion).

text: To re-evaluate and sort the top retrieved vector search documents based on semantic relevance, RAG pipelines implement {{c1::cross-encoder reranking}}.
explanation: While dense vector search (bi-encoders) is fast but approximate, cross-encoders compute joint attention over the query and document together. Running a reranker on the top 20 retrieved chunks ensures only the most relevant are sent to the LLM.
usage: Popular rerankers include Cohere Rerank and BGE-Reranker.
spanish: Para reevaluar y clasificar los documentos de búsqueda vectorial mejor recuperados según su relevancia semántica, las canalizaciones RAG implementan la reclasificación por codificador cruzado (cross-encoder reranking).

text: To search on small, precise vector chunks but feed the larger surrounding context to the LLM, pipelines implement {{c1::Parent Document Retrieval}}.
explanation: During indexing, documents are split into large parent documents and smaller child chunks. The vector database stores only child chunks. When a child is retrieved, the system fetches the larger parent document from storage to feed the LLM, preserving context.
usage: In LangChain: ParentDocumentRetriever manages child vector stores and parent docstores.
spanish: Para buscar en fragmentos vectoriales pequeños y precisos pero entregar el contexto circundante más grande al LLM, las canalizaciones implementan la Recuperación de Documento Padre (Parent Document Retrieval).

text: To combine semantic understanding and exact keyword matching, advanced RAG systems execute a {{c1::hybrid search}} merging vector and BM25 scores.
explanation: Hybrid search runs both dense vector search (for semantic meaning) and sparse keyword search (for exact names, codes, or products), merging their scores using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF).
usage: RRF formula combines rankings: \(RRF(d) = \sum \frac{1}{k + r_m(d)}\) where \(r_m(d)\) is document rank in search \(m\).
spanish: Para combinar la comprensión semántica y la coincidencia exacta de palabras clave, los sistemas RAG avanzados ejecutan una búsqueda híbrida (hybrid search) fusionando puntuaciones vectoriales y BM25.

text: To reduce the massive RAM requirements of millions of document embeddings, vector databases apply {{c1::Product Quantization (PQ)}}.
explanation: Product Quantization compresses high-dimensional vectors by dividing them into sub-vectors, clustering each sub-vector space, and storing only the cluster centroid index, reducing vector size by up to 95%.
usage: PQ is supported in vector libraries like FAISS and Milvus to scale storage.
spanish: Para reducir los enormes requisitos de RAM de millones de incrustaciones de documentos, las bases de datos vectoriales aplican la cuantización de productos (Product Quantization).

text: In Chinese, '你好' means {{c1::Hello / Hi}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐ hǎo}}.
explanation: '你好' (Nǐ hǎo) is the standard, universal greeting in Mandarin Chinese. It literally means 'You good?' and is used at any time of day.
usage:
Term: 你好 (Nǐ hǎo) | Use: General greeting for anyone
  • 你好! 我叫 Jesus。 — Hello! My name is Jesus.
spanish: En chino, '你好' significa 'Hola'. Pronunciación: Nǐ hǎo.

text: In Chinese, '早上好' means {{c1::Good morning}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Zǎo shang hǎo}}.
explanation: '早上好' (Zǎo shang hǎo) is a morning greeting used before noon. '早' (zǎo) alone is a casual shortcut, like saying 'Morning!'
usage:
Term: 早上好 (Zǎo shang hǎo) | Use: Morning greeting
  • 早上好! 今天天气真好。 — Good morning! The weather is beautiful today.
spanish: En chino, '早上好' significa 'Buenos días'. Pronunciación: Zǎo shang hǎo.

text: In Chinese, '晚上好' means {{c1::Good evening}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǎnshang hǎo}}.
explanation: '晚上好' (Wǎnshang hǎo) is used in the evening as a polite greeting. '晚' means 'late/evening'.
usage:
Term: 晚上好 (Wǎnshang hǎo) | Use: Evening greeting
  • 晚上好! 欢迎来到这里。 — Good evening! Welcome here.
spanish: En chino, '晚上好' significa 'Buenas noches'. Pronunciación: Wǎnshang hǎo.

text: In Chinese, '再见' means {{c1::Goodbye}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Zàijiàn}}.
explanation: '再见' (Zàijiàn) is the standard farewell. It literally means 'See again' (再=again, 见=see), similar to the French 'au revoir'.
usage:
Term: 再见 (Zàijiàn) | Use: Standard farewell
  • 再见! 明天见。 — Goodbye! See you tomorrow.
spanish: En chino, '再见' significa 'Adiós'. Pronunciación: Zàijiàn.

scenario: Chinese A1: Nice to Meet You 🗣️
text: In Chinese, '很高兴认识你' means {{c1::Nice to meet you}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Hěn gāoxìng rènshí nǐ}}.
explanation: '很高兴认识你' is the standard phrase when meeting someone for the first time. It literally means 'Very happy to know you'.
usage:
Term: 很高兴认识你 | Use: First meeting
  • 很高兴认识你! 你是哪里人? — Nice to meet you! Where are you from?
spanish: En chino, '很高兴认识你' significa 'Mucho gusto'. Pronunciación: Hěn gāoxìng rènshí nǐ.

text: In Chinese, '你好吗?' means {{c1::How are you?}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐ hǎo ma?}}.
explanation: '你好吗?' adds the question particle '吗' (ma) to the greeting '你好'. A common response is '我很好' (Wǒ hěn hǎo) — I'm very well.
usage:
Term: 你好吗? (Nǐ hǎo ma?)
  • A: 你好吗? — B: 我很好，谢谢! (I'm very well, thank you!)
spanish: En chino, '你好吗?' significa '¿Cómo estás?'. Pronunciación: Nǐ hǎo ma?

text: In Chinese, '谢谢' means {{c1::Thank you}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Xièxie}}.
explanation: '谢谢' (Xièxie) is the most common way to express gratitude. A polite response is '不客气' (Bú kèqi) — You're welcome.
usage:
Term: 谢谢 (Xièxie)
  • A: 谢谢! — B: 不客气! (You're welcome!)
spanish: En chino, '谢谢' significa 'Gracias'. Pronunciación: Xièxie.

text: In Chinese, '不客气' means {{c1::You're welcome}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Bú kèqi}}.
explanation: '不客气' (Bú kèqi) literally means 'don't be polite'. It's the standard response to '谢谢'. Another common alternative is '没事' (Méi shì) — No problem.
usage:
Term: 不客气 (Bú kèqi)
  • A: 谢谢你的帮助! — B: 不客气!
spanish: En chino, '不客气' significa 'De nada'. Pronunciación: Bú kèqi.

text: In Chinese, '对不起' means {{c1::Sorry / I apologize}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Duìbuqǐ}}.
explanation: '对不起' (Duìbuqǐ) is used for sincere apologies. For a lighter 'excuse me' to get someone's attention, use '不好意思' (Bù hǎoyìsi).
usage:
Term: 对不起 (Duìbuqǐ)
  • 对不起，我来晚了。 — Sorry, I'm late.
spanish: En chino, '对不起' significa 'Lo siento'. Pronunciación: Duìbuqǐ.

text: In Chinese, '明天见' means {{c1::See you tomorrow}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Míngtiān jiàn}}.
explanation: '明天见' combines '明天' (míngtiān = tomorrow) and '见' (jiàn = see). This structure is flexible: '下周见' = see you next week.
usage:
Term: 明天见 (Míngtiān jiàn)
  • 好的，明天见! — OK, see you tomorrow!
spanish: En chino, '明天见' significa 'Hasta mañana'. Pronunciación: Míngtiān jiàn.

text: In Chinese, '晚安' means {{c1::Good night}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǎn'ān}}.
explanation: '晚安' (Wǎn'ān) is used as a farewell at night or before bed. '晚' = evening/late, '安' = peace/safe.
usage:
Term: 晚安 (Wǎn'ān)
  • 晚安，好梦! — Good night, sweet dreams!
spanish: En chino, '晚安' significa 'Buenas noches (al despedirse)'. Pronunciación: Wǎn'ān.

text: In Chinese, '欢迎' means {{c1::Welcome}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Huānyíng}}.
explanation: '欢迎' (Huānyíng) is used to welcome guests or visitors. Shops and restaurants often display '欢迎光临' (Huānyíng guānglín) — Welcome (honored guest).
usage:
Term: 欢迎 (Huānyíng)
  • 欢迎来到北京! — Welcome to Beijing!
spanish: En chino, '欢迎' significa 'Bienvenido'. Pronunciación: Huānyíng.

scenario: Chinese A1: Long Time No See 🗣️
text: In Chinese, '好久不见' means {{c1::Long time no see}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Hǎo jiǔ bù jiàn}}.
explanation: Interestingly, the English phrase 'long time no see' is believed to be a direct calque from the Chinese '好久不见'. It is widely used in casual reunions.
usage:
Term: 好久不见 (Hǎo jiǔ bù jiàn)
  • 好久不见！你最近怎么样? — Long time no see! How have you been lately?
spanish: En chino, '好久不见' significa '¡Cuánto tiempo sin verte!'. Pronunciación: Hǎo jiǔ bù jiàn.

text: In Chinese, '请' means {{c1::Please}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Qǐng}}.
explanation: '请' (Qǐng) can mean 'please', 'invite', or 'treat (to a meal)'. As a polite particle it's placed at the start of requests: '请坐' = Please sit down.
usage:
Term: 请 (Qǐng)
  • 请跟我来。 — Please follow me.
  • 请问，洗手间在哪里? — Excuse me, where is the restroom?
spanish: En chino, '请' significa 'Por favor'. Pronunciación: Qǐng.

scenario: Chinese A1: What is Your Name 🗣️
text: In Chinese, '你叫什么名字?' means {{c1::What is your name?}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐ jiào shénme míngzi?}}
explanation: '你叫什么名字?' — literally 'You are called what name?' — is the standard way to ask someone's name. The answer is '我叫…' (Wǒ jiào…) — My name is…
usage:
Term: 你叫什么名字?
  • A: 你叫什么名字? — B: 我叫 Jesus。(My name is Jesus.)
spanish: En chino, '你叫什么名字?' significa '¿Cómo te llamas?'. Pronunciación: Nǐ jiào shénme míngzi?

scenario: Chinese A1: Where Are You From 🗣️
text: In Chinese, '你是哪里人?' means {{c1::Where are you from?}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐ shì nǎlǐ rén?}}
explanation: '你是哪里人?' literally means 'You are where person?' The answer is '我是墨西哥人' (Wǒ shì Mòxīgē rén) — I am Mexican.
usage:
Term: 你是哪里人?
  • A: 你是哪里人? — B: 我是墨西哥人。(I'm Mexican.)
spanish: En chino, '你是哪里人?' significa '¿De dónde eres?'. Pronunciación: Nǐ shì nǎlǐ rén?

text: In Chinese, '我听不懂' means {{c1::I don't understand (listening)}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǒ tīng bu dǒng}}.
explanation: '我听不懂' specifically means 'I cannot understand what I heard'. For not understanding written text, use '我看不懂'. A useful learner phrase.
usage:
Term: 我听不懂 (Wǒ tīng bu dǒng)
  • 对不起，我听不懂。请说慢一点。 — Sorry, I don't understand. Please speak more slowly.
spanish: En chino, '我听不懂' significa 'No entiendo (lo que escucho)'. Pronunciación: Wǒ tīng bu dǒng.

scenario: Chinese A1: Can You Speak Slower 🗣️
text: In Chinese, '请说慢一点' means {{c1::Please speak more slowly}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Qǐng shuō màn yīdiǎn}}.
explanation: An essential phrase for language learners. '慢' (màn) = slow, '一点' (yīdiǎn) = a little. Together: 'a little slower'.
usage:
Term: 请说慢一点
  • 你能说慢一点吗? — Can you speak a little slower?
spanish: En chino, '请说慢一点' significa 'Por favor habla más despacio'. Pronunciación: Qǐng shuō màn yīdiǎn.

scenario: Chinese A1: Do You Speak English 🗣️
text: In Chinese, '你会说英语吗?' means {{c1::Do you speak English?}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐ huì shuō Yīngyǔ ma?}}
explanation: '会说' (huì shuō) means 'can speak'. '英语' is English. To ask about Spanish: '你会说西班牙语吗?' (Nǐ huì shuō Xībānyáyǔ ma?)
usage:
Term: 你会说英语吗?
  • A: 你会说英语吗? — B: 会一点。(A little.)
spanish: En chino, '你会说英语吗?' significa '¿Hablas inglés?'. Pronunciación: Nǐ huì shuō Yīngyǔ ma?

text: In Chinese, '我很好' means {{c1::I'm very well / I'm fine}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǒ hěn hǎo}}.
explanation: '我很好' is the standard positive response to '你好吗?'. '很' (hěn) intensifies the adjective — it is almost always required in such sentences.
usage:
Term: 我很好 (Wǒ hěn hǎo)
  • A: 你好吗? — B: 我很好，谢谢! 你呢? (I'm well, thank you! And you?)
spanish: En chino, '我很好' significa 'Estoy muy bien'. Pronunciación: Wǒ hěn hǎo.

text: In Chinese, '我' means {{c1::I / me}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǒ}}.
explanation: '我' (Wǒ) is the first-person singular pronoun in Chinese. Unlike English, Chinese uses the same word for both subject ('I') and object ('me').
usage:
Term: 我 (Wǒ)
  • 我是学生。 — I am a student.
  • 他认识我。 — He knows me.
spanish: En chino, '我' significa 'Yo / Me'. Pronunciación: Wǒ.

text: In Chinese, '你' means {{c1::you (singular)}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐ}}.
explanation: '你' (Nǐ) is the second-person singular pronoun. A respectful/formal version is '您' (Nín), used when addressing elders or superiors.
usage:
Term: 你 (Nǐ)
  • 你是谁? — Who are you?
  • 我爱你。 — I love you.
spanish: En chino, '你' significa 'Tú / Usted'. Pronunciación: Nǐ.

text: In spoken Chinese, the pronoun for He, She, and It all sound the same: {{c1::tā}}. The characters differ: He=他, She=她, It=它.
explanation: In written Chinese, '他' (he), '她' (she), and '它' (it) are distinct. But in spoken Mandarin, all three are pronounced identically as 'tā'. Context determines meaning.
usage:
Term: 他/她/它 (tā)
  • 她是我的老师。 — She is my teacher.
  • 他很忙。 — He is very busy.
spanish: En chino, 他/她/它 todos suenan igual: 'tā' (él/ella/ello). Los caracteres son distintos.

text: In Chinese, '我们' means {{c1::We / us}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǒmen}}.
explanation: '我们' (Wǒmen) is the first-person plural pronoun. The suffix '们' (men) is added to singular pronouns to make them plural: 你们 (you all), 他们 (they).
usage:
Term: 我们 (Wǒmen)
  • 我们是朋友。 — We are friends.
  • 我们一起去吧! — Let's go together!
spanish: En chino, '我们' significa 'Nosotros'. Pronunciación: Wǒmen.

text: In Chinese, '他们' means {{c1::They}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Tāmen}}.
explanation: '他们' (Tāmen) is the third-person plural. For an all-female group '她们' is used, though '他们' is gender-neutral in plural.
usage:
Term: 他们 (Tāmen)
  • 他们在哪里? — Where are they?
  • 他们都是工程师。 — They are all engineers.
spanish: En chino, '他们' significa 'Ellos/Ellas'. Pronunciación: Tāmen.

text: In Chinese, the verb 'to be' (for identity/classification) is {{c1::是 (shì)}}. Example: 我是老师 = {{c2::I am a teacher}}.
explanation: '是' (shì) is used to link a subject with a noun (identity). Unlike English 'be', it is NOT used with adjectives. For adjectives, Chinese omits the verb entirely: 她很美 = She is beautiful.
usage:
Term: 是 (shì)
  • 这是我的手机。 — This is my phone.
  • 他是墨西哥人。 — He is Mexican.
spanish: En chino, 'ser/estar' con sustantivos se dice '是 (shì)'. No se usa con adjetivos.

text: In Chinese, '不是' means {{c1::am not / is not / are not}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Bú shì}}.
explanation: Negation in Chinese is formed by placing '不' (bù/bú) before the verb. '不是' (bú shì) negates '是'. Note the tone change: 不 changes from 4th to 2nd tone before another 4th tone.
usage:
Term: 不是 (Bú shì)
  • 我不是你的老师。 — I am not your teacher.
  • 这不是我的书包。 — This is not my backpack.
spanish: En chino, '不是 (bú shì)' significa 'no ser'. La negación se forma con '不' antes del verbo.

text: To turn any statement into a yes/no question in Chinese, add {{c1::吗 (ma)}} at the end. Example: 你是学生吗? = {{c2::Are you a student?}}
explanation: '吗' (ma) is a question particle that converts statements to yes/no questions with no word order change. This makes Chinese question formation simpler than in English.
usage:
Term: 吗 (ma)
  • 你饿吗? — Are you hungry?
  • 他是你朋友吗? — Is he your friend?
spanish: En chino, añadir '吗 (ma)' al final de una oración la convierte en pregunta de sí/no.

text: In Chinese, the possessive particle is {{c1::的 (de)}}. Example: 我的书 = {{c2::My book}}.
explanation: '的' (de) works like the English apostrophe 's. It follows a pronoun or noun to show possession. 你的 = your, 他的 = his, 我们的 = our.
usage:
Term: 的 (de)
  • 这是我的电脑。 — This is my computer.
  • 他们的办公室在三楼。 — Their office is on the third floor.
spanish: En chino, '的 (de)' funciona como el apóstrofe 's para indicar posesión. 我的 = mío, 你的 = tuyo.

text: In Chinese, '你们' means {{c1::You all / You (plural)}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐmen}}.
explanation: '你们' is the informal second-person plural. The formal plural '您们' (Nínmen) exists but is rarely used — in formal settings, addressing a group, '各位' (gèwèi = everyone) is preferred.
usage:
Term: 你们 (Nǐmen)
  • 你们好! — Hello everyone! (group greeting)
  • 你们准备好了吗? — Are you all ready?
spanish: En chino, '你们' significa 'Vosotros / Ustedes'. Pronunciación: Nǐmen.

scenario: Chinese A1: There Is / There Are — 有 🧩
text: In Chinese, '有' (yǒu) expresses {{c1::existence / there is / to have}}. Example: 这里有一家咖啡店 = {{c2::There is a coffee shop here}}.
explanation: '有' (yǒu) serves as both 'to have' and 'there is/are'. Its negative is '没有' (méiyǒu) — does not have / there is not.
usage:
Term: 有 (yǒu)
  • 我有一个问题。 — I have a question.
  • 这里没有信号。 — There is no signal here.
spanish: En chino, '有 (yǒu)' significa 'tener / haber'. Su negativo es '没有 (méiyǒu)'.

text: In Chinese, '在' (zài) expresses {{c1::location / to be at / to be in}}. Example: 我在北京 = {{c2::I am in Beijing}}.
explanation: '在' (zài) is used instead of '是' when talking about location. It means 'to be at/in' a place. Note: 我是北京 is grammatically wrong for location.
usage:
Term: 在 (zài)
  • 她在图书馆。 — She is at the library.
  • 文件在桌子上。 — The file is on the desk.
spanish: En chino, '在 (zài)' indica ubicación: 'estar en un lugar'. Se usa en lugar de '是' para expresar dónde está algo.

text: In Chinese, '我叫...' means {{c1::My name is... / I am called...}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǒ jiào...}}
explanation: '叫' (jiào) literally means 'to be called'. '我叫Jesus' — I am called Jesus. You can also say '我的名字是Jesus' (Wǒ de míngzi shì Jesus), which uses '是'.
usage:
Term: 我叫 (Wǒ jiào)
  • 大家好，我叫 Jesus。 — Hello everyone, my name is Jesus.
spanish: En chino, '我叫...' significa 'Me llamo...'. Pronunciación: Wǒ jiào.

text: In Chinese, adjective sentences require the intensifier {{c1::很 (hěn)}} before the adjective. Example: 她很忙 = {{c2::She is very busy}}.
explanation: In Chinese, when an adjective is used as the predicate, '很' must be placed before it. While '很' literally means 'very', in simple statements it acts as a linking word and its intensity is often neutral.
usage:
Term: 很 (hěn)
  • 他很高。 — He is tall.
  • 中文很难。 — Chinese is difficult.
spanish: En chino, '很 (hěn)' es necesario entre el sujeto y el adjetivo predicativo. Técnicamente significa 'muy'.

text: In Chinese, '也' means {{c1::also / too / as well}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Yě}}.
explanation: '也' (yě) is placed after the subject and before the verb, not at the end of a sentence like in English. Position matters: 我也是 = I am also / Me too.
usage:
Term: 也 (yě)
  • A: 我是工程师。— B: 我也是。(Me too / I'm also [one].)
  • 她也学中文。 — She also studies Chinese.
spanish: En chino, '也 (yě)' significa 'también'. Va después del sujeto y antes del verbo.

text: In Chinese, '这' means {{c1::this}} (Zhè) and '那' means {{c2::that}} (Nà).
explanation: '这' (zhè) and '那' (nà) are demonstrative pronouns/adjectives. Add '个' (ge) for 'this one / that one': 这个 / 那个. They are used with '是' to identify things.
usage:
Term: 这 (zhè) / 那 (nà)
  • 这是什么? — What is this?
  • 那个人是谁? — Who is that person?
spanish: En chino, '这 (zhè)' = este/esta y '那 (nà)' = ese/aquel.

text: In Chinese, '谁' means {{c1::Who}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Shéi / Shuí}}.
explanation: '谁' (shéi or shuí, both pronunciations are correct) is the question word for 'who'. Chinese question words stay in the same position as the answer would be.
usage:
Term: 谁 (Shéi)
  • 你是谁? — Who are you?
  • 谁是你的老板? — Who is your boss?
spanish: En chino, '谁 (shéi)' significa '¿Quién?'. Las palabras interrogativas mantienen su posición en la oración.

text: In Chinese, '什么' means {{c1::What}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Shénme}}.
explanation: '什么' (shénme) is the most common question word. Like other Chinese question words, it stays in the same position as the answer: 你叫什么名字? (You called what name?)
usage:
Term: 什么 (Shénme)
  • 这是什么? — What is this?
  • 你要什么? — What do you want?
spanish: En chino, '什么 (shénme)' significa '¿Qué?'. Permanece en la posición de la respuesta.

scenario: Chinese A1: Numbers — One to Five 🧩
text: In Chinese, the numbers 1-5 are: 一(yī), 二(èr), 三(sān), 四(sì), 五(wǔ). What is {{c1::三 (sān)}} and {{c2::五 (wǔ)}} in English?
explanation: Chinese numerals are phonetically simple and consistent. 一=1, 二=2, 三=3, 四=4, 五=5. Numbers are essential for counting, phone numbers, prices, and telling time.
usage:
Term: 一二三四五 (yī èr sān sì wǔ)
  • 我要三个包子。 — I want three buns.
  • 我们有五个人。 — We have five people.
spanish: En chino: 一(yī)=1, 二(èr)=2, 三(sān)=3, 四(sì)=4, 五(wǔ)=5.

scenario: Chinese A1: Not Have — 没有 🧩
text: In Chinese, '没有' means {{c1::don't have / there is not}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Méiyǒu}}.
explanation: '没有' (méiyǒu) is the negation of '有' (to have). It is also used like 'there is not': 没有人 = There is nobody. In everyday speech, '没有' is often shortened to '没' (méi).
usage:
Term: 没有 (Méiyǒu)
  • 我没有钱。 — I don't have money.
  • 没有问题! — No problem!
spanish: En chino, '没有 (méiyǒu)' significa 'no tener / no haber'. Es la negación de '有'.

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Tones}} is defined as: Each syllable has a specific pitch pattern (1: level, 2: rising, 3: dipping, 4: falling, 5: neutral).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Tones
Description: Each syllable has a specific pitch pattern (1: level, 2: rising, 3: dipping, 4: falling, 5: neutral).
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Tones' es: Each syllable has a specific pitch pattern (1: level, 2: rising, 3: dipping, 4: falling, 5: neutral).

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Erhua (儿化)}} is defined as: A process of retroflexion added to the end of syllables, common in Beijing dialect.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Erhua (儿化)
Description: A process of retroflexion added to the end of syllables, common in Beijing dialect.
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Erhua (儿化)' es: A process of retroflexion added to the end of syllables, common in Beijing dialect.

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Diphthongs/Triphthongs}} is defined as: Many combinations like /ai/, /ei/, /ao/, /ou/, /iau/, /uai/.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Diphthongs/Triphthongs
Description: Many combinations like /ai/, /ei/, /ao/, /ou/, /iau/, /uai/.
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Diphthongs/Triphthongs' es: Many combinations like /ai/, /ei/, /ao/, /ou/, /iau/, /uai/.

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Aspiration Contrast}} is defined as: Mandarin does not distinguish between voiced and voiceless stops. Instead, it uses **aspiration** (e.g., /p/ is unaspirated, /pʰ/ is aspirated).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Aspiration Contrast
Description: Mandarin does not distinguish between voiced and voiceless stops. Instead, it uses **aspiration** (e.g., /p/ is unaspirated, /pʰ/ is aspirated).
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Aspiration Contrast' es: Mandarin does not distinguish between voiced and voiceless stops. Instead, it uses **aspiration** (e.g., /p/ is unaspirated, /pʰ/ is aspirated).

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Three Series}} is defined as: - **Alveolar**: /ts, tsʰ, s/ (z, c, s)
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: The Three Series
Description: - **Alveolar**: /ts, tsʰ, s/ (z, c, s)
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'The Three Series' es: - **Alveolar**: /ts, tsʰ, s/ (z, c, s)

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Retroflex}} is defined as: /tʂ, tʂʰ, ʂ, ɻ/ (zh, ch, sh, r)
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Retroflex
Description: /tʂ, tʂʰ, ʂ, ɻ/ (zh, ch, sh, r)
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Retroflex' es: /tʂ, tʂʰ, ʂ, ɻ/ (zh, ch, sh, r)

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Alveolo-palatal}} is defined as: /tɕ, tɕʰ, ɕ/ (j, q, x)
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Alveolo-palatal
Description: /tɕ, tɕʰ, ɕ/ (j, q, x)
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Alveolo-palatal' es: /tɕ, tɕʰ, ɕ/ (j, q, x)

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Erhua (Retroflexion - 儿化)}} is defined as: Adding a retroflexed 'r' sound to the end of syllables, highly common in Beijing and Northern dialects. It modifies the main vowel:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Erhua (Retroflexion - 儿化)
Description: Adding a retroflexed 'r' sound to the end of syllables, highly common in Beijing and Northern dialects. It modifies the main vowel:
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Erhua (Retroflexion - 儿化)' es: Adding a retroflexed 'r' sound to the end of syllables, highly common in Beijing and Northern dialects. It modifies the main vowel:

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Tone Sandhi (Tonal Transformations)}} is defined as: - **3rd Tone Sandhi**: When two 3rd tones occur in succession, the first shifts to a 2nd tone (e.g., *nǐ hǎo* -> *ní hǎo* [ni˧˥ xaʊ˨˩˦]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Tone Sandhi (Tonal Transformations)
Description: - **3rd Tone Sandhi**: When two 3rd tones occur in succession, the first shifts to a 2nd tone (e.g., *nǐ hǎo* -> *ní hǎo* [ni˧˥ xaʊ˨˩˦]).
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Tone Sandhi (Tonal Transformations)' es: - **3rd Tone Sandhi**: When two 3rd tones occur in succession, the first shifts to a 2nd tone (e.g., *nǐ hǎo* -> *ní hǎo* [ni˧˥ xaʊ˨˩˦]).

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Bu Sandhi}} is defined as: *bù* (4th tone) shifts to 2nd tone *bú* when preceding another 4th tone (e.g., *bù qù* -> *bú qù*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Bu Sandhi
Description: *bù* (4th tone) shifts to 2nd tone *bú* when preceding another 4th tone (e.g., *bù qù* -> *bú qù*).
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Bu Sandhi' es: *bù* (4th tone) shifts to 2nd tone *bú* when preceding another 4th tone (e.g., *bù qù* -> *bú qù*).

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Yi Sandhi}} is defined as: *yī* (1st tone) becomes 4th tone *yì* before 1st, 2nd, and 3rd tones (e.g., *yī tiān* -> *yì tiān*), and becomes 2nd tone *yí* before 4th tone (e.g., *yī yàng* -> *yí yàng*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Yi Sandhi
Description: *yī* (1st tone) becomes 4th tone *yì* before 1st, 2nd, and 3rd tones (e.g., *yī tiān* -> *yì tiān*), and becomes 2nd tone *yí* before 4th tone (e.g., *yī yàng* -> *yí yàng*).
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Yi Sandhi' es: *yī* (1st tone) becomes 4th tone *yì* before 1st, 2nd, and 3rd tones (e.g., *yī tiān* -> *yì tiān*), and becomes 2nd tone *yí* before 4th tone (e.g., *yī yàng* -> *yí yàng*).

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Half 3rd Tone Rule}} is defined as: When a 3rd tone is followed by any tone other than another 3rd tone (i.e., 1st, 2nd, 4th, or neutral), it only dips down to low pitch and doesn't rise at the end. This is the most common realization of the 3rd tone in natural speech.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Half 3rd Tone Rule
Description: When a 3rd tone is followed by any tone other than another 3rd tone (i.e., 1st, 2nd, 4th, or neutral), it only dips down to low pitch and doesn't rise at the end. This is the most common realization of the 3rd tone in natural speech.
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Half 3rd Tone Rule' es: When a 3rd tone is followed by any tone other than another 3rd tone (i.e., 1st, 2nd, 4th, or neutral), it only dips down to low pitch and doesn't rise at the end. This is the most common realization of the 3rd tone in natural speech.

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Colloquial Syllable Contraction}} is defined as: Fast speech merges vowel boundaries and drops consonants:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Colloquial Syllable Contraction
Description: Fast speech merges vowel boundaries and drops consonants:
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Colloquial Syllable Contraction' es: Fast speech merges vowel boundaries and drops consonants:

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Tonal Identity}} is defined as: Tone is essential for meaning; changing the tone changes the word (e.g., *mā* "mother" vs *mǎ* "horse").
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Tonal Identity
Description: Tone is essential for meaning; changing the tone changes the word (e.g., *mā* "mother" vs *mǎ* "horse").
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Tonal Identity' es: Tone is essential for meaning; changing the tone changes the word (e.g., *mā* "mother" vs *mǎ* "horse").

text: In Chinese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Neutral Tone}} is defined as: Syllables in certain positions (like the second half of some compounds) lose their distinct tone and are pronounced short and light.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Chinese.
usage:
Rule: Neutral Tone
Description: Syllables in certain positions (like the second half of some compounds) lose their distinct tone and are pronounced short and light.
spanish: En la fonética de Chinese, la regla de 'Neutral Tone' es: Syllables in certain positions (like the second half of some compounds) lose their distinct tone and are pronounced short and light.

text: What is the Chinese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "Zhiao" Shortcut}}? In rapid conversational Mandarin, "wǒ bù zhī dào" (I don't know) is contracted to "wǒ bù zhiao". Practice blending the 'zh' and 'd' into a single fast 'j/zh' slide!
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Chinese.
usage:
Tip: The "Zhiao" Shortcut
In rapid conversational Mandarin, "wǒ bù zhī dào" (I don't know) is contracted to "wǒ bù zhiao". Practice blending the 'zh' and 'd' into a single fast 'j/zh' slide!
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Chinese para 'The "Zhiao" Shortcut'? In rapid conversational Mandarin, "wǒ bù zhī dào" (I don't know) is contracted to "wǒ bù zhiao". Practice blending the 'zh' and 'd' into a single fast 'j/zh' slide!

text: What is the Chinese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The Half 3rd Tone Rule}}? To sound natural, do not try to make the full dipping-rising sound for every 3rd tone. In 90% of cases (like in "lǎo shī" or "lǎo bǎn"), just make a low, flat chest tone and immediately jump to the next syllable.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Chinese.
usage:
Tip: The Half 3rd Tone Rule
To sound natural, do not try to make the full dipping-rising sound for every 3rd tone. In 90% of cases (like in "lǎo shī" or "lǎo bǎn"), just make a low, flat chest tone and immediately jump to the next syllable.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Chinese para 'The Half 3rd Tone Rule'? To sound natural, do not try to make the full dipping-rising sound for every 3rd tone. In 90% of cases (like in "lǎo shī" or "lǎo bǎn"), just make a low, flat chest tone and immediately jump to the next syllable.

text: In Chinese phonetics, the vowel {{c1::ü / yu}} is pronounced as a close front rounded vowel (/y/), which is formed by shaping your lips to say 'oo' (as in 'boot') while pronouncing the vowel 'ee' (as in 'beet').
explanation: The vowel 'ü' is distinct from 'u' in Mandarin. It occurs in words like 女 (nǚ - female) and 绿 (lǜ - green) to distinguish from 怒 (nù) and 路 (lù).
usage:
Rule: ü pronunciation
Examples: 女 (nǚ), 绿 (lǜ).
spanish: En la fonética del chino, la vocal 'ü / yu' se pronuncia como una vocal redondeada anterior cerrada (/y/), que se forma redondeando los labios como para decir 'u' mientras pronuncias la vocal 'i'.

text: In Chinese phonetics, the initial consonant {{c1::r}} is pronounced as a voiced retroflex fricative (/ʐ/) or retroflex approximant (/ɻ/), which sounds like a soft blend of the English 'r' and the 'z' in 'seizure'.
explanation: The Chinese 'r' initial involves curling the tongue backward toward the roof of the mouth. It is voiced and has some friction, unlike the English 'r'.
usage:
Consonant: r initial
Examples: 人 (rén - person), 热 (rè - hot).
spanish: En la fonética del chino, la consonante inicial 'r' se pronuncia como una fricativa retrofleja sonora (/ʐ/), lo que suena como una mezcla suave de la 'r' en inglés y la 'j' francesa.

text: In Chinese Pinyin, the final combination {{c1::ian}} is not pronounced 'ee-ahn'; instead, the 'a' shifts to an 'eh' sound, making the entire final sound like {{c2::yen}} (/jɛn/).
explanation: Many beginners mispronounce Pinyin 'ian' by making a broad 'a' sound. In standard Mandarin, 'ian' always rhymes with English 'pen' or 'yen'.
usage:
Final rhyme: ian
Examples: 天 (tiān - day), 面 (miàn - face).
spanish: En el Pinyin chino, la combinación final 'ian' no se pronuncia 'i-an'; en su lugar, la 'a' cambia a un sonido 'e', haciendo que suene como 'ien' (/jɛn/).

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "我们在玩儿呢 (Women zai wan er ne)."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Women zai waer ne.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Erhua (儿化)
The retroflex suffix blends with the final vowel of 'wan', dropping the nasal 'n' and adding r-coloring.
usage: Formal: 我们在玩儿呢 (Women zai wan er ne). → Connected: Women zai waer ne.
spanish: Spanish: Estamos jugando aquí.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "这是什么？ (Zhe shi shen me?)"
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Zhe shi shenme?}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Neutral Tone (轻声)
The second character 'me' loses its original tone, becoming short, flat, and soft.
usage: Formal: 这是什么？ (Zhe shi shen me?) → Connected: Zhe shi shenme?
spanish: Spanish: ¿Qué es esto?

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "我不知道 (Wo bu zhi dao)."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Wo buzdao.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Syllable Contraction
The adjacent syllables 'bu', 'zhi', and 'dao' merge into 'buzdao' by dropping the retroflex initial /zh/.
usage: Formal: 我不知道 (Wo bu zhi dao). → Connected: Wo buzdao.
spanish: Spanish: No lo sé.

text: I would like to check in this suitcase, and I have one {{c1::carry-on bag}} to bring into the cabin.
explanation: A carry-on bag (or hand luggage) is the small suitcase or backpack that passengers are permitted to bring onto the plane with them rather than checking it.
usage:
Pattern: carry-on bag / carry-on luggage
  • Make sure your carry-on bag fits in the overhead bin.
  • I only travel with a carry-on bag to save time.
spanish: Me gustaría registrar esta maleta, y tengo una maleta de mano para llevar en la cabina.

scenario: English Phonetics: Airport Gate Connected Speech ✈️
text: At the gate, the announcement "We will begin boarding in ten minutes" blends the last two words via consonant-to-consonant linking, making "in ten" sound like {{c1::"int-ten" [ɪnˈtɛn]}}.
explanation: When a word ends in /n/ and the next begins with /t/, the alveolar nasal /n/ assimilates or links directly to the alveolar stop /t/ to allow smooth, rapid articulation, resulting in a single elongated nasal-to-stop transition.
usage:
Phonetic Drill: in ten minutes → "in-ten" [ɪnˈtɛn]
Contrastive Practice: Pronounce both 'n' and 't' as a single continuous block without a glottal pause.
spanish: En la puerta, el anuncio "We will begin boarding in ten minutes" fusiona las últimas dos palabras mediante enlace consonante-consonante, haciendo que "in ten" suene como "int-ten".

scenario: English Daily Life: Airport Baggage Claim ✈️
text: At the baggage claim desk, if your luggage is missing, you should report it politely saying: "Excuse me, my bag {{c1::didn't come out}} on the carousel, and I'd like to {{c2::file a claim}}."
explanation: To "file a claim" is the official terminology for registering a lost item report. Saying "didn't come out" is the natural phrasal expression for baggage that did not arrive.
usage:
Pattern: file a claim / didn't come out on [carousel]
  • I need to file a claim for my damaged baggage.
  • My suitcase didn't come out on carousel 4.
spanish: En el mostrador de reclamo de equipaje, si tu maleta se perdió, debes reportarlo diciendo educadamente: "Excuse me, my bag didn't come out on the carousel, and I'd like to file a claim."

text: Could you please tell me where the {{c1::baggage drop-off}} counter is?
explanation: The 'baggage drop-off' is the designated counter where passengers who have already checked in online can drop off their luggage before heading to security.
usage:
Pattern: baggage drop-off / bag drop
  • Where is the bag drop for airline passengers?
  • You can bypass the main line if you only need the baggage drop-off.
spanish: ¿Podría decirme por favor dónde está el mostrador de entrega de equipaje?

text: I need to check in my luggage, but I want to keep this briefcase as my {{c1::personal item}}.
explanation: A 'personal item' is a small bag (like a purse, laptop bag, or small backpack) that must fit under the seat in front of you on a plane, distinct from standard carry-on luggage.
usage:
Pattern: personal item
  • Most budget airlines allow one personal item for free.
  • Your laptop bag counts as a personal item.
spanish: Necesito registrar mi equipaje, pero quiero conservar este maletín como mi artículo personal.

text: The flight has been delayed, so we need to wait at the {{c1::departure gate}}.
explanation: The 'departure gate' (or boarding gate) is the specific area in an airport terminal where passengers assemble to board their aircraft.
usage:
Pattern: departure gate / boarding gate
  • Our departure gate has changed from gate 12 to gate 15.
  • Please report to the departure gate immediately.
spanish: El vuelo se ha retrasado, así que tenemos que esperar en la puerta de salida/embarque.

text: Excuse me, does this flight have a {{c1::layover}} in New York?
explanation: A 'layover' (or connection) is a period of waiting between flights at an intermediate airport before reaching the final destination.
usage:
Pattern: layover / connection
  • We have a three-hour layover in Chicago.
  • I prefer direct flights because I hate long layovers.
spanish: Disculpe, ¿este vuelo tiene una escala en Nueva York?

text: We need to go through {{c1::security screening}} before we can access the duty-free area.
explanation: The 'security screening' is the checkpoint in an airport where passengers and their baggage are inspected by security officers and scanning machines.
usage:
Pattern: security screening / security check
  • The line at the security screening was incredibly long.
  • Remove all laptops and liquids before security screening.
spanish: Necesitamos pasar por el control de seguridad antes de poder acceder al área libre de impuestos.

text: Please have your {{c1::boarding pass}} and ID ready for inspection.
explanation: A 'boarding pass' is a document provided by an airline during check-in, giving a passenger permission to board the airplane for a specific flight.
usage:
Pattern: boarding pass / boarding document
  • You can show your digital boarding pass on your phone.
  • Please print your boarding pass at the kiosk.
spanish: Por favor, tenga su pase de abordar e identificación listos para inspección.

text: My flight was overbooked, and they offered me travel vouchers if I {{c1::volunteered to take}} a later flight.
explanation: To 'volunteer to take' a later flight means agreeing to give up your seat on an overbooked flight in exchange for compensation (such as travel vouchers or money).
usage:
Pattern: volunteer to take [flight]
  • No one volunteered to take the next flight, so they started bumped passengers.
  • I volunteered to take a later flight because the vouchers were worth $500.
spanish: Mi vuelo estaba sobrevendido, y me ofrecieron vales de viaje si me ofrecía como voluntario para tomar un vuelo posterior.

text: Could you tell me which terminal is for {{c1::domestic flights}}?
explanation: 'Domestic flights' are flights that stay within the boundaries of the same country, as opposed to international flights.
usage:
Pattern: domestic flights / domestic terminal
  • All domestic flights depart from Terminal 2.
  • I am traveling within the country, so I need to check in for domestic flights.
spanish: ¿Podría decirme qué terminal es para vuelos nacionales?

text: I missed my flight; is there a desk where I can {{c1::rebook my ticket}}?
explanation: To 'rebook a ticket' means to change the reservation on a ticket, typically after missing a flight or if a flight is canceled, allocating the passenger to a new flight.
usage:
Pattern: rebook my ticket / rebook a flight
  • The airline helped me rebook my ticket for the next available flight.
  • Can I rebook my ticket online without paying a fee?
spanish: Perdí mi vuelo; ¿hay algún mostrador donde pueda volver a reservar mi boleto?

text: Please place all metal objects in the bin before going through the {{c1::metal detector}}.
explanation: A 'metal detector' is the electronic security portal in an airport checkpoint that scans passengers for metallic weapons or devices.
usage:
Pattern: metal detector / walk-through scanner
  • Step through the metal detector one at a time.
  • My belt buckle triggered the metal detector.
spanish: Por favor, coloque todos los objetos metálicos en la bandeja antes de pasar por el detector de metales.

text: We need to clear {{c1::customs control}} before exiting the airport.
explanation: To clear 'customs control' means passing through the government checkpoint at an international border to declare goods and verify compliance with imports.
usage:
Pattern: customs control / clear customs
  • It took us an hour to clear customs control in London.
  • Do you have anything to declare at customs control?
spanish: Necesitamos pasar el control de aduanas antes de salir del aeropuerto.

text: Excuse me, where is the office for {{c1::lost and found}}?
explanation: The 'lost and found' (or lost property office) is the department in a public facility where lost items are kept for owners to claim.
usage:
Pattern: lost and found / lost property
  • I left my umbrella on the plane; I should check the lost and found.
  • The lost and found office is near the baggage claim.
spanish: Disculpe, ¿dónde está la oficina de objetos perdidos y encontrados?

text: The captain announced that we are preparing for {{c1::takeoff}}.
explanation: 'Takeoff' is the moment or process when an aircraft leaves the ground and rises into the air.
usage:
Pattern: takeoff / prepare for takeoff
  • Please fasten your seatbelts in preparation for takeoff.
  • The takeoff was very smooth despite the rain.
spanish: El capitán anunció que nos estamos preparando para el despegue.

text: Please remain seated with your seatbelt fastened until the plane has finished {{c1::landing}}.
explanation: 'Landing' is the process of bringing an aircraft down to the ground or runway at the end of a flight.
usage:
Pattern: landing / smooth landing
  • The pilot made a safe landing despite the crosswinds.
  • Remain seated until the landing is complete.
spanish: Por favor, permanezca sentado con su cinturón abrochado hasta que el avión haya terminado de aterrizar.

text: Is there a currency exchange booth in the {{c1::arrivals hall}}?
explanation: The 'arrivals hall' is the public area in an airport terminal where passengers emerge after clearing customs and baggage claim.
usage:
Pattern: arrivals hall / departures hall
  • My family was waiting for me in the arrivals hall.
  • You can buy a local SIM card in the arrivals hall.
spanish: ¿Hay una cabina de cambio de divisas en la sala de llegadas?

text: Passengers are allowed to bring one piece of checked luggage and one {{c1::cabin bag}}.
explanation: A 'cabin bag' (or hand luggage) is a small suitcase that complies with airline size rules to be stored in the overhead bins.
usage:
Pattern: cabin bag / hand luggage
  • Make sure your cabin bag is not too heavy.
  • I only traveled with a cabin bag to avoid baggage claim.
spanish: Los pasajeros pueden llevar una pieza de equipaje registrado y una maleta de cabina.

text: If your bags are overweight, you will have to pay an {{c1::excess baggage fee}}.
explanation: An 'excess baggage fee' is a charge made by an airline for luggage that exceeds the weight or size allowance.
usage:
Pattern: excess baggage fee / overweight luggage fee
  • They charged me a $50 excess baggage fee because my suitcase was 5kg over.
  • Avoid the excess baggage fee by wearing your heavy coat.
spanish: Si sus maletas tienen sobrepeso, tendrá que pagar una tarifa por exceso de equipaje.

text: Excuse me, could you tell me how to {{c1::get to}} the nearest train station?
explanation: get to is the most common conversational phrasal verb meaning to reach or arrive at a destination.
usage:
Pattern: get to [place]
  • How do I get to the museum from here?
  • It takes 20 minutes to get to the office.
spanish: Disculpe, ¿podría decirme cómo llegar a la estación de tren más cercana?

text: Go straight ahead for two blocks, then {{c1::take a left}} at the traffic light.
explanation: To 'take a left' is a very common idiomatic phrase meaning to turn left at a specific intersection or landmark.
usage:
Pattern: take a left / make a left / turn left
  • Take a left at the next street.
  • You will need to take a left after you pass the school.
spanish: Siga derecho por dos cuadras, luego doble a la izquierda en el semáforo.

text: Excuse me, is there a pharmacy {{c1::within walking distance}} of here?
explanation: 'Within walking distance' means close enough to be reached easily by walking (typically less than 10-15 minutes away).
usage:
Pattern: within walking distance [of something]
  • The hotel is within walking distance of the beach.
  • We live within walking distance of the office.
spanish: Disculpe, ¿hay alguna farmacia a corta distancia a pie de aquí?

text: You can't miss it; it's right {{c1::across from}} the post office.
explanation: 'Across from' means directly opposite or on the other side of a street, hallway, or space.
usage:
Pattern: across from [place] / opposite
  • The cafe is across from the library.
  • He sat across from me during the meeting.
spanish: No tiene pérdida; está justo enfrente de la oficina de correos.

text: To get to the museum, you need to {{c1::cross the street}} and walk past the bank.
explanation: To 'cross the street' means to walk from one side of a road to the opposite side.
usage:
Pattern: cross the street / cross the road
  • Look both ways before you cross the street.
  • We crossed the street to avoid the construction work.
spanish: Para llegar al museo, debe cruzar la calle y pasar el banco.

text: Excuse me, could you {{c1::point me in the direction of}} the nearest subway station?
explanation: To 'point someone in the direction of' means to show or tell someone the general path or heading to reach a destination.
usage:
Pattern: point [someone] in the direction of [place]
  • Can you point me in the direction of the restrooms?
  • She pointed us in the direction of the highway entrance.
spanish: Disculpe, ¿podría indicarme la dirección hacia la estación de metro más cercana?

text: The stadium is located on the {{c1::outer edge}} of the city.
explanation: The 'outer edge' refers to the boundary, periphery, or outskirts of an area, such as a city limit.
usage:
Pattern: outer edge / outskirts
  • They built a new retail park on the outer edge of town.
  • We live on the outer edge of the metropolitan area.
spanish: El estadio está ubicado en las afueras/extremo exterior de la ciudad.

text: If you hit the gas station, you've {{c1::gone too far}}; turn back.
explanation: To 'go too far' means to travel past the intended destination or target point.
usage:
Pattern: go too far / pass the destination
  • If you reach the river, you've gone too far.
  • I think we went too far; we should turn around.
spanish: Si llega a la gasolinera, se ha pasado; regrese.

text: Take the second exit at the {{c1::roundabout}} to stay on Main Street.
explanation: A 'roundabout' (or traffic circle) is a circular intersection where drivers travel counterclockwise (or clockwise in UK) around a central island.
usage:
Pattern: roundabout / traffic circle
  • Go straight through the first roundabout.
  • Turn right at the roundabout.
spanish: Tome la segunda salida en la rotonda para permanecer en la calle principal.

text: Is it quicker to walk or to {{c1::hail a cab}} from here?
explanation: To 'hail a cab' (or hail a taxi) means to signal a taxi on the street (typically by raising your arm) to stop and pick you up.
usage:
Pattern: hail a cab / catch a taxi
  • We stood in the rain trying to hail a cab for fifteen minutes.
  • It is easy to hail a cab in this neighborhood.
spanish: ¿Es más rápido caminar o pedir/hacerle señas a un taxi desde aquí?

text: You will find the library right around the {{c1::corner}}.
explanation: 'Around the corner' is a very common idiom meaning extremely close or nearby, just on the next street bend.
usage:
Pattern: around the corner / just past the bend
  • The convenience store is right around the corner.
  • Spring is just around the corner.
spanish: Encontrará la biblioteca justo a la vuelta de la esquina.

text: Continue walking until you reach the next {{c1::intersection}}.
explanation: An 'intersection' (or junction) is a point where two or more roads cross or meet.
usage:
Pattern: intersection / junction / crossroads
  • Turn right at the next intersection.
  • There was an accident at the busy intersection.
spanish: Siga caminando hasta que llegue a la siguiente intersección/cruce.

text: Excuse me, is this road the quickest way to get to the {{c1::highway}}?
explanation: A 'highway' is a main public road, typically one connecting major towns or cities, designed for high-speed traffic.
usage:
Pattern: highway / freeway / interstate
  • Take the exit for the highway.
  • The highway was backed up with traffic.
spanish: Disculpe, ¿esta carretera es la forma más rápida de llegar a la autopista?

text: The bank is on the left side, just past the {{c1::pedestrian crossing}}.
explanation: A 'pedestrian crossing' (or crosswalk) is a specified part of a road where pedestrians have the right of way to cross safely.
usage:
Pattern: pedestrian crossing / crosswalk / zebra crossing
  • Drivers must stop at the pedestrian crossing.
  • Cross the street only at the pedestrian crossing.
spanish: El banco está en el lado izquierdo, justo después del paso de peatones.

text: Excuse me, can you tell me how to get to the nearest {{c1::landmark}}?
explanation: A 'landmark' is an object or feature of a landscape or town that is easily seen and recognized from a distance, useful as a reference point.
usage:
Pattern: landmark / monument / focal point
  • The Statue of Liberty is a famous landmark in New York.
  • The clock tower is a key landmark in our city.
spanish: Disculpe, ¿podría decirme cómo llegar al punto de referencia más cercano?

text: Keep going straight for about half a mile and you'll see a big {{c1::billboard}}.
explanation: A 'billboard' (or hoarding) is a large outdoor board for displaying advertisements, commonly located along highways.
usage:
Pattern: billboard / advertisement board
  • We advertised our product on a huge billboard near the highway.
  • I noticed the billboard for the new movie.
spanish: Siga recto durante aproximadamente media milla y verá una gran valla publicitaria/espectacular.

text: If you cross the bridge, you've gone {{c1::in the wrong direction}}.
explanation: To go 'in the wrong direction' means heading away from, rather than toward, the target destination.
usage:
Pattern: in the wrong direction / off course
  • We walked for two miles in the wrong direction.
  • Turn around; you are going in the wrong direction.
spanish: Si cruzas el puente, has ido en la dirección equivocada.

text: The museum is just a short {{c1::drive}} from here.
explanation: A 'short drive' is a relative measurement of time meaning that a destination is very close when traveling by car (typically under 10-15 minutes).
usage:
Pattern: short drive / quick ride
  • The airport is just a short drive from our hotel.
  • It is a short drive to the shopping center.
spanish: El museo está a solo un viaje corto en coche desde aquí.

text: Is there a shortcut through the park to avoid the {{c1::main street}}?
explanation: A 'main street' is the principal street of a town or city, typically containing the primary shops and traffic load.
usage:
Pattern: main street / high street
  • The main street was crowded with shoppers.
  • We walked down the main street of the village.
spanish: ¿Hay algún atajo a través del parque para evitar la calle principal?

text: Pardon me, does this bus {{c1::go towards}} the city center?
explanation: To 'go towards' means to travel in the direction of a specific destination or landmark.
usage:
Pattern: go towards / head in the direction of
  • We walked towards the lake.
  • Does this train go towards the airport?
spanish: Disculpe, ¿este autobús va hacia el centro de la ciudad?

text: Could I get a medium latte {{c1::to go}}, please?
explanation: to go is used when you want to take your food or drink out of the shop instead of consuming it there.
usage:
Pattern: Could I get a [size] [drink] to go, please?
  • Could I get a large Americano to go, please?
  • Could I get a small espresso to go, please?
spanish: ¿Me da un latte mediano para llevar, por favor?

text: I'd like to {{c1::substitute}} whole milk {{c1::with}} oat milk, please.
explanation: substitute A with B means to use B instead of A. This is the standard way to customize food or drink orders.
usage:
Pattern: substitute [original] with [alternative]
  • Can I substitute white bread with wheat bread?
  • I'd like to substitute fries with a side salad, please.
spanish: Me gustaría cambiar la leche entera por leche de avena, por favor.

text: Do you have any {{c1::sugar-free}} syrup options?
explanation: sugar-free means containing no sugar. Syrups are sweet liquid flavorings added to coffee (like vanilla or caramel).
usage:
Pattern: [adjective]-free (e.g., gluten-free, caffeine-free, tax-free)
  • Is this cookie gluten-free?
  • I'd prefer decaf, do you have caffeine-free coffee?
spanish: ¿Tienen opciones de jarabe sin azúcar?

text: Can I {{c1::pay by card}}?
explanation: pay by card means to pay using a credit or debit card rather than cash.
usage:
Pattern: pay by [method] (e.g., pay by card, pay by phone, pay by check) but pay in cash
  • You can pay by phone using Apple Pay.
  • Is it cheaper if I pay in cash?
spanish: ¿Puedo pagar con tarjeta?

text: I would like to order a large cold brew with a {{c1::splash of}} oat milk.
explanation: A 'splash of' is an informal measurement meaning a very small amount of liquid (typically milk, cream, or water) added to a drink.
usage:
Pattern: splash of [liquid]
  • Just a splash of milk in my tea, please.
  • He likes his whiskey with a splash of soda.
spanish: Me gustaría pedir un café extraído en frío grande con un chorrito de leche de avena.

text: Do you have any {{c1::dairy-free}} options for the latte?
explanation: 'Dairy-free' refers to foods or beverages that contain no milk or milk derivatives, suitable for lactose-intolerant or vegan customers.
usage:
Pattern: dairy-free milk / plant-based milk
  • Soy and almond milk are common dairy-free alternatives.
  • Is this caramel syrup dairy-free?
spanish: ¿Tienen alguna opción sin lácteos para el café con leche?

text: I'll have a double espresso, and can I get that {{c1::sweetened with}} honey?
explanation: To get something 'sweetened with' means to add a specific sweetener (like honey, sugar, syrup, or stevia) to a drink or food.
usage:
Pattern: sweetened with [sweetener]
  • I prefer my tea sweetened with raw sugar.
  • This lemonade is sweetened with agave nectar.
spanish: Tomaré un espresso doble, ¿y me lo pueden dar endulzado con miel?

text: Is there a password for the {{c1::guest Wi-Fi}} here?
explanation: 'Guest Wi-Fi' is a secondary wireless network provided by businesses for clients and visitors, keeping the internal network secure.
usage:
Pattern: guest Wi-Fi / public network
  • The guest Wi-Fi is free for customers who buy a drink.
  • Ask the barista for the guest Wi-Fi password.
spanish: ¿Hay contraseña para el Wi-Fi para invitados aquí?

text: I'd like to buy a bag of whole bean coffee, could you {{c1::grind it for}} drip?
explanation: To 'grind it for [method]' means to crush whole coffee beans into the specific particle size required for a brewing method (e.g. drip, espresso, french press).
usage:
Pattern: grind [coffee] for [brewing method]
  • Could you grind these beans for a French press?
  • I grind my own coffee beans for espresso every morning.
spanish: Me gustaría comprar una bolsa de café en grano entero, ¿podrían molerlo para cafetera de filtro?

text: Can I get a pastry to go, and please {{c1::warm it up}} for me?
explanation: To 'warm up' something means to make it warm or hot, usually by putting it in a microwave, oven, or toaster.
usage:
Pattern: warm [something] up / heat up
  • Would you like me to warm up your croissant?
  • I need to warm up my lunch in the microwave.
spanish: ¿Me da una factura/pan dulce para llevar, y por favor me la calienta?

text: I'll have a decaf latte, and please make sure it is {{c1::extra hot}}.
explanation: 'Extra hot' is a specific order request asking the barista to steam the milk to a higher temperature (typically around 160-170°F).
usage:
Pattern: extra hot / steam the milk extra hot
  • Could you make my mocha extra hot?
  • I like my cappuccino extra hot when it is freezing outside.
spanish: Tomaré un café con leche descafeinado, y por favor asegúrese de que esté extra caliente.

text: Do you have any sugar substitutes like stevia or {{c1::artificial sweeteners}}?
explanation: An 'artificial sweetener' is a food additive that provides a sweet taste like that of sugar while containing significantly less food energy.
usage:
Pattern: artificial sweeteners / sugar substitutes
  • I don't use sugar; I prefer artificial sweeteners.
  • Does this coffee shop have any artificial sweeteners like Splenda?
spanish: ¿Tienen algún sustituto de azúcar como stevia o edulcorantes artificiales?

text: I'd like to sit outside; is there any table available on the {{c1::patio}}?
explanation: A 'patio' (or terrace) is an outdoor space adjacent to a cafe or house, typically paved and used for dining or recreation.
usage:
Pattern: patio / outdoor seating / terrace
  • Let's sit on the patio to enjoy the sunny weather.
  • The patio was packed with customers.
spanish: Me gustaría sentarme afuera; ¿hay alguna mesa disponible en la terraza/patio?

text: Can I get a slice of banana bread and please {{c1::toast it}} for me?
explanation: To 'toast' a pastry means to heat it using a toaster or grill until it is warm and slightly crispy on the outside.
usage:
Pattern: toast it / toasted bread
  • Would you like me to toast the bagel for you?
  • I prefer my bread lightly toasted.
spanish: ¿Me da una rebanada de pan de plátano y por favor me la tuesta?

text: I'll buy this mug; does it come with a lid or is it sold {{c1::as is}}?
explanation: 'As is' is a legal and commercial phrase meaning that the product is sold in its current condition, with all faults, without any warranty.
usage:
Pattern: sold as is / buy as is
  • The defective device was sold as is with no returns.
  • We bought the display model as is for a discount.
spanish: Compraré esta taza; ¿viene con tapa o se vende tal como está?

text: Excuse me, where can I find some napkins and {{c1::stirrers}}?
explanation: A 'stirrer' is a small wooden or plastic stick used to mix sugar or milk into a hot beverage like coffee or tea.
usage:
Pattern: wooden stirrers / plastic stirrers
  • Wooden stirrers are more eco-friendly than plastic ones.
  • Please grab a stirrer from the condiment counter.
spanish: Disculpe, ¿dónde puedo encontrar algunas servilletas y agitadores?

text: I'd like to purchase some coffee beans; which blend do you {{c1::recommend for}} espresso?
explanation: To 'recommend for [method]' is the standard collocation used when asking a barista or merchant which product matches a specific preparation method.
usage:
Pattern: recommend for [brewing method / use case]
  • Which coffee beans do you recommend for cold brew?
  • The barista recommended this dark roast for espresso.
spanish: Me gustaría comprar algunos granos de café; ¿qué mezcla me recomienda para espresso?

text: Could you add some cocoa powder or cinnamon on {{c1::top of}} the foam?
explanation: 'On top of' is a prepositional phrase meaning directly on the upper surface of something.
usage:
Pattern: on top of [surface / foam]
  • Sprinkle some chocolate flakes on top of the cream.
  • He put his keys on top of the table.
spanish: ¿Podría agregar un poco de cacao en polvo o canela encima de la espuma?

text: Do you offer free refills on drip coffee if I {{c1::dine in}}?
explanation: To 'dine in' (or eat in) means to consume your food or drink inside the restaurant or cafe premises, as opposed to taking it 'to go'.
usage:
Pattern: dine in / eat in / to go
  • Would you like to dine in or is this to go?
  • Dine-in customers get free water refills.
spanish: ¿Ofrecen recargas gratuitas de café de filtro si consumo aquí?

text: I'll pay with my phone using the contactless {{c1::payment reader}}.
explanation: A 'payment reader' (or card terminal) is the electronic device that scans credit cards or mobile wallets using NFC for transactions.
usage:
Pattern: payment reader / card reader / card terminal
  • Hold your phone near the payment reader to complete the transaction.
  • The payment reader is not responding.
spanish: Pagaré con mi teléfono usando el lector de pagos sin contacto.

text: Can I get a {{c1::coffee}} to go, please?
explanation: In everyday speech, native speakers often say 'Can I get...' instead of 'Could I have...' for a casual request. The phrase is common in cafés and shops.
usage:
Pattern: Can I get ...?
  • Can I get a latte?
  • Can I get a table for two?
spanish: ¿Podría pedir un café para llevar, por favor?

text: What would you {{c1::recommend}} from the menu today?
explanation: Recommend is a common polite phrase when asking for a suggestion from a waiter, barista, or host.
usage:
Pattern: What would you recommend?
  • What would you recommend for lunch?
  • What do you recommend for someone who is vegetarian?
spanish: ¿Qué recomendaría usted del menú hoy?

scenario: Colloquial English: Special of the Day ⭐
text: What’s the {{c1::special of the day}}?
explanation: The special of the day is a restaurant or café promotion or featured dish that the staff recommends.
usage:
Pattern: special of the day / house special
  • What’s the special of the day?
  • The house special is the grilled chicken sandwich.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el especial del día?

text: Could you make it {{c1::extra hot}}?
explanation: In cafés, extra hot means that the drink should be heated more than usual. This phrase is very common in casual service interactions.
usage:
Pattern: extra hot / extra milk / no sugar
  • Could you make it extra hot?
  • Can I get it with oat milk?
spanish: ¿Podría hacerlo extra caliente?

text: Is this {{c1::gluten-free}}?
explanation: Gluten-free is a common diet-related phrase when asking about food ingredients or allergies.
usage:
Pattern: Is this gluten-free?
  • Is this dish gluten-free?
  • Do you have anything without dairy?
spanish: ¿Esto es sin gluten?

text: Can we {{c1::split the bill}}?
explanation: To split the bill means to divide the total cost between people, especially in restaurants or cafés.
usage:
Pattern: split the bill / separate checks
  • Can we split the bill?
  • Could we get separate checks?
spanish: ¿Podemos dividir la cuenta?

scenario: Colloquial English: That Hit the Spot 😋
text: That really {{c1::hit the spot}}.
explanation: This idiom means the food or drink was exactly what you needed and tasted great.
usage:
Pattern: hit the spot
  • That coffee really hit the spot.
  • The soup hit the spot on a cold day.
spanish: Eso sí que me satisfizo mucho.

scenario: Colloquial English: Cut Back on Sugar 🍰
text: I’m trying to {{c1::cut back on}} sugar these days.
explanation: To cut back on something means to reduce the amount of it. It is a very natural phrase in casual conversation.
usage:
Pattern: cut back on
  • I’m trying to cut back on caffeine.
  • We are cutting back on spending.
spanish: Estoy tratando de reducir el azúcar estos días.

text: Explaining to the landlord: "The basement has accumulated {{c1::mold and mildew}} due to the high {{c2::moisture levels}}, so we need a dehumidifier."
explanation: Mold and mildew refer to fungal growths caused by dampness. **Moisture** refers to wetness or condensation in the air.
usage:
Pattern: accumulate mold due to high moisture / run a dehumidifier
  • High moisture levels in the bathroom will cause mold and mildew.
  • We bought a dehumidifier to keep the basement dry.
spanish: Explicándole al propietario: "El sótano ha acumulado moho y humedad debido a los altos niveles de humedad, por lo que necesitamos un deshumidificador".

text: Troubleshooting the setup: "The smart lock has a {{c1::sync issue}} with the hub, so the door sensor doesn't {{c2::trigger the security routine}}."
explanation: Sync issue refers to synchronization failure. **Trigger a routine** means to automatically start a sequence of pre-programmed actions.
usage:
Pattern: have a sync issue / trigger a routine based on [event]
  • My phone is having a sync issue with the server.
  • Connecting the sensor will trigger the alarm routine.
spanish: Resolviendo problemas de la configuración: "La cerradura inteligente tiene un problema de sincronización con el concentrador, por lo que el sensor de la puerta no activa la rutina de seguridad".

text: Discussing the rent: "We need to pay the first month's utilities and the {{c1::security deposit}} before we can inspect the {{c2::floor plan}}."
explanation: Security deposit is a sum of money paid in advance to protect the landlord against damage. **Floor plan** is the scale diagram showing room layouts.
usage:
Pattern: pay a security deposit / check the floor plan
  • The landlord returned our security deposit when we moved out.
  • Let's study the floor plan to decide where to put the sofa.
spanish: Discutiendo el alquiler: "Debemos pagar los servicios del primer mes y el depósito de seguridad antes de poder inspeccionar el plano de distribución de la casa".

text: Instructing the barista: "If your {{c1::grind size}} is too coarse, it causes {{c2::channeling}}, resulting in a watery crema."
explanation: Grind size determines the speed of water flow. **Channeling** occurs when water finds paths of least resistance through the coffee puck instead of extracting evenly.
usage:
Pattern: adjust the grind size / prevent channeling during extraction
  • If the grind size is too fine, the espresso will extract too slowly.
  • Tamping the coffee evenly prevents channeling.
spanish: Instruyendo al barista: "Si el tamaño de la molienda es demasiado grueso, causa canalización, lo que resulta en una crema aguada".

text: Explaining the game: "This is a {{c1::turn-based game}} with specific card mechanics, including a roll of dice to resolve any {{c2::tie-breaker}}."
explanation: Turn-based denotes a gameplay mechanic where players take turns. **Tie-breaker** is the rule or action that determines a winner when scores are equal.
usage:
Pattern: turn-based game / resolve a tie-breaker
  • Civilization is a famous turn-based strategy game.
  • The team won the match in a sudden-death tie-breaker.
spanish: Explicando el juego: "Este es un juego por turnos con mecánicas de cartas específicas, que incluye un lanzamiento de dados para resolver cualquier desempate".

text: Don't forget to {{c1::take out the trash}} before the garbage truck arrives tomorrow morning.
explanation: To 'take out the trash' means to carry refuse or garbage containers from inside the house to the outdoor collection spot.
usage:
Pattern: take out the trash / take out the rubbish
  • It is your turn to take out the trash tonight.
  • I took out the trash before going to bed.
spanish: No olvides sacar la basura antes de que llegue el camión de la basura mañana por la mañana.

text: I need to {{c1::run some errands}}, including dropping off the dry cleaning and buying groceries.
explanation: To 'run errands' means to take short trips to complete daily tasks or chores outside the house (e.g. post office, grocery store, cleaners).
usage:
Pattern: run errands / do errands
  • I have to run some errands this afternoon, so I'll be out of the office.
  • She spent the whole Saturday running errands.
spanish: Necesito hacer algunos mandados/recados, incluyendo dejar la ropa en la tintorería y comprar víveres.

text: The kitchen sink is leaking, so I need to {{c1::call a plumber}} to fix it.
explanation: To 'call a plumber' is a standard collocation for contacting a professional who installs and repairs pipes, fixtures, and water systems.
usage:
Pattern: call a plumber / hire a plumber
  • We had to call a plumber to unclog the drain.
  • The plumber charged $100 just to inspect the pipes.
spanish: El fregadero de la cocina gotea, así que necesito llamar a un plomero para que lo arregle.

text: We need to {{c1::stock up on}} toilet paper and paper towels this weekend.
explanation: To 'stock up on' means to buy a large quantity of something (usually household supplies or food) to keep for future use.
usage:
Pattern: stock up on [goods]
  • People stocked up on water and canned food before the storm.
  • I always stock up on coffee when it goes on sale.
spanish: Necesitamos abastecernos de papel higiénico y toallas de papel este fin de semana.

text: Could you please {{c1::put away the groceries}} in the pantry?
explanation: To 'put away groceries' means to place purchased food items in their proper storage locations (fridge, freezer, cabinets, pantry) after shopping.
usage:
Pattern: put away the groceries
  • Help me put away the groceries before the ice cream melts.
  • She was busy putting away the groceries.
spanish: ¿Podrías por favor guardar las compras en la despensa?

text: I need to do a load of laundry and hang the clothes to dry on the {{c1::clothesline}}.
explanation: A 'clothesline' is a cord or wire stretched between two points outdoors or indoors, used for hanging wet clothes to dry.
usage:
Pattern: hang clothes on the clothesline
  • The clothesline broke under the weight of the wet blankets.
  • I hung my shirts out on the clothesline.
spanish: Necesito lavar una carga de ropa y colgar la ropa para que se seque en el tendedero.

text: Could you please sweep the kitchen floor and {{c1::mop it}} afterwards?
explanation: To 'mop' the floor means to clean it using a mop (a bundle of thick strings or sponge on a stick) and water/cleanser.
usage:
Pattern: mop the floor / mop it up
  • Make sure you mop the floor after cooking.
  • I mopped up the spilled milk.
spanish: ¿Podrías por favor barrer el piso de la cocina y trapearlo después?

text: The roof is leaking during heavy rain; we need to hire a contractor to {{c1::patch it up}}.
explanation: To 'patch up' is a phrasal verb meaning to repair or fix something in a quick, temporary, or modular way.
usage:
Pattern: patch [something] up / repair
  • We patched up the hole in the drywall.
  • The plumber patched up the leaking pipe until we could replace it.
spanish: El techo gotea durante la lluvia intensa; necesitamos contratar a un contratista para que lo parche/repare.

text: Don't forget to lock the back door and close all the windows before you {{c1::head out}}.
explanation: To 'head out' is an informal phrasal verb meaning to leave a place or begin a journey.
usage:
Pattern: head out / leave / set off
  • We are planning to head out early tomorrow morning.
  • I'm heading out to the grocery store.
spanish: No olvides cerrar con llave la puerta trasera y cerrar todas las ventanas antes de salir.

text: We need to replace the burnt-out {{c1::light bulb}} in the hallway.
explanation: A 'light bulb' is the glass bulb that fits into a lamp or fixture to provide light electrically.
usage:
Pattern: replace the light bulb / LED bulb
  • We bought energy-efficient LED light bulbs for the living room.
  • The light bulb in my desk lamp just went out.
spanish: Necesitamos reemplazar la bombilla/foco quemado en el pasillo.

text: Could you help me clear the table and {{c1::load the dishwasher}}?
explanation: To 'load the dishwasher' means to place dirty plates, glasses, and utensils inside the dishwashing machine before running a cycle.
usage:
Pattern: load the dishwasher / unload the dishwasher
  • It is easier to load the dishwasher if you rinse the plates first.
  • She unloaded the dishwasher this morning.
spanish: ¿Podrías ayudarme a limpiar la mesa y llenar el lavavajillas?

text: We should tidy up the living room before our guests {{c1::arrive}}.
explanation: To 'arrive' is the standard verb meaning to reach a destination, commonly used for visitors or packages.
usage:
Pattern: guests arrive / package arrives
  • The guests arrived at 7:00 PM.
  • We are waiting for the package to arrive.
spanish: Deberíamos ordenar la sala antes de que lleguen nuestros invitados.

text: I need to water the plants in the garden and trim the {{c1::lawn}}.
explanation: A 'lawn' is an area of grass in a garden or park that is kept cut short and neat.
usage:
Pattern: mow the lawn / trim the lawn / cut the grass
  • He spent the afternoon mowing the lawn.
  • Our lawn needs to be watered regularly during the summer.
spanish: Necesito regar las plantas del jardín y podar el césped.

text: The battery in the smoke detector is dying; it keeps making a {{c1::chirping sound}}.
explanation: A 'chirping sound' is a short, high-pitched repeating noise made by electronic devices (like smoke detectors) to warn that the battery is low.
usage:
Pattern: chirping sound / beep sound
  • The smoke detector was making an annoying chirping sound all night.
  • Replace the battery to stop the chirping sound.
spanish: La batería del detector de humo se está agotando; sigue haciendo un pitido intermitente.

text: Please put the leftovers in a container and store them in the {{c1::fridge}}.
explanation: 'Fridge' is the very common abbreviation for refrigerator, the appliance used to keep food and drinks cold.
usage:
Pattern: put in the fridge / keep in the refrigerator
  • Is there any milk left in the fridge?
  • Store the meat in the fridge immediately.
