spanish: BLUF — Bottom Line Up Front: empieza cada actualización con la conclusión o la acción necesaria, antes de cualquier contexto o antecedentes. Principio estándar en comunicación ejecutiva.

scenario: Executive English 📊: Closing with Next Steps
text: To close an executive update with clarity, use: "{{c1::Next steps: [action], owned by [person], by [date].}}"
explanation: Executive updates that end without clear next steps waste the meeting. The three-element formula — action + owner + deadline — is the minimum viable closure. 'Owned by' is more accountable than 'handled by' or 'assigned to'.
usage:
• "Next step: finalize the vendor contract, owned by Sarah, by end of Wednesday."
  • "Two next steps: (1) security review — owned by IT, by Friday; (2) stakeholder sign-off — owned by me, by next Monday."
spanish: 'Próximos pasos: [acción], a cargo de [persona], para el [fecha].' — la fórmula mínima para cerrar cualquier actualización ejecutiva con claridad.

scenario: Executive English 📊: Proactive Bad News Delivery
text: When delivering bad news proactively, lead with: "{{c1::I want to give you a heads-up before this becomes a bigger issue.}}"
explanation: This phrase does critical work: (1) it positions you as proactive and trustworthy — you're not hiding problems; (2) 'heads-up' is a neutral, informal signal that shifts register to collaborative; (3) 'before this becomes bigger' shows situational awareness. Never let executives learn bad news from others first.
usage:
• "I want to give you a heads-up before this becomes a bigger issue: the deployment we ran last night introduced a latency spike. We've isolated the cause and are rolling it back."
  • "Quick heads-up: we're behind on the integration. Here's the recovery plan."
spanish: 'Quiero avisarte antes de que esto se convierta en un problema mayor.' — fundamental para generar confianza como líder: siempre lleva las malas noticias tú primero.

scenario: Executive English 📊: Progress vs. Plan Framing
text: To frame progress against a plan, use: "{{c1::We're on track / slightly behind / ahead of schedule on [milestone], here's where we land.}}"
explanation: 'On track', 'slightly behind', and 'ahead of schedule' are executive-grade traffic light signals. They allow rapid comprehension. Always anchor the signal to a specific milestone, not a vague 'the project'. The phrase 'here's where we land' invites a zoomed-out view.
usage:
• "We're slightly behind on Phase 2 of the data migration. We'll land 3 days late but within budget."
  • "We're actually ahead of schedule on the model training — we can start evaluation a week early."
  • "On track for the go-live date, but I want to flag a dependency that could shift things."
spanish: 'Estamos en tiempo / ligeramente atrasados / adelantados en [hito], así es como termina.' — señales de semáforo para ejecutivos que permiten comprensión rápida.

text: When you don't have full information yet, use: "{{c1::We don't have the full picture yet, but here's what we know and what we're doing to close the gap.}}"
explanation: Executive environments punish both overconfidence (wrong) and paralysis (slow). This phrase threads the needle: it signals intellectual honesty (we don't know everything) while projecting leadership (we're actively working it). The phrase 'close the gap' is a management-register idiom for filling a knowledge deficit.
usage:
• "We don't have the full picture yet on the outage root cause. What we know: the issue started at 2:14 AM. We've isolated it to the caching layer and have the team focused on a fix."
  • "I'll give you a preliminary read: we're not sure yet if this affects all regions or just US-East."
spanish: 'No tenemos el panorama completo todavía, pero esto es lo que sabemos y lo que estamos haciendo para llenar los vacíos.' — comunica honestidad intelectual sin perder autoridad.

text: Lead with data in executive updates: "{{c1::The numbers this week: [metric A], [metric B]. The story behind them is...}}"
explanation: 'The numbers this week' is a crisp, professional opener that signals data-driven communication. Executives expect quantitative signals before qualitative narrative. The bridging phrase 'the story behind them' acknowledges that numbers need context — but you're giving the data first, not last.
usage:
• "The numbers this week: CSAT at 87%, up 4 points. First contact resolution at 91%. The story behind them: the new escalation routing we implemented Tuesday is already showing impact."
  • "Quick metrics snapshot: P1 incidents — zero. Response SLA — 98.7% met. Model latency — averaging 320ms, down from 480ms."
spanish: 'Los números de esta semana: [métrica A], [métrica B]. La historia detrás de ellos es...' — comunicación ejecutiva orientada a datos primero, narrativa después.

text: To escalate an issue to leadership correctly, frame it as: "{{c1::I'm bringing this to your attention because it's beyond my authority / requires your visibility.}}"
explanation: Escalation is a professional skill, not a sign of weakness. This phrasing is important for two reasons: (1) 'beyond my authority' signals you know your mandate and are respecting organizational hierarchy; (2) 'requires your visibility' frames it as strategic awareness, not just passing a problem upward.
usage:
• "I'm escalating this because the vendor SLA breach requires legal involvement, which is beyond my authority to initiate."
  • "I wanted to flag this to you directly — the potential churn risk on this account requires your visibility given the ARR involved."
spanish: 'Lo traigo a tu atención porque está más allá de mi autoridad / requiere tu visibilidad.' — escalar es una habilidad profesional, no una señal de debilidad.

text: In casual speech, {{c1::want to}} often becomes {{c2::wanna}}, and {{c3::going to}} becomes {{c4::gonna}}.
explanation: These reductions are very common in everyday conversation and are part of natural connected speech. They sound smoother and less formal.
usage:
Pattern: wanna / gonna
  • I wanna grab coffee.
  • We’re gonna be late.
spanish: En el habla casual, 'want to' suele convertirse en 'wanna' y 'going to' en 'gonna'.

text: The phrase {{c1::I don’t know}} is often reduced to {{c2::I dunno}} in fast speech.
explanation: Connected speech frequently reduces function words and weakens vowels. This helps speech sound more natural and less mechanical.
usage:
Pattern: I dunno / I don’t know
  • I dunno what to do.
  • I don’t know yet.
spanish: La frase 'I don’t know' suele reducirse a 'I dunno' en el habla rápida.

text: In casual speech, {{c1::sort of}} and {{c2::kind of}} are often pronounced as weaker, faster chunks.
explanation: These phrases are common when speakers want to soften a statement or sound less absolute.
usage:
Pattern: sort of / kind of
  • I’m sort of tired.
  • It’s kind of expensive.
spanish: En el habla casual, 'sort of' y 'kind of' suelen sonar como grupos más débiles y rápidos.

scenario: Phonetics: Gonna / Wanna in Real Speech 🎧
text: When speakers say {{c1::wanna}} or {{c2::gonna}}, the consonants and vowels blend together to create a smoother rhythm.
explanation: This is one of the clearest examples of connected speech. The result is a shorter, more fluid sentence.
usage:
Pattern: gonna / wanna
  • I wanna leave.
  • She’s gonna call later.
spanish: Cuando los hablantes dicen 'wanna' o 'gonna', las consonantes y vocales se mezclan para crear un ritmo más fluido.

text: Function words like {{c1::to}}, {{c2::of}}, and {{c3::and}} often reduce to a weak schwa sound in fast speech.
explanation: This is a key feature of natural English rhythm and helps learners sound more native-like.
usage:
Pattern: schwa reduction
  • What do you want?
  • I’m gonna go to the store.
spanish: Palabras funcionales como 'to', 'of' y 'and' a menudo se reducen a un sonido débil en el habla rápida.

text: When one word ends in a consonant and the next begins with a vowel, the sound often {{c1::links}} smoothly.
explanation: Linking helps create the fluid rhythm of spoken English. It is especially useful in casual everyday phrases.
usage:
Pattern: linking / resyllabification
  • go out
  • an apple
spanish: Cuando una palabra termina en consonante y la siguiente empieza con vocal, el sonido suele enlazarse suavemente.

text: Native speakers usually give the most important word in the sentence the strongest {{c1::stress}}.
explanation: Sentence stress is crucial for clarity and intelligibility. It also helps listeners understand the meaning instantly.
usage:
Pattern: sentence stress / content words
  • I WANT a coffee.
  • We’re GOING to the airport.
spanish: Los hablantes nativos normalmente dan mayor énfasis a la palabra más importante de la oración.

text: Natural English often comes in {{c1::chunks}} rather than isolated words, especially in everyday speech.
explanation: Chunking helps learners process phrases like 'gonna grab a coffee' as one smooth unit instead of several separate words.
usage:
Pattern: chunking / phrase stress
  • gonna grab a coffee
  • let me think
spanish: El inglés natural suele venir en bloques de expresión en lugar de palabras aisladas, especialmente en el habla cotidiana.

text: In American English, a /t/ between two vowels (when the second is unstressed) turns into a {{c1::Flap T [ɾ]}}, making "put it on" sound like "pur-id-on".
explanation: The Flap T (or tap) is a sound made by briefly tapping the tongue tip against the alveolar ridge. It is acoustically identical to the Spanish single 'r' or a light 'd'. It allows speakers to bridge vowels without stopping the voice.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • Get it out → "ged-id-out"
  • What a day → "whad-a-day"
  • Write a book → "wry-duh-book"

Reference: Learn more on Rachel's English (search: 'Rachel's English Flap T').
spanish: En inglés americano, la /t/ entre dos vocales se convierte en una 'Flap T' [ɾ] (como la 'r' suave en español). Por eso, "put it on" suena como "pur-id-on".

text: When a word ending in /t/ meets a word beginning with /y/, they merge into a {{c1::/tʃ/ ("ch" as in chair)}} sound. E.g., "meet you" is pronounced "meetchu".
explanation: This is a type of assimilation called coalescence (or palatalization). The mouth prepares for the palatal /y/ while pronouncing the alveolar /t/, resulting in the postalveolar affricate /tʃ/.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • don't you → "donchoo"
  • what you need → "whachoo need"
  • can't you → "canchoo"

Reference: Look up "Assimilation in English" on English with Greg.
spanish: Cuando una palabra que termina en /t/ se une con una que empieza con /y/, se fusionan en el sonido /tʃ/ ('ch'). Ejemplo: "meet you" → "meetchu".

text: When a word ending in /d/ meets a word beginning with /y/, they merge into a {{c1::/dʒ/ ("j" as in judge)}} sound. E.g., "would you" is pronounced "woodja".
explanation: Similar to T+Y coalescence, the voiced alveolar stop /d/ and palatal glide /y/ combine to form the voiced postalveolar affricate /dʒ/.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • could you → "couldja"
  • did you → "didja"
  • find you → "findja"

Reference: Practice shadowing with "Hadar Shemesh connected speech" on YouTube.
spanish: Cuando una palabra que termina en /d/ se une con una que empieza con /y/, se fusionan en el sonido /dʒ/ ('y' o 'j' fuerte). Ejemplo: "would you" → "woodja".

text: Before a consonant sound, a final /t/ is often not released, but stopped in the throat as a {{c1::Glottal Stop [ʔ]}}. E.g., "not yet" becomes "no' yet".
explanation: A glottal stop is produced by blocking the airflow in the vocal tract (specifically the glottis) rather than using the tongue to release air. This is a hallmark of native speakers trying to conserve energy.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • flat tire → "fla' tire"
  • button → "bu'n"
  • football → "foo'ball"

Watch: "Glottal Stop vs. Stop T" on Rachel's English.
spanish: Antes de una consonante, la /t/ final a menudo se detiene en la garganta (parada glotal [ʔ]) en lugar de liberarse. Ejemplo: "not yet" → "no' yet".

text: To maintain a steady rhythm, native speakers link a final consonant to a following initial vowel, so that "{{c1::turn on}}" sounds like "tur-non".
explanation: This is known as liaison. In English, a word-final consonant behaves as if it belongs to the onset of the next syllable if that syllable starts with a vowel, avoiding breaks in airflow.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • an apple → "a-napple"
  • hold on → "hol-don"
  • read it → "rea-dit"
spanish: Para mantener un ritmo constante, los nativos enlazan la consonante final con la vocal inicial siguiente. Ejemplo: "turn on" suena como "tur-non".

text: When linking a rounded vowel (e.g. oo, oh, ow) to another vowel, native speakers insert an intrusive {{c1::/w/ sound}}. E.g., "go out" is pronounced "go-w-out".
explanation: When the lips are rounded for the first vowel, the mouth naturally glides through a /w/ shape to transition to the next vowel, preventing an unnatural glottal break.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • do it → "do-w-it"
  • you are → "you-w-are"
  • how about → "how-w-about"
spanish: Al conectar una vocal redondeada (como /u/ o /o/) con otra vocal, se inserta naturalmente un sonido /w/ de transición. Ejemplo: "go out" → "go-w-out".

text: When linking a front vowel (e.g. ee, ay, ie) to another vowel, native speakers insert an intrusive {{c1::/j/ (or 'y') sound}}. E.g., "he is" is pronounced "he-y-is".
explanation: Because the tongue is high and forward for the first vowel, the transition to the next vowel naturally produces a palatal glide /j/ ("y" sound).
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • she agrees → "she-y-agrees"
  • I am → "I-y-am"
  • the end → "the-y-end"
spanish: Al conectar una vocal anterior/alta (como /i/ o /ai/) con otra vocal, se introduce un sonido de transición /j/ ("y"). Ejemplo: "he is" → "he-y-is".

text: When a word ends with the same consonant sound that the next word begins with, native speakers {{c1::elongate or hold the sound}} instead of pronouncing it twice.
explanation: This is called gemination. Pronouncing both consonants individually requires stopping and restarting airflow, which hinders speech flow. Instead, a single, elongated consonant is produced.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • black cat → "bla-cat" (held /k/)
  • bad dog → "ba-dog" (held /d/)
  • social life → "socia-life" (held /l/)
  • gas station → "ga-station" (held /s/)
spanish: Cuando una palabra termina con la misma consonante con la que empieza la siguiente, se alarga el sonido de la consonante en lugar de pronunciarla dos veces. Ejemplo: "black cat" → "bla-cat".

text: In fast speech, the /h/ sound in unstressed pronouns (he, him, his, her) is often {{c1::elided (dropped)}}, causing "tell him" to sound like "tell-im".
explanation: Because grammatical pronouns are unstressed, speakers drop the /h/ to save vocal effort. The final consonant of the preceding word then links directly to the pronoun's remaining vowel.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • ask her → "ask-er"
  • what did he say → "what-dide-say"
  • is he here → "is-e-here"

Warning: Do not drop the /h/ if the pronoun is stressed or at the beginning of a sentence.
spanish: En el habla rápida, la /h/ en pronombres átonos (him, her, etc.) suele omitirse, haciendo que "tell him" suene como "tell-im".

text: When /t/ or /d/ is sandwiched between two other consonants, it is often {{c1::dropped entirely}} to ease pronunciation. E.g., "last night" becomes "las-night".
explanation: Three consonants in a row (like /st/ and /n/ in "last night") require complex tongue movement. Dropping the middle alveolar stop (/t/ or /d/) allows a smoother transition.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • next door → "nex-door"
  • hold tight → "hole-tight"
  • you and me → "you n me"
  • we went to → "we wen-to"
spanish: Cuando /t/ o /d/ están entre otras dos consonantes, a menudo se omiten por completo. Ejemplo: "last night" → "las-night".

text: In unstressed positions, the preposition "to" is reduced from /tuː/ to {{c1::/tə/ (sounding like "tuh")}} before consonants. E.g., "try to run" → "try-tuh run".
explanation: English is stress-timed. Functional words like "to" reduce their vowels to the neutral schwa /ə/ when they do not carry main sentence stress, helping emphasize content words.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • nice to meet you → "nice-tuh meet you"
  • I need to go → "I need-tuh go"
  • have to do → "have-tuh do" (becomes "hafta")
spanish: La preposición "to" en posiciones átonas se reduce a "tuh" /tə/ (schwa). Ejemplo: "try to run" → "try-tuh run".

text: The preposition "for" is unstressed in sentences and reduces from /fɔːr/ to {{c1::/fər/ (sounding like "fer")}}, as in "this is for you" → "this is fer-you".
explanation: Vowel reduction turns the full vowel /ɔː/ into an r-colored schwa /ɚ/ or /ər/. The full pronunciation /fɔː/ is reserved for when the word is stressed or at the end of a clause.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • for free → "fer-free"
  • for example → "fer-example"
  • waiting for a cab → "waiting fer-a cab"
spanish: La palabra "for" se reduce a "fer" /fər/ cuando no está acentuada en una frase. Ejemplo: "this is for you" suena como "this is fer-you".

text: The conjunction "and" is unstressed and heavily reduced, dropping both its vowel and its final /d/, leaving only {{c1::/n/ or /ən/}}. E.g., "black and white" becomes "black n white".
explanation: In rapid speech, "and" is shortened to a syllabic /n/ sound. The /d/ is only pronounced if followed by a vowel and spoken slowly, or when emphasizing addition.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • rock and roll → "rock n roll"
  • you and me → "you n me"
  • chips and dip → "chips n dip"
spanish: La conjunción "and" pierde su vocal y su /d/, reduciéndose al simple sonido nasal "n" /n/ o /ən/. Ejemplo: "black and white" suena como "black n white".

text: The preposition "of" reduces its vowel and drops the /v/ before consonants, sounding like {{c1::/ə/ ("uh" or "a")}}. E.g., "cup of tea" becomes "cup-a tea".
explanation: The /v/ is elided when followed by a consonant to avoid awkward transitions. When followed by a vowel, the /v/ is usually kept and linked (e.g., "out of office" → "out-uh-voffice").
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • lots of time → "lots-a time"
  • piece of cake → "piece-a cake"
  • out of here → "outta here"
spanish: La preposición "of" se acorta a un simple sonido "a" /ə/ (schwa) antes de una consonante. Ejemplo: "cup of tea" → "cup-a tea".

text: When a word ending in /s/ meets a word starting with /y/, they assimilate into a {{c1::/ʃ/ ("sh" as in shoe)}} sound. E.g., "bless you" becomes "blesh-you".
explanation: The alveolar fricative /s/ and the palatal approximant /y/ coalesce into the postalveolar fricative /ʃ/. The tongue shifts back slightly, combining the two articulations into one.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • this year → "thisheer"
  • dress you → "dresh-you"
  • press your luck → "presh-your luck"
spanish: El encuentro de /s/ y /y/ produce el sonido de la "sh" inglesa /ʃ/. Ejemplo: "bless you" suena como "blesh-you".

text: When a word ending in /z/ meets a word starting with /y/, they assimilate into a {{c1::/ʒ/ ("zh" as in measure)}} sound. E.g., "how's your day" becomes "how-zher day".
explanation: The voiced alveolar fricative /z/ and the palatal approximant /y/ coalesce into the voiced postalveolar fricative /ʒ/. This is the voiced equivalent of the S+Y assimilation.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • where's your car → "where-zher car"
  • as you know → "azh-you know"
  • close your eyes → "clozh-your eyes"
spanish: La combinación de /z/ y /y/ genera el sonido /ʒ/ (la 'y' argentina o la 's' en 'measure'). Ejemplo: "how's your" suena como "how-zher".

text: At the end of words or before consonants, the "Dark L" [ɫ] is often vocalized, sounding like a {{c1::vowel-like /oʊ/ or /ʊ/ ("uh")}} rather than a consonant. E.g., "feel" → "fee-uh".
explanation: In many dialects of English, the tongue tip does not touch the roof of the mouth for a word-final L. Instead, only the back of the tongue is raised, turning the L into a back vowel sound.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • milk → "mi-uk"
  • tall → "taw-uh"
  • real → "ree-uh"
spanish: La 'L' al final de las palabras (Dark L) a menudo se vocaliza en la parte trasera de la boca, sonando similar a una 'u' o 'o'. Ejemplo: "feel" suena como "fee-uh".

text: In casual speech, the velar nasal sound /ŋ/ at the end of "-ing" verbs is reduced to the alveolar nasal {{c1::/n/}}, so that "working" is spoken as "workin'".
explanation: This is a highly common reduction where the point of articulation shifts from the back of the mouth (velar /ŋ/) to the front (alveolar /n/). This is often represented spelling-wise with an apostrophe.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • What are you doing? → "What-ya doin'?"
  • nothing → "nothin'"
  • running late → "runnin' late"
spanish: En contextos informales, el sonido "-ing" nasal se acorta al sonido simple de la "n". Ejemplo: "working" suena como "workin'".

text: In native speech, the positive auxiliary "can" is reduced to {{c1::/kən/ ("kun")}}, while the negative "can't" retains a clear stressed vowel /kænt/ or uses a glottal stop /kæn'/.
explanation: Because positive "can" is a grammatical helper, it is unstressed and reduces to schwa. The negative "can't" contains the core negative meaning, so it remains stressed and retains its full vowel, making stress the primary cue to tell them apart.
usage:
Contrastive Drills:
  • I can do it → "I kun do it"
  • I can't do it → "I caan' do it" (longer vowel, stop T)
spanish: El afirmativo "can" se reduce a un rápido "kun" /kən/, mientras que el negativo "can't" se acentúa con una vocal larga y clara o parada glotal. La clave está en el ritmo y el acento.

text: In highly casual speech, the entire phrase "What are you..." is reduced and merged into the single word {{c1::"whatcha" /wɒtʃə/}}. E.g., "What are you doing?" becomes "Whatcha doin'?".
explanation: This involves multiple reductions: "are" is reduced to a schwa or omitted, and "what" links to "you" via T+Y coalescence, forming "whatcha".
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • What are you talking about? → "Whatcha talkin' 'bout?"
  • What are you thinking? → "Whatcha thinkin'?"

Reference: Learn about spoken reductions in Rachel's English videos.
spanish: En el habla informal, toda la frase "What are you..." se comprime y pronuncia como "whatcha" (/wɒtʃə/). Ejemplo: "What are you doing?" → "Whatcha doin'?".

text: In American English, a /t/ following a /n/ in an unstressed syllable is often {{c1::omitted (dropped)}}, making "twenty" sound like "tweny" and "internet" sound like "innernet".
explanation: Because /n/ and /t/ are both pronounced at the alveolar ridge, and /n/ is nasalized, the nasal airflow dominates when the syllable is unstressed, leading the speaker to skip the stop /t/ completely.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • twenty → "tweny"
  • internet → "innernet"
  • center → "cener"
  • international → "innernational"
spanish: En inglés americano, la /t/ después de una /n/ en una sílaba átona a menudo se omite. Ejemplo: "twenty" suena como "tweny" e "internet" como "innernet".

text: In non-rhotic accents (e.g., British RP), speakers insert a {{c1::Linking or Intrusive R}} between two words when the first ends in a vowel and the second starts with a vowel, as in "law and order" sounding like "law-r-and-order".
explanation: Non-rhotic speakers do not pronounce 'r' at the end of words (e.g., 'car' is /kɑː/). However, if the next word starts with a vowel, they activate it as a 'Linking R' (e.g., 'car is' → /kɑːr ɪz/). If there is no 'r' in the spelling but the vowels clash, they insert an 'Intrusive R' (e.g., 'idea of' → /aɪˈdɪə.rəv/).
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • law and order → "law-r-an-order"
  • idea of it → "idea-r-of-it"
  • media event → "media-r-event"
spanish: En acentos no róticos (como el británico RP), los hablantes insertan una R de enlace ('Linking/Intrusive R') entre palabras cuando una termina en vocal y la otra empieza en vocal. Ejemplo: "law and order" → "law-r-and-order".

text: Highly frequent word combinations are compressed into single colloquial units: "going to" becomes {{c1::gonna}}, "want to" becomes {{c2::wanna}}, and "don't know" becomes {{c3::dunno}}.
explanation: Lexical chunking occurs because these high-frequency phrases are retrieved as a single cognitive unit, allowing the articulatory organs to take extreme shortcuts.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • going to go → "gonna go"
  • want to eat → "wanna eat"
  • I don't know → "I dunno"
  • got to leave → "gotta leave"
spanish: Combinaciones de palabras muy frecuentes se comprimen en unidades coloquiales: "going to" se convierte en "gonna", "want to" en "wanna", y "don't know" en "dunno".

text: When a stop consonant (/p, t, k, b, d, g/) occurs at the end of a word or before another consonant, native speakers often use an {{c1::unreleased stop [̚]}}, meaning they block the air without letting it explode out.
explanation: Instead of releasing the air pressure with a small burst of air (as in citation form), the airflow is stopped by the tongue or lips and held, resulting in an abrupt cut-off of the preceding vowel.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • stop it → "stop-it" (held lip closure)
  • hot dog → "hot-dog" (tongue stays on alveolar ridge)
  • cat food → "cat-food" (abrupt stop before 'f')
spanish: Cuando una consonante oclusiva (/p, t, k, b, d, g/) está al final de una palabra o antes de otra consonante, se suele retener la liberación de aire (oclusión retenida), cortando el sonido de golpe.

text: In American English, a /d/ between vowels behaves exactly like a /t/, turning into a {{c1::Flap D [ɾ]}} (which sounds identical to a Flap T), making "ladder" homophonous with "latter".
explanation: Because the tongue tap is so fast, the distinction between the voiced /d/ and voiceless /t/ is neutralized in this position, resulting in the same alveolar flap [ɾ] sound.
usage:
Contrastive Pairs (Sound Identical):
  • ladder vs. latter (/ˈlæ.ɾər/)
  • riding vs. writing (/ˈraɪ.ɾɪŋ/)
  • beading vs. beating (/ˈbiː.ɾɪŋ/)
spanish: En inglés americano, la /d/ entre vocales se convierte en una 'Flap D' [ɾ] (idéntica a la Flap T), haciendo que palabras como "ladder" (escalera) y "latter" (último) suenen igual.

text: Before voiceless stops (/p, t, k/) at the end of syllables, speakers often reinforce the consonant with a simultaneous closing of the vocal cords, a process called {{c1::Pre-Glottalization or Glottal Reinforcement}}.
explanation: The glottis closes briefly just before or during the oral closure for the stop consonant. This makes the onset of the consonant sound sharper and prevents voicing.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • napkin → "na[ʔ]pkin"
  • actor → "a[ʔ]ctor"
  • backward → "ba[ʔ]kward"
spanish: Antes de las consonantes sordas /p, t, k/ al final de una sílaba, los hablantes a menudo cierran las cuerdas vocales simultáneamente, reforzando el sonido (pre-glotalización).

text: Complex auxiliary verb structures are heavily contracted in native conversation: "What did you do?" becomes {{c1::"whadja do?"}} and "would have been" becomes {{c2::"woulda bin"}}.
explanation: Auxiliary and modal verbs carry low semantic weight compared to main verbs and nouns. In rapid speech, they undergo extreme vowel reduction and coalescence to speed up delivery.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • What did you do? → "whadja do?"
  • Did you ever...? → "jever...?"
  • would have been → "woulda bin"
  • What do you mean? → "whadaya mean?"
spanish: Las estructuras de verbos auxiliares complejos se contraen de forma extrema: "What did you do?" se convierte en "whadja do?" y "would have been" en "woulda bin".

text: When an unstressed vowel is followed by /l/, /n/, or /m/, the vowel is often dropped completely, and the consonant itself becomes the nucleus of the syllable, known as a {{c1::Syllabic Consonant [n̩, l̩, m̩]}} (e.g., "button" → /ˈbʌ.tn̩/).
explanation: Instead of pronouncing the schwa /ə/ and then the consonant, the tongue transitions directly from the preceding consonant to the nasal/lateral position, eliminating the vowel sound entirely.
usage:
Practice Drills:
  • button → "butt-n" (/ˈbʌ.tn̩/)
  • little → "litt-l" (/ˈlɪ.tl̩/)
  • rhythm → "rhyth-m" (/ˈrɪð.m̩/)
  • sudden → "sudd-n" (/ˈsʌd.n̩/)
spanish: Cuando una vocal átona es seguida por /l/, /n/ o /m/, la vocal se elimina y la consonante actúa como núcleo de la sílaba (consonante silábica). Ejemplo: "button" suena como "butt-n".

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Put it in a bottle of water."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Pu-di-tin a bo-dle of wa-der.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Flap T
The /t/ sounds are located between vowels in unstressed syllables. They tap against the alveolar ridge, sounding like a Spanish 'r' or light 'd', linking all words together.
usage: Formal: Put it in a bottle of water. → Connected: Pu-di-tin a bo-dle of wa-der.
spanish: Spanish: Ponlo en una botella de agua.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "The international center has twenty computers."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::The innernational cener has tweny computers.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Nasal T-Deletion
Unstressed /t/ directly following /n/ is omitted because the nasal articulation dominates at the alveolar ridge, letting speakers skip the stop sound.
usage: Formal: The international center has twenty computers. → Connected: The innernational cener has tweny computers.
spanish: Spanish: El centro internacional tiene veinte computadoras.

scenario: Pronunciation Drill: Linking and Intrusive R 🗣️
text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "I have no idea of the law and order."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::I have no idea-r-of the law-r-and order.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Linking and Intrusive R
In non-rhotic accents, an 'r' sound is inserted to bridge contiguous vowel sounds, preventing a glottal break (hiatus).
usage: Formal: I have no idea of the law and order. → Connected: I have no idea-r-of the law-r-and order.
spanish: Spanish: No tengo idea de la ley y el orden.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "I can't go to the football match."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::I can' ɡo to the foo'ball match.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Glottal Stop
Before another consonant, final /t/ is not released with air; instead, the airflow is cut off briefly in the glottis (throat).
usage: Formal: I can't go to the football match. → Connected: I can' ɡo to the foo'ball match.
spanish: Spanish: No puedo ir al partido de fútbol.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Drop it in the hot dog bag."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Drop-it in the hah-dog bag.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Unreleased Stops
The final stop consonants /p/, /t/, and /g/ are unreleased (held), cutting off the vowels abruptly without a puff of air.
usage: Formal: Drop it in the hot dog bag. → Connected: Drop-it in the hah-dog bag.
spanish: Spanish: Déjalo en la bolsa de los perros calientes.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "What did you do? Did you ever think it would have been easy?"
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Whadja do? Jever think it woulda bin easy?}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Advanced Auxiliary Chunks
The auxiliary sequences compress heavily via coalescence (what did you → whadja; did you → jever) and reduction (would have → woulda).
usage: Formal: What did you do? Did you ever think it would have been easy? → Connected: Whadja do? Jever think it woulda bin easy?
spanish: Spanish: ¿Qué hiciste? ¿Alguna vez pensaste que habría sido fácil?

text: Unlike syllable-timed languages like Spanish, English is a {{c1::stress-timed language (isocronía)}}, meaning the duration between stressed syllables remains constant, while unstressed syllables are compressed.
explanation: This means that 'CATS CHASE MICE' and 'The CATS have been CHASING the MICE' take roughly the same amount of time to say. Unstressed words like 'have been' or 'the' are squeezed into tiny fractions of a second, forcing reductions like the schwa.
usage: Contrast: CATS CHASE MICE (~1.5s) vs. The CATS have been CHASING the MICE (~1.5s).
spanish: A diferencia de los idiomas con ritmo silábico (como el español), el inglés es un idioma con ritmo acentual (isocronía), lo que significa que el tiempo entre sílabas acentuadas es constante y las sílabas átonas se comprimen.

text: The General American (GenAm) accent is characterized by full rhoticity, extensive use of {{c1::Flap T and Flap D}} between vowels, and the dropping of /t/ after /n/ in unstressed syllables.
explanation: Rhoticity means 'r' is pronounced everywhere it is written (e.g. 'car' /kɑːr/). Flapping turns intervocalic /t/ and /d/ into [ɾ], making 'latter' and 'ladder' homophones. Nasal T-deletion turns 'internet' to 'innernet'.
usage: GenAm features: internet → 'innernet' | better → 'bed-er' | water → 'wa-der'.
spanish: El acento americano general se caracteriza por su roticidad completa, el uso de la T y D suaves (flaps) entre vocales, y la caída de la T después de la N en sílabas átonas.

text: The British RP and Cockney accents are non-rhotic and feature prominent {{c1::glottal stops [ʔ]}} in place of intervocalic or final /t/ (e.g. 'bottle' → 'bo'el'), along with intrusive /r/ linking.
explanation: Non-rhotic means 'r' is silent unless followed by a vowel. Glottal stop [ʔ] replaces /t/ by closing the vocal folds briefly in the throat. Intrusive R bridges vowel-to-vowel gaps (e.g. 'law and order' → 'law-r-and-order').
usage: British features: bottle → 'bo'el' | law and order → 'law-r-and-order' | idea of it → 'idea-r-of-it'.
spanish: Los acentos británicos RP y Cockney no son róticos, presentan abundante oclusión glotal en lugar de la T intermedia o final, e insertan una R intrusiva de enlace.

text: Southern US English and AAVE (African American Vernacular English) often feature {{c1::monophthongization}} of /aɪ/ (e.g. 'my' → 'mah'), G-dropping, and final consonant cluster reduction (e.g. 'cold' → 'cole').
explanation: Monophthongization turns a two-part vowel glide (diphthong) into a single long vowel. Consonant cluster reduction drops the final stop consonant when sandwiched at the end of words.
usage: Dialect examples: my friend → 'mah friend' | cold night → 'cole night' | running → 'runnin'' | fixing to → 'finna'.
spanish: El inglés del sur de EE. UU. y el AAVE suelen presentar monoptongación (reducción de diptongos a una sola vocal larga, como 'my' → 'mah'), omisión de G final, y reducción de grupos consonánticos finales ('cold' → 'cole').

text: Australian English is non-rhotic, features extensive Flap T usage, and is famous for a vowel shift where the diphthong /eɪ/ sounds closer to {{c1::/aɪ/ (like the 'i' in 'bite')}}.
explanation: Australian vowels are shifted; for example, 'today' sounds similar to 'to-die' to American ears. It also relies heavily on colloquial abbreviations ending in '-ie' or '-o' (e.g. 'breakfast' → 'brekkie', 'service station' → 'servo').
usage: Aussie features: today → 'to-die' | breakfast → 'brekkie' | service station → 'servo'.
spanish: El inglés australiano no es rótico, usa flaps frecuentes y presenta un cambio vocálico donde el diptongo /eɪ/ se acerca a /aɪ/ ('today' suena similar a 'to-die'), además de abundantes abreviaciones coloquiales.

text: The daily 15-minute auditory decoding routine consists of: 5 mins of active phonetic transcription, 5 mins of {{c1::comparison with subtitles & identifying patterns}}, and 5 mins of shadowing (3x repetitions).
explanation: This routine trains the brain to bridge the gap between spelling and native fast pronunciation. Regular transcription exercises target the 'Jungle Stage' of listening.
usage:
Daily Protocol:
  • Min 0-5: Active transcription (write down what you hear phonetically)
  • Min 5-10: Analyze patterns (compare with actual subtitles)
  • Min 10-15: Shadowing (repeat 3x trying to mimic speed)
spanish: La rutina diaria de 15 minutos para decodificación auditiva consiste en: 5 min de transcripción fonética activa, 5 min de cotejo e identificación de patrones, y 5 min de shadowing (repitiendo 3 veces).

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "I must go to the last station. (Challenge: The next-door neighbor said it must be last night.)"
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::I mus-go to the las-station. (Challenge: The nex-door neighbor said it mus-be las-night.)}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Shadowing: Cluster Elision
Cluster Elision (Rule of 3 Consonants): When 3 consonants meet, the middle /t/ or /d/ is dropped.
🎥 Search: 'English with Martin Blacutt regla de 3 consonantes elision' on YouTube.
🔗 YouGlish search terms: last night / next door
usage: Formal: I must go to the last station. (Challenge: The next-door neighbor said it must be last night.) → Connected: I mus-go to the las-station. (Challenge: The nex-door neighbor said it mus-be las-night.)
spanish: Spanish: Lento: I must go to the last station. Conectado: I mus-go to the las-station. Reto: El vecino de al lado dijo que debió ser anoche.

scenario: Pronunciation Drill: Shadowing: Flap T / Flap D 🗣️
text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Put it in a bottle of water while riding the ladder. (Challenge: What a better city to get a job.)"
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Pu-di-tin a bo-dle of wa-der while ri-ding the la-der. (Challenge: What a be-rer ci-dy to ge-da job.)}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Shadowing: Flap T / Flap D
Flap T & D (Intervocalic Flapping): Intervocalic /t/ and /d/ turn into soft flap [ɾ].
🎥 Search: 'Rachel's English - How to pronounce Flap T' on YouTube.
🔗 YouGlish search terms: put it on / better water
usage: Formal: Put it in a bottle of water while riding the ladder. (Challenge: What a better city to get a job.) → Connected: Pu-di-tin a bo-dle of wa-der while ri-ding the la-der. (Challenge: What a be-rer ci-dy to ge-da job.)
spanish: Spanish: Lento: Ponlo en una botella de agua mientras montas la escalera. Reto: Qué mejor ciudad para conseguir un trabajo.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Don't you know what would you do if I asked you? (Challenge: Did you see what caught you off guard?)"
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Don-choo know what wood-joo do if I ask-shoo? (Challenge: Did-joo see what caught-choo off guard?)}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Shadowing: Yod Coalescence
Yod Coalescence: Merging /t, d, s, z/ with palatal /j/ creates CH, DJ, SH, ZH.
🎥 Search: 'BBC Learning English - Tim's Pronunciation Workshop: Assimilation' on YouTube.
🔗 YouGlish search terms: don't you / would you
usage: Formal: Don't you know what would you do if I asked you? (Challenge: Did you see what caught you off guard?) → Connected: Don-choo know what wood-joo do if I ask-shoo? (Challenge: Did-joo see what caught-choo off guard?)
spanish: Spanish: Lento: ¿No sabes qué harías si te lo pidiera? Reto: ¿Viste qué te tomó por sorpresa?

scenario: Pronunciation Drill: Shadowing: Dark L Vocalization 🗣️
text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "I feel that the milk in the bottle is cold."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::I feew that the miwk in the boddle is cowd.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Shadowing: Dark L Vocalization
Dark L Vocalization: The back-of-the-mouth L [ɫ] vocalizes to [w] or [ʊ] before consonants or final pauses.
🎥 Search: 'Rachel's English Dark L sound' on YouTube.
🔗 YouGlish search terms: feel cold / milk bottle
usage: Formal: I feel that the milk in the bottle is cold. → Connected: I feew that the miwk in the boddle is cowd.
spanish: Spanish: Lento: Siento que la leche en la botella está fría. Conectado: I feew that the miwk in the boddle is cowd.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Go out and do it because I am ready."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Go-w-out and do-w-it because I-y-am ready.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Shadowing: Intrusive Glides
Intrusive Glides: Inserting linking /w/ or /j/ ('y') to smooth out adjacent vowel clashes.
🎥 Search: 'Linking vowels with W and Y sound English' on YouTube.
🔗 YouGlish search terms: go out / do it
usage: Formal: Go out and do it because I am ready. → Connected: Go-w-out and do-w-it because I-y-am ready.
spanish: Spanish: Lento: Sal y hazlo porque estoy listo. Conectado: Go-w-out and do-w-it because I-y-am ready.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "What did you do? Did you ever think it would have been easy? (Challenge: What do you mean how's it going?)"
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Whadja do? Jever think it woulda bin easy? (Challenge: Whadaya mean howzit goin'?)}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Shadowing: Auxiliary Chunks
Complex Auxiliary Contractions: Compression of auxiliary groups and questions.
🎥 Search: 'English reductions whadja jever whadaya' on YouTube.
🔗 YouGlish search terms: what did you do / would have been
usage: Formal: What did you do? Did you ever think it would have been easy? (Challenge: What do you mean how's it going?) → Connected: Whadja do? Jever think it woulda bin easy? (Challenge: Whadaya mean howzit goin'?)
spanish: Spanish: Lento: ¿Qué hiciste? ¿Pensaste que habría sido fácil? Reto: ¿A qué te refieres con cómo te va?

text: In connected speech, a sequence like {{c1::next door}} often becomes a softer, faster cluster that sounds like {{c2::nex door}}.
explanation: When three consonants appear close together, native speakers often reduce the middle consonant, especially in casual speech.
usage:
Pattern: cluster reduction / consonant elision
  • Last night sounds like las-night.
  • Next door sounds like nex-door.
spanish: En el habla conectada, una secuencia como 'next door' suele sonar como una agrupación más suave y rápida, casi como 'nex door'.

text: In American English, a {{c1::t}} between vowels may sound like a quick {{c2::flap}} rather than a clear stop.
explanation: This is common in words like water, better, or city, where the sound becomes a light tap in the mouth.
usage:
Pattern: flapping / alveolar flap
  • water → wa-ter
  • better → be-er
spanish: En inglés americano, una 't' entre vocales puede sonar como un golpe breve en lugar de un bloqueo claro.

text: In some varieties of English, a {{c1::t}} before a nasal or at the end of a syllable can become a {{c2::glottal stop}}.
explanation: This creates a small catch in the throat and is very common in everyday speech.
usage:
Pattern: glottal stop / t-glottalization
  • button → bu'n
  • can't go → ca'n go
spanish: En algunas variedades de inglés, una 't' antes de una nasal o al final de una sílaba puede convertirse en una parada glotal.

text: Function words often reduce to a weak vowel called {{c1::schwa}} in fast speech.
explanation: Words like to, of, and, or can become very short and unstressed, making the sentence sound smoother.
usage:
Pattern: schwa / reduced vowel
  • I can go → I kən go
  • fish and chips → fish n chips
spanish: Las palabras funcionales a menudo se reducen a una vocal débil llamada schwa en el habla rápida.

text: In non-rhotic accents, speakers may add an {{c1::intrusive r}} between vowels to make the phrase smoother.
explanation: This is common in phrases like law and order or idea of it, where a subtle r bridges two vowel sounds.
usage:
Pattern: linking r / intrusive r
  • law and order → law-r-and-order
  • idea of it → idea-r-of-it
spanish: En acentos no róticos, los hablantes pueden agregar una r intrusiva entre vocales para que la frase suene más fluida.

explanation:
/ɪ/ — short, relaxed, mid-high front vowel
/iː/ — long, tense, high front vowel

🧠 Articulatory Tip (ES): Para /ɪ/, relaja la boca y la lengua completamente. Es corta. Para /iː/, sonríe exageradamente y tensa los lados de la lengua. Es más larga.

text:
🔊 /ʌ/ 🔊 /ɑː/ 📖 IPAchart
Phoneme A /ʌ/
IPA description: {{c1::short, open-mid back unrounded vowel}}
explanation:
/ʌ/ — short, open-mid back unrounded vowel
/ɑː/ — long, open back unrounded vowel

🧠 Articulatory Tip (ES): Para /ʌ/, haz un sonido neutro y corto como si te hubieran golpeado levemente en el estómago. Para /ɑː/, abre la boca completamente hacia abajo como cuando el doctor te examina la garganta.

🔊 nut/nʌt/not/nɑːt/
🔊 but/bʌt/bought/bɔːt/
🔊 suck/sʌk/sock/sɑːk/
usage:

text:

Phoneme A /æ/

explanation:
/æ/ — short, near-open front unrounded vowel
/ɛ/ — short, open-mid front unrounded vowel

🧠 Articulatory Tip (ES): Para /æ/, abre la boca de manera lateral (sonrisa exagerada) y empuja la lengua hacia adelante y abajo. Para /ɛ/, abre la boca de manera neutra (parecido a la 'e' de cama).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Tense vs. Lax}} is defined as: Tense vowels (e.g., /iː/, /uː/) are longer and more peripheral than lax vowels (e.g., /ɪ/, /ʊ/).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Tense vs. Lax
Description: Tense vowels (e.g., /iː/, /uː/) are longer and more peripheral than lax vowels (e.g., /ɪ/, /ʊ/).
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Tense vs. Lax' es: Tense vowels (e.g., /iː/, /uː/) are longer and more peripheral than lax vowels (e.g., /ɪ/, /ʊ/).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Schwa /ə/}} is defined as: The most frequent sound in English, occurring in almost all unstressed syllables.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: The Schwa /ə/
Description: The most frequent sound in English, occurring in almost all unstressed syllables.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'The Schwa /ə/' es: The most frequent sound in English, occurring in almost all unstressed syllables.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Rhotic Vowels}} is defined as: In GA, vowels are often "r-colored" (e.g., /ɚ/, /ɝ/).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Rhotic Vowels
Description: In GA, vowels are often "r-colored" (e.g., /ɚ/, /ɝ/).
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Rhotic Vowels' es: In GA, vowels are often "r-colored" (e.g., /ɚ/, /ɝ/).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Aspiration}} is defined as: Voiceless stops /p, t, k/ are aspirated ([pʰ, tʰ, kʰ]) when they occur at the beginning of a stressed syllable, unless preceded by /s/.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Aspiration
Description: Voiceless stops /p, t, k/ are aspirated ([pʰ, tʰ, kʰ]) when they occur at the beginning of a stressed syllable, unless preceded by /s/.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Aspiration' es: Voiceless stops /p, t, k/ are aspirated ([pʰ, tʰ, kʰ]) when they occur at the beginning of a stressed syllable, unless preceded by /s/.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Rhoticity}} is defined as: - **Rhotic (GA)**: /r/ is pronounced in all positions.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Rhoticity
Description: - **Rhotic (GA)**: /r/ is pronounced in all positions.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Rhoticity' es: - **Rhotic (GA)**: /r/ is pronounced in all positions.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Non-rhotic (RP)}} is defined as: /r/ is only pronounced before a vowel.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Non-rhotic (RP)
Description: /r/ is only pronounced before a vowel.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Non-rhotic (RP)' es: /r/ is only pronounced before a vowel.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::L-Vocalization}} is defined as: /l/ has two main allophones: "Clear L" [l] before vowels and "Dark L" [ɫ] in coda positions.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: L-Vocalization
Description: /l/ has two main allophones: "Clear L" [l] before vowels and "Dark L" [ɫ] in coda positions.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'L-Vocalization' es: /l/ has two main allophones: "Clear L" [l] before vowels and "Dark L" [ɫ] in coda positions.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Flapping}} is defined as: In GA, /t/ and /d/ become a voiced flap [ɾ] between vowels when the second vowel is unstressed (e.g., *water*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Flapping
Description: In GA, /t/ and /d/ become a voiced flap [ɾ] between vowels when the second vowel is unstressed (e.g., *water*).
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Flapping' es: In GA, /t/ and /d/ become a voiced flap [ɾ] between vowels when the second vowel is unstressed (e.g., *water*).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Elision (Omission)}} is defined as: Deletion of sounds in fast speech.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Elision (Omission)
Description: Deletion of sounds in fast speech.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Elision (Omission)' es: Deletion of sounds in fast speech.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::T-Omission}} is defined as: /t/ is often dropped after /n/ in fast speech (e.g., *interview* -> "innerview" [ˈɪnərvjuː], *wanted* -> "wanned" [ˈwɑnɪd]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: T-Omission
Description: /t/ is often dropped after /n/ in fast speech (e.g., *interview* -> "innerview" [ˈɪnərvjuː], *wanted* -> "wanned" [ˈwɑnɪd]).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::D-Omission}} is defined as: /d/ is often dropped in consonant clusters (e.g., *you and me* -> "you an' me" [juː ən miː], *stand there* -> "stan' there").
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: D-Omission
Description: /d/ is often dropped in consonant clusters (e.g., *you and me* -> "you an' me" [juː ən miː], *stand there* -> "stan' there").

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::H-Dropping}} is defined as: The initial /h/ of unstressed pronouns (*he, him, her, his*) is often dropped in normal conversation (e.g., *Tell him* -> "tell 'im" [tɛl ɪm], *What did he say?* -> "what did 'e say?" [wʌt dɪd i sɛj]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: H-Dropping

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::N-to-M / N-to-NG}} is defined as: e.g., *ten mice* -> /tɛm maɪs/, *one cup* -> /wʌŋ kʌp/.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Linking & Liaison}} is defined as: Fusing a final consonant with an initial vowel (e.g., *an apple* -> /əˈnæpəl/, *not at all* -> /ˌnɒtəˈtɔːl/).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::/w/ Intrusion}} is defined as: After close-back vowels /uː, oʊ, aʊ/ (e.g., *go out* -> /ɡoʊ ʷ aʊt/).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::/j/ Intrusion}} is defined as: After close-front vowels /iː, eɪ, aɪ/ (e.g., *pay all* -> /peɪ ʲ ɔːl/).
