text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Reduction}} is defined as: Unstressed grammatical/function words undergo extreme reduction to the schwa /ə/ or near-close vowels:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Vowel Reduction
Description: Unstressed grammatical/function words undergo extreme reduction to the schwa /ə/ or near-close vowels:
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Vowel Reduction' es: Unstressed grammatical/function words undergo extreme reduction to the schwa /ə/ or near-close vowels:

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Word Stress}} is defined as: English is a **stress-timed** language. Primary stress is phonemic (e.g., *RE-cord* (noun) vs. *re-CORD* (verb)).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Word Stress
Description: English is a **stress-timed** language. Primary stress is phonemic (e.g., *RE-cord* (noun) vs. *re-CORD* (verb)).
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Word Stress' es: English is a **stress-timed** language. Primary stress is phonemic (e.g., *RE-cord* (noun) vs. *re-CORD* (verb)).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Sentence Stress}} is defined as: Content words (nouns, verbs, adjectives) are usually stressed, while function words (prepositions, articles) are reduced.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Sentence Stress
Description: Content words (nouns, verbs, adjectives) are usually stressed, while function words (prepositions, articles) are reduced.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Sentence Stress' es: Content words (nouns, verbs, adjectives) are usually stressed, while function words (prepositions, articles) are reduced.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Intonation}} is defined as: Pitch rises for questions and falls for statements, though this varies by dialect.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Intonation
Description: Pitch rises for questions and falls for statements, though this varies by dialect.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Intonation' es: Pitch rises for questions and falls for statements, though this varies by dialect.

text: What is the English pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Connected Speech Pro-Tip}}? To sound natural, do not pronounce every word separately. Link final consonants to initial vowels, drop your 'h' sounds in unstressed pronouns like 'him' or 'her', and reduce small grammar words like 'can' and 'to' to a short, quick schwa 'ə'.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for English.
usage:
Tip: Connected Speech Pro-Tip
To sound natural, do not pronounce every word separately. Link final consonants to initial vowels, drop your 'h' sounds in unstressed pronouns like 'him' or 'her', and reduce small grammar words like 'can' and 'to' to a short, quick schwa 'ə'.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de English para 'Connected Speech Pro-Tip'? To sound natural, do not pronounce every word separately. Link final consonants to initial vowels, drop your 'h' sounds in unstressed pronouns like 'him' or 'her', and reduce small grammar words like 'can' and 'to' to a short, quick schwa 'ə'.

text: What is the English pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "Wanned" Effect}}? In American English, when 't' comes after 'n' and is followed by an unstressed vowel, it is almost always omitted in fast speech. Try saying "plen-ty", "twen-ty", and "in-ter-net" without the 't' sound!
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for English.
usage:
Tip: The "Wanned" Effect
In American English, when 't' comes after 'n' and is followed by an unstressed vowel, it is almost always omitted in fast speech. Try saying "plen-ty", "twen-ty", and "in-ter-net" without the 't' sound!
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de English para 'The "Wanned" Effect'? In American English, when 't' comes after 'n' and is followed by an unstressed vowel, it is almost always omitted in fast speech. Try saying "plen-ty", "twen-ty", and "in-ter-net" without the 't' sound!

text: What is the English pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Tip}}? Use the `ə` symbol often; it is the key to natural-sounding English rhythm.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for English.
usage:
Tip: Tip
Use the `ə` symbol often; it is the key to natural-sounding English rhythm.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de English para 'Tip'? Use the `ə` symbol often; it is the key to natural-sounding English rhythm.

scenario: Shadowing: Can I get a coffee? ☕
text: Can I get a {{c1::coffee}} to go, please?
explanation: This phrase is ideal for shadowing because it includes a common reduction and a natural rhythm. Notice the smooth linking in 'get a'.
usage:
Pattern: Can I get a ...?
  • Can I get a latte?
  • Can I get a table for two?
spanish: ¿Podría pedir un café para llevar, por favor?

scenario: Shadowing: What’s the special of the day? 🍽️
text: What’s the {{c1::special}} of the day?
explanation: The phrase is short, fast, and natural. It is excellent for practicing reduced vowels and connected speech.
usage:
Pattern: what’s the special of the day
  • What’s the special of the day?
  • What do you recommend?
spanish: ¿Cuál es el especial del día?

scenario: Shadowing: I’m just pulling your leg 😂
text: I’m just {{c1::pulling your leg}}.
explanation: This idiom is useful for shadowing because it sounds very natural in fast speech and includes a clear rhythm pattern.
usage:
Pattern: pulling your leg
  • Relax, I’m just pulling your leg.
  • He was pulling my leg.
spanish: Solo estoy bromeando contigo.

text: We’re {{c1::gonna}} be late.
explanation: Gonna is a core connected-speech pattern. It is ideal for practicing reduced forms and relaxed rhythm.
usage:
Pattern: gonna / wanna
  • I’m gonna call you later.
  • We’re gonna miss the train.
spanish: Vamos a llegar tarde.

scenario: Shadowing: I’m not sure what to think 🤷
text: I’m not really sure {{c1::what to think}} about it.
explanation: The phrase sounds very natural in informal speech and helps with weak forms and sentence stress.
usage:
Pattern: not sure what to think
  • I’m not sure what to think about that.
  • She wasn’t sure what to say.
spanish: No estoy realmente seguro de qué pensar al respecto.

scenario: Shadowing: I’ve got a lot on my plate 🍽️
text: I’ve got a lot on my {{c1::plate}} right now.
explanation: This phrase is very useful for practicing fluency and stress placement in idiomatic speech.
usage:
Pattern: have a lot on your plate
  • I’ve got a lot on my plate this week.
  • She’s got too much on her plate.
spanish: Tengo muchas cosas que atender en este momento.

scenario: Support Ops 🚨: Escalation Opening — Urgency Without Panic
text: To open an escalation call with authority but without panic, say: "{{c1::I'm escalating this because we've crossed the impact threshold where I need your support to resolve it quickly.}}"
explanation: This phrase does two things simultaneously: (1) it signals that escalation is the correct action (threshold crossed), not a sign of helplessness; (2) 'support to resolve quickly' frames the executive as an enabler, not a rescue. Avoid 'I don't know what to do' — it erodes confidence.
usage:
• "This is a P1 for us — I'm escalating because the issue is cross-team and I need your authority to expedite the vendor response."
  • "I wouldn't escalate if I hadn't tried everything at my level. We need your weight behind this to unblock the infrastructure team."
spanish: 'Lo escalo porque hemos cruzado el umbral de impacto donde necesito tu apoyo para resolverlo rápidamente.' — escalar es la acción correcta, no una señal de incapacidad.

scenario: Support Ops 🚨: Impact Statement in Escalation
text: Before escalating, quantify the impact with: "{{c1::This is affecting [N] customers / [$ revenue] / [SLA metric] since [time].}}"
explanation: Executives make priority decisions based on business impact, not technical severity. A 'database error' means nothing; 'affecting 3,400 enterprise users and $180K in at-risk renewals since 6 AM' makes priority clear. Always lead with who/what is affected, not with the technical cause.
usage:
• "This is affecting approximately 2,100 active users in the US-West region and has been ongoing since 3:47 PM. SLA breach window opens in 90 minutes."
  • "Impact: two enterprise accounts that represent $240K ARR are unable to access core functionality. We've had 14 contacts in the last 2 hours."
spanish: 'Esto está afectando a [N] clientes / [$X en ingresos] / [métrica SLA] desde [hora].' — los ejecutivos priorizan por impacto de negocio, no por severidad técnica.

scenario: Support Ops 🚨: What You've Already Tried
text: In an escalation, always state what you've ruled out with: "{{c1::We've already tried X, Y, and Z. None resolved it. The blocker is [specific issue].}}"
explanation: Executives and senior engineers will ask 'did you try X?' before helping. Pre-empting this question with a clear 'what we've done' list saves time and signals competence. It changes the conversation from 'let me fix your problem' to 'here's a clear picture — what do you know that I don't?'
usage:
• "We've already: restarted all pods, rolled back the last deployment, checked the database connections. Issue persists. The blocker is the upstream API that requires the vendor's ops team to cycle."
  • "I've tried three workarounds: cache bypass, direct DB query, and region failover. None worked. We need infrastructure admin access to proceed."
spanish: 'Ya intentamos X, Y y Z. Ninguno lo resolvió. El bloqueador es [problema específico].' — anticiparse a las preguntas sobre lo intentado demuestra competencia y acelera la resolución.

text: The gold standard for async escalation is the 3-line format: {{c1::Line 1: What broke. Line 2: Impact right now. Line 3: What I need from you.}}
explanation: When escalating via Slack, email, or paging, long messages get skimmed or missed. The 3-line format forces radical compression and ensures the reader knows immediately what action to take. This is the incident communication equivalent of BLUF. Every word after line 3 is optional context.
usage:
• Line 1: "Auth service returning 503s for all EU users since 08:22 UTC."
    Line 2: "~4,500 users can't log in. SLA breach in 38 minutes."
    Line 3: "Need @infra-team to check the load balancer config. Can you join the bridge call?"
spanish: El estándar dorado para escalar de forma asíncrona: Línea 1: Qué se rompió. Línea 2: Impacto ahora mismo. Línea 3: Qué necesito de ti. Compresión radical para máxima claridad.

scenario: Support Ops 🚨: Managing Customer During Escalation
text: While escalating internally, keep the customer stable with: "{{c1::I'm actively working on this with our team right now. I'll have an update for you in [X minutes/hours].}}"
explanation: The worst thing during an escalation is customer silence. 'Working on it' without a timeline creates anxiety. The time commitment — even if it's just a check-in update, not a resolution — gives the customer a predictable next touchpoint. Never say 'I don't know when this will be fixed.'
usage:
• "I've escalated this to our senior infrastructure team and I'm in a live call with them now. I'll have an update for you by 2:30 PM — that's 45 minutes from now."
  • "I'm not going to leave you hanging. Even if we don't have a fix, I'll update you every 30 minutes until we do."
spanish: 'Estoy trabajando activamente en esto con nuestro equipo ahora mismo. Tendré una actualización para ti en [X minutos/horas].' — nunca dejes al cliente en silencio durante una escalada.

text: After an escalation is resolved, close it with: "{{c1::A brief summary of what happened, what we did, and what we're putting in place to prevent it.}}"
explanation: The post-escalation debrief is often skipped in favor of moving on. This is a mistake. A structured close-out — cause, response, prevention — builds institutional trust, creates documentation for future incidents, and signals operational maturity. Keep it to 3-5 bullets, not a full report.
usage:
• "Quick close-out: root cause was a misconfigured load balancer rule pushed at 08:15. Resolution was a config rollback at 09:47. Prevention: adding automated config validation to the deployment pipeline by next Thursday."
  • "Full postmortem will follow, but the headline: we resolved it in 85 minutes, customer was kept informed throughout, and we've already opened 3 follow-up tickets for system hardening."
spanish: 'Un breve resumen de qué ocurrió, qué hicimos y qué ponemos en marcha para prevenirlo.' — el cierre post-escalada construye confianza institucional y documenta aprendizajes.

text: In support operations, escalation tiers map to: {{c1::L1 (first contact, standard playbook), L2 (technical deep-dive, specialist), L3 (engineering or vendor-level), and Executive (business impact decision needed)}}.
explanation: Understanding and naming escalation tiers correctly prevents both over-escalation (involving executives in L2 issues) and under-escalation (keeping L3 issues at L2 too long). Use tier language explicitly: 'I'm escalating to L3 because we've exhausted the L2 playbook' signals structured thinking to leadership.
usage:
• "This is an L3 escalation — we've been through the L2 runbook twice and need engineering to look at the source code."
  • "I'm keeping this at L2 for now — the specialist team has a lead. I'll escalate to executive if we don't have resolution in 2 hours."
spanish: Los niveles de escalada: L1 (contacto inicial, guión estándar), L2 (técnico especializado), L3 (ingeniería o proveedor), Ejecutivo (decisión de impacto de negocio). Nombrarlos correctamente previene la escalada excesiva.

scenario: Support Ops 🚨: Pushing Back on Inappropriate Escalation
text: When a customer escalates unnecessarily, de-escalate with: "{{c1::I understand this feels urgent. Let me assure you we're already treating this with the highest priority. Involving [name/team] at this stage would actually slow us down — here's why.}}"
explanation: Customers sometimes escalate to executives not because they need executive action, but because they feel unheard. This phrase validates the urgency feeling while making a logical case for why staying at the current level is faster. It redirects the focus from 'who's in charge' to 'what gets this solved fastest.'
usage:
• "I hear you — this is critical for your business. I want to be transparent: our CTO joining the call right now would take us 30 minutes to set up. My team has the exact expertise needed and we're live on this right now."
  • "Escalating to the VP at this point would actually reset the clock. Let's give my team 90 more minutes with the authority I already have."
spanish: 'Entiendo que esto se siente urgente. Déjame asegurarte que ya lo estamos tratando con la máxima prioridad. Involucrar a [nombre] en esta etapa realmente nos ralentizaría — aquí está el porqué.'

text: To open an incident bridge call efficiently, say: "{{c1::This is [name], incident commander. We have a [P1/P2] incident. Here's what we know: [2 sentences]. Our working hypothesis is [X]. I need [team A] on diagnosis and [team B] on customer communication. Let's go.}}"
explanation: Incident bridge calls without structure waste the first 10 minutes on 'who's on the call' and 'what do we know.' The incident commander role, explicit team assignments, and a working hypothesis cut that down to 60 seconds. 'Let's go' is a signal to begin parallel workstreams — not more discussion.
usage: • "This is Carlos, IC. P1 — auth service down for EU region since 08:22. 4,500 users affected. Working hypothesis: load balancer misconfiguration from the 08:15 deployment. Engineering: dig into the LB config. Support: draft customer comms now. Let's go."
spanish: 'Soy [nombre], incident commander. Tenemos un incidente [P1/P2]. Esto es lo que sabemos: [2 oraciones]. Hipótesis de trabajo: [X]. Necesito a [equipo A] en diagnóstico y a [equipo B] en comunicación al cliente. Vamos.' — apertura de bridge call sin pérdida de tiempo.

text: During an active incident, the standard update cadence is: {{c1::every 15-30 minutes for internal stakeholders, every 30-60 minutes for affected customers}}, with each update including: status, latest finding, next action.
explanation: Inconsistent updates during incidents create anxiety and trigger unnecessary escalations. Setting an explicit cadence — 'I'll update every 30 minutes until resolved' — prevents executives from interrupting the response team to check status. Each update must answer: where are we, what did we just learn, what happens next.
usage:
• "Update at 09:45: Still investigating. We've confirmed the issue is isolated to the authentication microservice. Next: Engineering is doing a config rollback. Next update: 10:15."
  • Template: "[Time] Update: Status [Open/Contained/Resolved]. Latest: [finding]. Next: [action]. ETA for next update: [time]."
spanish: Durante un incidente activo, la cadencia de actualizaciones estándar es: cada 15-30 min para stakeholders internos, cada 30-60 min para clientes afectados. Cada actualización: estado, último hallazgo, próxima acción.

text: A customer-facing incident statement must contain: {{c1::what is affected (not what broke technically), how many users, when it started, and an expected restoration time or next update time}}.
explanation: External incident communications are NOT technical troubleshooting notes. Customers don't need to know it's a 'LB misconfiguration' — they need to know 'EU login is unavailable and you can expect an update by 10:30 AM.' Oversharing technical details creates liability and confusion; undersharing creates panic and churn.
usage:
• Good: "We are currently experiencing an issue affecting login for users in the EU region. Our team is actively working to restore service. We expect to provide a next update by 10:30 AM UTC. We apologize for the inconvenience."
  • Avoid: "Our load balancer threw a 502 due to a bad deployment at 08:15." (Too technical, implies fault.)
spanish: Una declaración de incidente dirigida al cliente debe contener: qué está afectado (no la causa técnica), cuántos usuarios, cuándo comenzó y un tiempo de restauración esperado o próxima actualización. No compartir detalles técnicos en comunicaciones externas.

text: In incident management, P1 means {{c1::complete service outage or critical data loss with immediate customer impact}}, P2 means degraded service affecting many users, P3 means minor impact or workaround available.
explanation: Consistent severity language prevents 'everything is P1' fatigue. When severity is inflated, response teams can't prioritize. When it's deflated, executives are caught off guard. The ITIL/PagerDuty standard defines P1 as requiring immediate mobilization, 24/7 response, and executive notification. P2 requires a response within the business day.
usage:
• P1: "Payment processing is completely down. Zero transactions completing. Mobilizing full response team now."
  • P2: "API response times are 4x above baseline for 20% of users. Degraded but functional. Investigating."
  • P3: "One report module is returning stale data. Workaround available. Fix scheduled for next sprint."
spanish: P1: interrupción completa del servicio o pérdida crítica de datos con impacto inmediato al cliente. P2: servicio degradado que afecta a muchos usuarios. P3: impacto menor o hay solución alternativa. La coherencia en el lenguaje de severidad previene la fatiga de 'todo es P1'.

text: In a war room or bridge call, apply the rule: {{c1::speak only when you have a confirmed finding, a decision to make, or an action to assign — not to think out loud}}.
explanation: War room noise is one of the biggest time wasters during P1 incidents. When 10 people are speculating simultaneously, the signal-to-noise ratio collapses. The incident commander's job is to enforce structured communication: 'Do you have a finding or are you still investigating?' Thinking out loud belongs in side channels (Slack thread), not the main bridge.
usage:
• IC: "Engineering — do you have something confirmed?" Eng: "Yes. The LB config shows rule #14 was modified at 08:13." IC: "Good. Can you revert it? How long?" Eng: "10 minutes." IC: "Go. I'll update stakeholders."
  • Avoid: Open speculation on the bridge call. Move hypotheses to a side Slack thread.
spanish: En un war room o bridge call: habla solo cuando tengas un hallazgo confirmado, una decisión que tomar o una acción que asignar — no para pensar en voz alta. El IC debe imponer comunicación estructurada.

text: When declaring an incident resolved, use: "{{c1::As of [time], the incident is resolved. Service is fully restored for all users. Root cause: [brief]. Full postmortem within [timeframe].}}"
explanation: A clean resolution announcement closes the loop for all stakeholders simultaneously. The four elements — time, scope of restoration, brief root cause, postmortem commitment — give customers confidence that the issue is truly over and that the organization is learning from it. Never declare resolved without confirming with monitoring data.
usage:
• "As of 10:52 AM UTC, the authentication service is fully restored for all EU users. Root cause: a load balancer config change at 08:13 was reverted. We'll publish a full postmortem within 5 business days."
  • Internal: "Incident closed. Full details in the incident ticket #INC-2341. Postmortem call scheduled for Thursday."
spanish: 'A partir de [hora], el incidente está resuelto. El servicio está completamente restaurado para todos los usuarios. Causa raíz: [breve]. Postmortem completo en [plazo].' — cierra el ciclo para todos los stakeholders.

text: In incident retrospectives, avoid blame language. Replace 'John forgot to...' with {{c1::'The process didn't have a safeguard for...' or 'The system allowed X to happen without a check.'}}
explanation: Blameless postmortems (pioneered by Google SRE and Etsy) identify systemic failures, not individual failures. When a human makes a mistake, the blameless question is: 'What would have prevented this system from allowing that mistake?' Blame kills psychological safety, which reduces future incident reporting — making the system more fragile.
usage:
• Blame: "John deployed without testing."
  • Blameless: "The deployment pipeline didn't require a mandatory test gate before production push."
  • Blame: "Sarah missed the alert."
  • Blameless: "The alerting threshold was set too high, resulting in a delayed notification."
spanish: En las retrospectivas de incidentes, evita el lenguaje de culpa. Reemplaza 'John olvidó...' con 'El proceso no tenía una salvaguarda para...' Los postmortems sin culpa identifican fallas sistémicas, no individuales.

text: When paging a stakeholder mid-incident, the message must be under {{c1::5 lines}}, contain the severity level, impact, and one explicit ask.
explanation: Executives paged during incidents often receive walls of text or vague 'call me' messages. The sub-5-line constraint forces prioritization. The explicit ask ('can you join the bridge?' / 'I need your sign-off to engage vendor support') removes ambiguity. The recipient should know in 10 seconds whether to drop what they're doing.
usage:
• "@CTO — P1 ACTIVE. Auth service down for 4,500 EU users since 08:22. SLA breach in 45 min. I need your authorization to engage [Vendor] premium support. Can you reply Y/N?"
  • "[Name] — P2 incident on payment processing. Degraded, not down. No exec action needed yet. I'll update you at 11:00."
spanish: Al enviar un mensaje de alerta a un ejecutivo durante un incidente: máximo 5 líneas, incluye el nivel de severidad, el impacto y una solicitud explícita. El receptor debe saber en 10 segundos si necesita actuar.

text: The 5 Whys technique involves asking '{{c1::Why?}}' repeatedly until you reach the root cause, typically 3-5 levels deep — not just the proximate cause.
explanation: The 5 Whys was developed by Taiichi Ohno at Toyota. The power is stopping at the systemic root, not the first obvious answer. Example: Why did the service fail? → Config was wrong. Why was config wrong? → Deploy script had a bug. Why did that bug reach prod? → Code review skipped due to time pressure. Why? → Deadline pressure from leadership. Root cause: organizational incentive structure.
usage: • Why #1: "The deployment failed." → Why #2: "The config file had incorrect values." → Why #3: "The template was outdated." → Why #4: "We had no automated config validation." → Why #5: "Config validation wasn't part of our CI/CD pipeline." Root cause: missing CI gate.
spanish: La técnica de los 5 Por qués consiste en preguntar '¿Por qué?' repetidamente hasta llegar a la causa raíz, típicamente 3-5 niveles de profundidad — no solo la causa próxima. Desarrollada por Toyota.

scenario: RCA Language 🔍: Proximate vs. Root Cause
text: The proximate cause is {{c1::the immediate trigger of the incident}}, while the root cause is {{c1::the underlying systemic condition that made the trigger possible}}.
explanation: Fixing only the proximate cause is like treating symptoms, not disease. Example: Proximate — a developer pushed bad code to production. Root cause — there was no automated test gate blocking bad code from reaching prod. Fixing the proximate (fire the developer) doesn't fix the systemic gap; fixing the root (add the CI gate) prevents recurrence for all developers.
usage:
• Proximate: "The developer deployed on a Friday afternoon without testing."
  • Root cause: "The deployment policy didn't block Friday afternoon pushes and the CI pipeline lacked mandatory smoke tests."
  • Always ask: "If a different person did the same thing, would this happen again?" If yes, you haven't reached the root cause.
spanish: La causa próxima es el desencadenante inmediato del incidente. La causa raíz es la condición sistémica subyacente que hizo posible el desencadenante. Arreglar solo la causa próxima es tratar síntomas, no la enfermedad.

text: A standard postmortem document contains: {{c1::Timeline, Impact, Root Cause, Contributing Factors, Action Items with owners and dates, and Lessons Learned}}.
explanation: Google SRE defines postmortem as a written record that ensures an incident is documented, its root cause is understood, and preventive actions are implemented. The timeline is factual (no blame). Contributing factors are conditions that made the root cause more likely. Action items must be SMART: specific, measurable, assigned, realistic, time-bound.
usage:
• Template sections:
    1. Summary (2-3 sentences)
    2. Timeline (bullet points, UTC timestamps)
    3. Impact (users, duration, revenue)
    4. Root cause
    5. Contributing factors
    6. Action items (who + what + by when)
    7. Lessons learned
spanish: Un postmortem estándar contiene: Cronología, Impacto, Causa Raíz, Factores Contribuyentes, Elementos de Acción con dueños y fechas, y Lecciones Aprendidas. Según el estándar Google SRE.

scenario: RCA Language 🔍: Action Item Language in RCAs
text: Weak RCA action items say 'improve monitoring'. Strong ones say: "{{c1::Add a PagerDuty alert for error rate > 5% on the auth service, owned by [name], by [specific date].}}"
explanation: Vague action items in postmortems never get done. 'Improve monitoring', 'strengthen processes', 'better communication' — these are aspirations, not actions. Every action item needs: a specific change, an owner (one person), and a deadline. The test: can you verify in 30 seconds whether this was done?
usage:
• Weak: "We'll improve our deployment process."
  • Strong: "Add a mandatory pre-prod smoke test to the CI pipeline, owned by DevOps lead, completed by 2026-07-28."
  • Weak: "Better alerting."
  • Strong: "Configure PagerDuty p50 latency alert at 500ms threshold for payment-api, owned by SRE team, by July 21."
spanish: Elementos de acción débiles dicen 'mejorar el monitoreo'. Los fuertes dicen: 'Agregar una alerta de PagerDuty para tasa de error > 5% en el servicio de auth, a cargo de [nombre], antes del [fecha específica].' Cada acción necesita: un cambio específico, un dueño y una fecha límite.

scenario: RCA Language 🔍: Contributing Factors vs. Root Cause
text: Contributing factors are {{c1::conditions that increased the likelihood or severity of an incident without being the direct cause}} — for example, time pressure, unclear ownership, or lack of documentation.
explanation: Modern incident analysis (following Sidney Dekker's Safety Differently approach) recognizes that most incidents have multiple contributing factors alongside a root cause. Identifying contributing factors helps address the organizational conditions that make incidents more likely, not just the specific technical failure.
usage:
• Root cause: "No rate limiting on the API endpoint."
  • Contributing factors: "(1) The team was under deadline pressure and skipped the security review. (2) There was no checklist item for rate limiting in the deploy process. (3) Oncall rotation had a gap that delayed initial detection."
spanish: Los factores contribuyentes son condiciones que aumentaron la probabilidad o gravedad de un incidente sin ser la causa directa — por ejemplo, presión de tiempo, propiedad poco clara o falta de documentación.

text: When presenting RCA findings to customers or leadership, use: "{{c1::We identified the root cause as X. We've taken Y action to prevent recurrence, and here's what we're monitoring to confirm effectiveness.}}"
explanation: This three-part customer RCA communication (cause → fix → validation) gives stakeholders the three things they most need: understanding, confidence, and assurance. Skipping the validation part ('what we're watching') leaves customers wondering if the fix will actually hold.
usage: • "We identified that the root cause was an unvalidated configuration change in our load balancer rules. We've reverted the change, added a mandatory validation step to our deployment pipeline, and we're monitoring error rates on a 5-minute interval for the next 72 hours."
spanish: 'Identificamos la causa raíz como X. Hemos tomado la acción Y para prevenir la recurrencia, y esto es lo que estamos monitoreando para confirmar la efectividad.' — comunicación de RCA al cliente: causa + corrección + validación.

text: In French, how do you say 'Hello / Good day'? {{c1::Bonjour}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Bonjour | Meaning: Hello / Good day
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hello / Good day' en French? Bonjour

text: In French, how do you say 'Good evening'? {{c1::Bonsoir}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Bonsoir | Meaning: Good evening
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good evening' en French? Bonsoir

text: In French, how do you say 'Hi / Bye - Informal'? {{c1::Salut}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Salut | Meaning: Hi / Bye - Informal
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hi / Bye - Informal' en French? Salut

text: In French, how do you say 'How are you? - Formal'? {{c1::Comment allez-vous ?}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Comment allez-vous ? | Meaning: How are you? - Formal
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'How are you? - Formal' en French? Comment allez-vous ?

text: In French, how do you say 'Goodbye'? {{c1::Au revoir}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Au revoir | Meaning: Goodbye
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Goodbye' en French? Au revoir

text: In French, how do you say 'Nice to meet you'? {{c1::Enchanté}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Enchanté | Meaning: Nice to meet you
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Nice to meet you' en French? Enchanté

text: In French, how do you say 'See you soon'? {{c1::À bientôt}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: À bientôt | Meaning: See you soon
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'See you soon' en French? À bientôt

text: When entering a French bakery at 7:00 PM, you should greet the staff with '{{c1::Bonsoir}}' instead of 'Bonjour'.
explanation: Bonjour is used during the day (Hello/Good morning). Bonsoir is used after 6 PM (Good evening). Saying 'Bonjour' late in the evening sounds unnatural in France.
usage:
Time guidelines:
  • Bonjour (used from morning until approximately 6 PM)
  • Bonsoir (used after 6 PM)
spanish: Al entrar a una panadería francesa a las 7:00 PM, debes saludar al personal con 'Bonsoir' en lugar de 'Bonjour'.

text: To ask a stranger or colleague 'How are you?' formally in French, you say: '{{c1::Comment allez-vous ?}}'
explanation: vous is the formal pronoun of respect. For friends or children, you use the informal 'Ça va ?' or 'Comment tu vas ?'.
usage:
Pronunciation tip: Pronounce the 't' in 'Comment' linked with 'allez' as a 't' sound: Comment allez-vous ? (/kɔ.mɑ̃.t‿a.le.vu/).
  • Formal: Bonjour Madame, comment allez-vous ?
  • Informal: Salut Marc, ça va ?
spanish: Para preguntar formalmente a un extraño o colega '¿Cómo está usted?' en francés, dices: 'Comment allez-vous ?'.

text: In French, the informal word '{{c1::Salut}}' can be used to say both 'Hi' and 'Bye'.
explanation: Salut is a highly versatile informal greeting. It should only be used with friends, family, or people you know well.
usage:
Usage scenarios:
  • Arriving: Salut, ça va ? (Hi, how's it going?)
  • Leaving: Allez, salut ! (Okay, bye!)
spanish: En francés, la palabra informal 'Salut' se puede usar tanto para decir 'Hola' como 'Adiós'.

text: In French, how do you say 'Welcome'? {{c1::Bienvenue}}
explanation: Used to welcome someone to a place. If saying 'welcome' to do something, it is followed by 'de' or 'à'.
usage: Term: Bienvenue | Example: Bienvenue en France ! (Welcome to France!)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Welcome' en francés? Bienvenue

text: In French, how do you say 'See you tomorrow'? {{c1::À demain}}
explanation: Literally 'until tomorrow'. It is a common parting phrase among colleagues and friends.
usage: Term: À demain | Example: Bonne soirée, à demain ! (Good evening, see you tomorrow!)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'See you tomorrow' en francés? À demain

text: In French, how do you say 'Good night' (when going to bed)? {{c1::Bonne nuit}}
explanation: Unlike 'Bonsoir' (good evening), 'Bonne nuit' is specifically used right before sleeping or when someone is going to bed.
usage: Term: Bonne nuit | Example: Bonne nuit, fais de beaux rêves. (Good night, sweet dreams.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good night' en francés? Bonne nuit

text: In French, the formal/polite way to say 'Please' is '{{c1::S'il vous plaît}}', and the informal way is '{{c2::S'il te plaît}}'.
explanation: Literally translates to 'if it pleases you'. Match 'vous' or 'tu' depending on the relationship.
usage: Formal: Un café, s'il vous plaît. | Informal: Passe-moi le sel, s'il te plaît.
spanish: En francés, la forma formal de decir 'Por favor' es 'S'il vous plaît' y la informal es 'S'il te plaît'.

text: In French, how do you say 'Thank you very much'? {{c1::Merci beaucoup}}
explanation: The most common way to show high gratitude. Be careful with pronunciation: 'beaucoup' ends with a silent 'p'.
usage: Term: Merci beaucoup | Example: Merci beaucoup pour votre aide. (Thank you very much for your help.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Thank you very much' en francés? Merci beaucoup

text: In French, a standard informal way to say 'You're welcome' is '{{c1::De rien}}'.
explanation: Literally means 'of nothing'. More formal versions include 'Je vous en prie' or 'Je t'en prie'.
usage: Term: De rien | Dialogue: - Merci ! - De rien.
spanish: En francés, una forma estándar e informal de decir 'De nada' es 'De rien'.

text: In French, the informal question to ask someone their name is '{{c1::Comment t'appelles-tu ?}}' or 'Comment tu t'appelles ?'.
explanation: Literally 'How do you call yourself?'. The formal equivalent is 'Comment vous appelez-vous ?'.
usage: Informal: Salut ! Comment tu t'appelles ? | Formal: Bonjour, comment vous appelez-vous ?
spanish: En francés, la pregunta informal para preguntar el nombre a alguien es 'Comment t'appelles-tu ?'.

text: In French, how do you say 'My name is [Name]'? {{c1::Je m'appelle}} [Name]
explanation: Using the pronominal verb 's'appeler' (to be called). Literally 'I call myself'.
usage: Term: Je m'appelle | Example: Je m'appelle Jesus.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Me llamo...' en francés? Je m'appelle

text: In French, how do you say 'Have a nice day'? {{c1::Bonne journée}}
explanation: Used when leaving or saying goodbye during the day to wish someone a good day ahead.
usage: Term: Bonne journée | Example: Au revoir, bonne journée ! (Goodbye, have a nice day!)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Have a nice day' en francés? Bonne journée

text: In French, how do you say 'Have a nice evening'? {{c1::Bonne soirée}}
explanation: Used when departing in the evening to wish someone a pleasant rest of the evening.
usage: Term: Bonne soirée | Example: Bonsoir, bonne soirée à tous. (Good evening, have a nice evening everyone.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Have a nice evening' en francés? Bonne soirée

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, how do you say 'I am'? {{c1::Je suis}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Je suis | Meaning: I am
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'I am' en French? Je suis

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, how do you say 'You are'? {{c1::Tu es}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Tu es | Meaning: You are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'You are' en French? Tu es

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, how do you say 'He/She is'? {{c1::Il/Elle est}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Il/Elle est | Meaning: He/She is
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'He/She is' en French? Il/Elle est

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, how do you say 'We are'? {{c1::Nous sommes}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Nous sommes | Meaning: We are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'We are' en French? Nous sommes

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, how do you say 'You are (formal/plural)'? {{c1::Vous êtes}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Vous êtes | Meaning: You are (formal/plural)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'You are (formal/plural)' en French? Vous êtes

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, how do you say 'They are'? {{c1::Ils/Elles sont}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Ils/Elles sont | Meaning: They are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'They are' en French? Ils/Elles sont

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, what is the third-person singular pronoun often used in spoken language to mean 'we' (informal)? {{c1::On}}
explanation: Though grammatically singular and conjugated like 'il/elle' (e.g., 'on est'), 'on' is the standard way to say 'we' in conversational French.
usage: Term: On | Example: On est fatigués. (We are tired.)
spanish: ¿Cuál es el pronombre en francés de tercera persona singular usado coloquialmente para decir 'nosotros'? On

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, what is the infinitive form of the verb 'to be'? {{c1::Être}}
explanation: One of the two most important auxiliary verbs in French (the other is 'avoir').
usage: Term: Être | Example: Aimer être libre. (To love to be free.)
spanish: ¿Cuál es el infinitivo del verbo 'ser/estar' en francés? Être

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: To negate the verb 'être' in French, you wrap the verb with {{c1::ne ... pas}} (or {{c1::n' ... pas}} before a vowel). Example: I am not = {{c2::Je ne suis pas}}.
explanation: Standard negation in French uses two particles around the conjugated verb.
usage: Example: Il n'est pas français. (He is not French.)
spanish: Para negar un verbo en francés, se coloca el verbo entre 'ne' y 'pas'. 'Je ne suis pas' = Yo no soy/estoy.

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, the stressed (disjunctive) pronoun for 'me' (used after prepositions or for emphasis) is {{c1::moi}}, and for 'you' (informal) is {{c2::toi}}.
explanation: Stressed pronouns are used after prepositions like 'avec' (with) or 'pour' (for), and in phrases like 'Et toi ?' (And you?).
usage: Examples: C'est pour moi ? (Is it for me?) | Je viens avec toi. (I am coming with you.)
spanish: En francés, el pronombre tónico para 'mí' es 'moi' y para 'ti' es 'toi'.

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, the stressed pronoun for 'him' is {{c1::lui}} and for 'her' is {{c2::elle}}.
explanation: Stressed pronouns are used in compound subjects, after prepositions, or for comparison.
usage: Examples: Chez lui (At his house) | Avec elle (With her)
spanish: En francés, el pronombre tónico para 'él' es 'lui' y para 'ella' es 'elle'.

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, the stressed pronoun for 'us' is {{c1::nous}} and for 'you' (plural/formal) is {{c2::vous}}.
explanation: These are identical to the subject pronouns in form but serve as stressed pronouns in different contexts.
usage: Examples: Venez chez nous ! (Come to our place!) | C'est à vous. (It's yours / up to you.)
spanish: En francés, el pronombre tónico para 'nosotros' es 'nous' y para 'ustedes/vosotros' es 'vous'.

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, the stressed pronoun for 'them' (masculine/mixed) is {{c1::eux}} and for 'them' (feminine) is {{c2::elles}}.
explanation: Make sure to use 'eux' for masculine plural instead of 'ils' after prepositions.
usage: Examples: Sans eux (Without them - masc) | Pour elles (For them - fem)
spanish: En francés, el pronombre tónico para 'ellos' es 'eux' y para 'ellas' es 'elles'.

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, adjectives following the verb 'être' must agree in gender. For example, if 'He is happy' is 'Il est heureux', 'She is happy' is '{{c1::Elle est heureuse}}'.
explanation: Most adjectives add an '-e' for feminine, but '-eux' changes to '-euse'.
usage: Rule: Adjective gender agreement. Example: Elle est heureuse.
spanish: En francés, los adjetivos que siguen a 'être' deben concordar en género. 'Il est heureux' (Él está feliz) -> 'Elle est heureuse' (Ella está feliz).

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, adjectives following the verb 'être' must agree in number. For example, if 'He is tall' is 'Il est grand', 'They (masc) are tall' is '{{c1::Ils sont grands}}'.
explanation: Plural adjectives usually add a silent '-s' at the end.
usage: Rule: Adjective plural agreement. Example: Elles sont grandes. (They are tall - fem)
spanish: En francés, los adjetivos que siguen a 'être' deben concordar en número. 'Il est grand' -> 'Ils sont grands' (se añade una 's' muda).

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: To ask a question using 'être' in French formally, you invert the subject and verb with a hyphen: 'Are you sure (formal)?' is '{{c1::Êtes-vous sûr ?}}'.
explanation: Subject-verb inversion is the most formal way to construct questions in French.
usage: Formal question: Est-il là ? (Is he here?) | Es-tu prêt ? (Are you ready? - informal)
spanish: Para hacer una pregunta formal con 'être' en francés, se invierte el sujeto y el verbo con un guion. 'Êtes-vous sûr ?' = ¿Está seguro?

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, how do you ask 'Is it ...?' or 'Are they ...?' (often used before nouns)? {{c1::Est-ce que}} or {{c1::C'est}}.
explanation: 'C'est' means 'It is'. 'Est-ce que' is a question formula meaning literally 'Is it that...?'
usage: Example: C'est un livre. (It's a book.) | Est-ce que tu es fatigué ? (Are you tired?)
spanish: ¿Cómo se pregunta '¿Es...?' o '¿Es que...?' en francés? 'Est-ce que' (para preguntas en general) o 'C'est' (esto es).

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, how do you say 'These are / They are' (plural of 'C'est')? {{c1::Ce sont}}
explanation: 'Ce sont' is used to identify plural people or things.
usage: Term: Ce sont | Example: Ce sont mes amis. (These are my friends.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Estos son / Ellos son' en francés? Ce sont

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, what is the demonstrative pronoun used to say 'this/that' or 'it' in a neutral way? {{c1::Ça}} or {{c1::Cela}}.
explanation: 'Ça' is highly conversational and informal; 'cela' is its formal equivalent.
usage: Term: Ça | Example: Ça va ? (How's it going? / Is that okay?) | C'est ça. (That's it.)
spanish: ¿Cuál es el pronombre demostrativo neutro en francés para decir 'eso / esto'? Ça (coloquial) o Cela (formal).

scenario: French A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In French, how do you say 'Who is it?' or 'Who is that?'? {{c1::Qui est-ce ?}} or {{c1::Qui c'est ?}}.
explanation: 'Qui est-ce ?' is formal; 'Qui c'est ?' or 'C'est qui ?' is very common in spoken informal French.
usage: Dialogue: - Toc toc ! - Qui est-ce ? (- Knock knock! - Who is it?)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Quién es?' en francés? 'Qui est-ce ?' o 'Qui c'est ?'.

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Nasalization}} is defined as: A defining feature where air passes through the nose and mouth simultaneously.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: Nasalization
Description: A defining feature where air passes through the nose and mouth simultaneously.
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Nasalization' es: A defining feature where air passes through the nose and mouth simultaneously.

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Rounded Front Vowels}} is defined as: Vowels like /y/ (*tu*) and /ø/ (*deux*) are produced by rounding the lips while the tongue is in the front of the mouth.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: Rounded Front Vowels
Description: Vowels like /y/ (*tu*) and /ø/ (*deux*) are produced by rounding the lips while the tongue is in the front of the mouth.
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Rounded Front Vowels' es: Vowels like /y/ (*tu*) and /ø/ (*deux*) are produced by rounding the lips while the tongue is in the front of the mouth.

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Semi-vowels}} is defined as: French uses three distinct glides: /j/ (*fille*), /w/ (*oui*), and the labio-palatal /ɥ/ (*nuit*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: Semi-vowels
Description: French uses three distinct glides: /j/ (*fille*), /w/ (*oui*), and the labio-palatal /ɥ/ (*nuit*).
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Semi-vowels' es: French uses three distinct glides: /j/ (*fille*), /w/ (*oui*), and the labio-palatal /ɥ/ (*nuit*).

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Uvular /ʁ/}} is defined as: The standard French 'r' is a voiced uvular fricative or approximant.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: The Uvular /ʁ/
Description: The standard French 'r' is a voiced uvular fricative or approximant.
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'The Uvular /ʁ/' es: The standard French 'r' is a voiced uvular fricative or approximant.

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Palatal Nasal /ɲ/}} is defined as: Found in words like *montagne*.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: Palatal Nasal /ɲ/
Description: Found in words like *montagne*.
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Palatal Nasal /ɲ/' es: Found in words like *montagne*.

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Lack of Aspiration}} is defined as: Unlike English, French voiceless stops (/p, t, k/) are never aspirated.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: Lack of Aspiration
Description: Unlike English, French voiceless stops (/p, t, k/) are never aspirated.
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Lack of Aspiration' es: Unlike English, French voiceless stops (/p, t, k/) are never aspirated.

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Liaison}} is defined as: Pronouncing a normally silent final consonant before a word starting with a vowel or mute H (e.g., *les amis* -> [le.z‿a.mi], *c'est un* -> [sɛ.t‿œ̃]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: Liaison
Description: Pronouncing a normally silent final consonant before a word starting with a vowel or mute H (e.g., *les amis* -> [le.z‿a.mi], *c'est un* -> [sɛ.t‿œ̃]).
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Liaison' es: Pronouncing a normally silent final consonant before a word starting with a vowel or mute H (e.g., *les amis* -> [le.z‿a.mi], *c'est un* -> [sɛ.t‿œ̃]).

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Enchaînement}} is defined as: Carrying over a final *pronounced* consonant to the next vowel with no pause (e.g., *il est* -> [i.lɛ], *avec elle* -> [a.vɛ.kɛl]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: Enchaînement
Description: Carrying over a final *pronounced* consonant to the next vowel with no pause (e.g., *il est* -> [i.lɛ], *avec elle* -> [a.vɛ.kɛl]).
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Enchaînement' es: Carrying over a final *pronounced* consonant to the next vowel with no pause (e.g., *il est* -> [i.lɛ], *avec elle* -> [a.vɛ.kɛl]).

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Silent Final Letters}} is defined as: Most final consonants are silent (e.g., *parler* [paʁle], *chat* [ʃa]), with exceptions often remembered via the word **c-a-r-e-f-u-l** (C, R, F, L are usually pronounced, e.g., *sac*, *mer*, *chef*, *sel*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: Silent Final Letters
Description: Most final consonants are silent (e.g., *parler* [paʁle], *chat* [ʃa]), with exceptions often remembered via the word **c-a-r-e-f-u-l** (C, R, F, L are usually pronounced, e.g., *sac*, *mer*, *chef*, *sel*).
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Silent Final Letters' es: Most final consonants are silent (e.g., *parler* [paʁle], *chat* [ʃa]), with exceptions often remembered via the word **c-a-r-e-f-u-l** (C, R, F, L are usually pronounced, e.g., *sac*, *mer*, *chef*, *sel*).

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::"Ne" Omission}} is defined as: In modern colloquial spoken French, the negation particle *ne* is almost always omitted (e.g., *Je ne sais pas* -> "Je sais pas" [ʒə sɛ pa], *Il n'est pas là* -> "Il est pas là" [i lɛ pa la]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: "Ne" Omission
Description: In modern colloquial spoken French, the negation particle *ne* is almost always omitted (e.g., *Je ne sais pas* -> "Je sais pas" [ʒə sɛ pa], *Il n'est pas là* -> "Il est pas là" [i lɛ pa la]).
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de '"Ne" Omission' es: In modern colloquial spoken French, the negation particle *ne* is almost always omitted (e.g., *Je ne sais pas* -> "Je sais pas" [ʒə sɛ pa], *Il n'est pas là* -> "Il est pas là" [i lɛ pa la]).

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::E-Caduc (Schwa) Deletion}} is defined as: The unstressed /ə/ is dropped in rapid speech whenever it doesn't create a cluster of three consonants (e.g., *un petit peu* -> "un p'tit peu" [œ̃ pti pø], *je te dis* -> "je t'dis" [ʒtdi] -> [ʃtdi] via voicing assimilation).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: E-Caduc (Schwa) Deletion
Description: The unstressed /ə/ is dropped in rapid speech whenever it doesn't create a cluster of three consonants (e.g., *un petit peu* -> "un p'tit peu" [œ̃ pti pø], *je te dis* -> "je t'dis" [ʒtdi] -> [ʃtdi] via voicing assimilation).
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'E-Caduc (Schwa) Deletion' es: The unstressed /ə/ is dropped in rapid speech whenever it doesn't create a cluster of three consonants (e.g., *un petit peu* -> "un p'tit peu" [œ̃ pti pø], *je te dis* -> "je t'dis" [ʒtdi] -> [ʃtdi] via voicing assimilation).

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Pronoun Contractions & Drops}} is defined as: - **Tu-Contraction**: *Tu* + vowel contracts to *t'* (e.g., *Tu es* -> "T'es" [tɛ], *Tu as* -> "T'as" [ta]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: Pronoun Contractions & Drops
Description: - **Tu-Contraction**: *Tu* + vowel contracts to *t'* (e.g., *Tu es* -> "T'es" [tɛ], *Tu as* -> "T'as" [ta]).
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Pronoun Contractions & Drops' es: - **Tu-Contraction**: *Tu* + vowel contracts to *t'* (e.g., *Tu es* -> "T'es" [tɛ], *Tu as* -> "T'as" [ta]).

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Il/Elle drops}} is defined as: The liquid /l/ in *il* or *ils* is often dropped before consonants (e.g., *il y a* -> "y'a" [ja], *il fait chaud* -> "i' fait chaud" [i fɛ ʃo], *ils veulent* -> "i' veulent" [i vœl]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: Il/Elle drops
Description: The liquid /l/ in *il* or *ils* is often dropped before consonants (e.g., *il y a* -> "y'a" [ja], *il fait chaud* -> "i' fait chaud" [i fɛ ʃo], *ils veulent* -> "i' veulent" [i vœl]).
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Il/Elle drops' es: The liquid /l/ in *il* or *ils* is often dropped before consonants (e.g., *il y a* -> "y'a" [ja], *il fait chaud* -> "i' fait chaud" [i fɛ ʃo], *ils veulent* -> "i' veulent" [i vœl]).

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Ce-Contraction}} is defined as: *Qu'est-ce qu'il y a* -> "Qu'est-ce qu'y'a" [kɛskja].
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: Ce-Contraction
Description: *Qu'est-ce qu'il y a* -> "Qu'est-ce qu'y'a" [kɛskja].
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Ce-Contraction' es: *Qu'est-ce qu'il y a* -> "Qu'est-ce qu'y'a" [kɛskja].

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Final Liquid Dropping}} is defined as: In casual speech, final liquids /l/ and /ʁ/ in words ending in a consonant + liquid are dropped (e.g., *table* -> "tab'" [tab], *mettre* -> "mett'" [mɛt], *possible* -> "possib'" [pɔsib]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: Final Liquid Dropping
Description: In casual speech, final liquids /l/ and /ʁ/ in words ending in a consonant + liquid are dropped (e.g., *table* -> "tab'" [tab], *mettre* -> "mett'" [mɛt], *possible* -> "possib'" [pɔsib]).
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Final Liquid Dropping' es: In casual speech, final liquids /l/ and /ʁ/ in words ending in a consonant + liquid are dropped (e.g., *table* -> "tab'" [tab], *mettre* -> "mett'" [mɛt], *possible* -> "possib'" [pɔsib]).

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Fixed Stress}} is defined as: Stress always falls on the last syllable of a rhythmic group (phrase).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: Fixed Stress
Description: Stress always falls on the last syllable of a rhythmic group (phrase).
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Fixed Stress' es: Stress always falls on the last syllable of a rhythmic group (phrase).

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Syllable-timing}} is defined as: Each syllable is given approximately equal duration.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
usage:
Rule: Syllable-timing
Description: Each syllable is given approximately equal duration.
spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Syllable-timing' es: Each syllable is given approximately equal duration.

text: What is the French pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Spoken French Shortcut: Drop "Ne"}}? If you say "ne" in everyday conversation in France, you will sound like a textbook. Drop it! Say "Je sais pas" instead of "Je ne sais pas".
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for French.
usage:
Tip: Spoken French Shortcut: Drop "Ne"
If you say "ne" in everyday conversation in France, you will sound like a textbook. Drop it! Say "Je sais pas" instead of "Je ne sais pas".
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de French para 'Spoken French Shortcut: Drop "Ne"'? If you say "ne" in everyday conversation in France, you will sound like a textbook. Drop it! Say "Je sais pas" instead of "Je ne sais pas".

text: What is the French pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "T'es" and "Y'a" Contractions}}? Casual French is heavily contracted. Always contract "tu" before vowels ("T'as faim ?" instead of "Tu as faim ?") and use "y'a" instead of "il y a".
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for French.
usage:
Tip: The "T'es" and "Y'a" Contractions
Casual French is heavily contracted. Always contract "tu" before vowels ("T'as faim ?" instead of "Tu as faim ?") and use "y'a" instead of "il y a".
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de French para 'The "T'es" and "Y'a" Contractions'? Casual French is heavily contracted. Always contract "tu" before vowels ("T'as faim ?" instead of "Tu as faim ?") and use "y'a" instead of "il y a".

text: What is the French pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Tip}}? Master the `ɥ` sound by saying `i` while rounding your lips into a tight `u` shape.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for French.
usage:
Tip: Tip
Master the `ɥ` sound by saying `i` while rounding your lips into a tight `u` shape.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de French para 'Tip'? Master the `ɥ` sound by saying `i` while rounding your lips into a tight `u` shape.

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::La Liaison}} is defined as: A normally silent final consonant is pronounced and linked to the initial vowel of the following word (e.g., *les enfants* → /le.zɑ̃.fɑ̃/).
explanation: Liaison maintains phonetic flow (enchaînement) by avoiding hiatus (the clashing of two vowels). The silent consonant is resurrected to serve as the onset of the next word's syllable.
usage:
Rule: La Liaison
Examples:
  • les enfants → "le-zanfan"
  • vous avez → "vou-zavez"
  • un petit ami → "un peti-tami"
spanish: La Liaison: Pronunciación de una consonante final muda al unirse con una palabra que empieza por vocal. Ejemplo: "les enfants" → "le-zanfan".

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Elision of 'E' Muet (Schwa Deletion)}} is defined as: Unstressed mute 'e' is dropped in fast speech, merging consonants (e.g., *je ne sais pas* → "j'sais pas" /ʃe.pa/).
explanation: Dropping the schwa /ə/ (e caduc) is the most prominent feature of rapid spoken French, reducing the syllable count and leading to consonant voicing assimilation (e.g., /ʒ/ becomes voiceless /ʃ/ before /s/).
usage:
Rule: Elision of 'E' Muet
Examples:
  • je ne sais pas → "j'sais pas" (sounds like "she-pa")
  • petit → "p'tit"
  • tout de suite → "tout'suite"
spanish: Caída de la 'E' muda: Omisión del sonido schwa francés en el habla rápida, provocando asimilación de consonantes contiguas.

text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Pronoun Compression}} is defined as: Pronoun groups contract by elision of vowels and merging of syllables in rapid speech (e.g., *il y a* → "y'a" /ja/, *tu as* → "t'as").
explanation: Subject and object pronouns before verbs are unstressed functional elements and undergo severe contraction in native conversational speed.
usage:
Rule: Pronoun Compression
Examples:
  • il y a → "y'a"
  • tu as → "t'as"
  • je suis → "chuis" (/ʃɥi/)
spanish: Compresión de Pronombres: Contracción y elisión de vocales en grupos de pronombres rápidos. Ejemplo: "il y a" → "y'a".

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Nous avons des enfants."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Nou-zavon de-zanfan.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: La Liaison
The normally silent final 's' in 'nous' and 'des' is voiced as [z] and links directly to the initial vowels.
usage: Formal: Nous avons des enfants. → Connected: Nou-zavon de-zanfan.
spanish: Spanish: Tenemos niños.

scenario: Pronunciation Drill: Elision of E Muet 🗣️
text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Je ne sais pas quoi faire."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::J'sais pas quoi faire.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Elision of E Muet
The mute 'e' in 'ne' is dropped, creating a consonant cluster /ʒs/ which assimilates to voiceless [ʃs] ('sh-pais').
usage: Formal: Je ne sais pas quoi faire. → Connected: J'sais pas quoi faire.
spanish: Spanish: No sé qué hacer.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Il y a un problème."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Y'a un problème.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Pronoun Compression
The pronoun sequence 'il y a' is compressed to the single syllable 'y'a' (/ja/) in conversational speech.
usage: Formal: Il y a un problème. → Connected: Y'a un problème.
spanish: Spanish: Hay un problema.

scenario: Cognitive Listening: The 3 Stages of Speech 🧠
text: In cognitive listening science, the model of {{c1::The 3 Stages of Speech}} (by Richard Cauldwell) describes three phases of language pronunciation: (1) **Garden Stage** (clear, dictionary pronunciation), (2) **Greenhouse Stage** (careful textbook speech), and (3) **Jungle Stage** (rapid, spontaneous native speech with deformations).
explanation: Language learners struggle in real life because they are trained in the structured rules of the Garden or Greenhouse stages, but native conversations occur in the Jungle, where sounds are compressed and blurred.
usage:
The Three Stages:
  • Garden: Clear, isolated, dictionary citation form.
  • Greenhouse: Textbook standard connected speech.
  • Jungle: Physical sound deformation: Squish (compression), Stream (no pauses), and Blur (acoustic masking).
spanish: El Modelo de los 3 Estadios del Habla (Richard Cauldwell): Garden (Jardín - diccionario), Greenhouse (Invernadero - cuidado) y Jungle (Selva - espontáneo y deformado).

text: In cognitive listening science, Cauldwell's rule for decoding rapid speech is: Do not try to listen to every isolated word; instead, perform {{c1::Tonic Syllable Hunting}} (target the main stressed syllable of each rhythmic group) and let the context fill in the gaps.
explanation: Because fast speech compresses unstressed vowels and drops consonants, hunting for every single word is cognitively overwhelming. Locking onto the stressed syllables provides the anchor points for the brain to deduce meaning.
usage: Action: Practice 'Tonic Syllable Hunting' rather than word-for-word decoding. Identify the rhythmic beats of the sentence.
spanish: Regla de Cauldwell para la Selva: No busques descifrar cada vocablo; caza la Sílaba Tónica (Tonic Syllable Hunting) de cada compás y deduce el resto por el contexto.

text: In cognitive listening science, the {{c1::Perceptual Magnet Effect}} (Patricia Kuhl) explains that the adult brain creates magnets based on native phonemes, causing it to incorrectly map new foreign sounds to native categories (e.g. ship vs sheep).
explanation: Infants can distinguish all human speech sounds. By adulthood, the brain establishes strong neural 'magnets' for native language sounds. When exposed to foreign phonemes, the brain pulls them towards the closest native magnet, creating auditory confusion.
usage: Correction: Use Minimal Pair Ear Training to 'demagnetize' the native filter and build new distinct neural categories.
spanish: Imán Perceptual (Patricia Kuhl): Filtro neuronal del cerebro adulto que deforma los sonidos extranjeros, encajándolos a la fuerza en los fonemas de su lengua materna.

text: In acoustic linguistics, the rhythm of languages is measured using the {{c1::nPVI (Normalized Pairwise Variability Index)}}, where stress-timed languages (e.g. English, German) have a high index, while syllable-timed languages (e.g. Spanish, French) have a low index.
explanation: nPVI measures the variability in duration between adjacent vowels. Stress-timed languages compress unstressed vowels to schwas, leading to massive duration differences (high index). Syllable-timed languages give equal duration to syllables (low index, metronomic rhythm).
usage:
Métricas:
  • Stress-Timed (English, German): High nPVI (>65).
  • Syllable-Timed (Spanish, French, Italian): Low nPVI (<45).
  • Mora-Timed (Japanese): Ultra-low nPVI (<35).
spanish: Métrica nPVI: Clasificación acústica del ritmo lingüístico. Mide la variación de duración entre sílabas contiguas; alta en Stress-Timed (inglés) y baja en Syllable-Timed (español).

text: In language acquisition, the {{c1::Speed-Ramping Method}} conditions processing speed by listening to a short clip in a specific order: 0.8x (map junctions) → 1.0x → 1.25x (over-speed training) → 1.0x (now perceived as slow motion).
explanation: By forcing the brain to process speech at 1.25x speed, the subsequent return to 1.0x normal speed feels significantly slower, lowering the processing strain and enhancing comprehension.
usage: Routine: Take a 15-second audio clip. Listen: 0.8x → 1.0x → 1.25x (3 reps) → 1.0x (normal).
spanish: Método Speed-Ramping (Escalado de Velocidad): Entrenamiento del procesamiento neuro-auditivo acelerando y desacelerando el audio (0.8x → 1.0x → 1.25x → 1.0x).

text: In language acquisition, Stephen Krashen's {{c1::Narrow Listening}} technique involves consuming audio content from the **same speaker** on the **same topic** for 2-3 weeks to lower cognitive load by stabilizing the speaker's idiolect.
explanation: Each native speaker has a unique idiolect (speech patterns, pitch, preferred contractions). Keeping the speaker and topic stable allows the brain to map that individual's sounds easily, freeing up cognitive capacity to learn language structure.
usage: Action: Choose one podcaster/speaker on one specific topic, and consume their content exclusively for 2 weeks.
spanish: Narrow Listening (Escucha Estrecha): Consumo de contenido del mismo hablante y tema durante 2-3 semanas para familiarizarse con su idiolecto y reducir la fatiga mental.

text: In language acquisition, the {{c1::Micro-Dictation}} protocol (Arguelles) consists of transcribing a 10-second fast native clip without subtitles, leaving gaps `[???]` for incomprehensible parts, and then checking which phonetic reduction rule caused the gap.
explanation: Transcribing short segments forces you to confront exactly what your brain missed. Comparing your draft with the transcript reveals your specific perceptual blind spots (e.g. not recognizing a dropped H or a glottal stop).
usage: Protocol: Transcribe 10s audio → mark gaps [???] → compare with text → log the elision/reduction rule.
spanish: Protocolo de Micro-Dictado (Arguelles): Transcripción de clips cortos marcando vacíos [???] para identificar qué regla de habla rápida causó la confusión auditiva.

text: In prosodic pragmatics, intonation contours carry structural meaning: a {{c1::Falling Pitch (📉)}} indicates certainty and WH-questions, a {{c2::Rising Pitch (📈)}} indicates Yes/No questions and lists, and a {{c3::Fall-Rise Pitch (📉📈)}} conveys politeness or reservations.
explanation: Pitch contours carry pragmatic context beyond words. High Rising Terminal (HRT) is a rising contour on statements used in Australian/American English to check for listener agreement.
usage:
Contours:
  • Falling (📉): 'Where is the station?' (Certainty/WH-question)
  • Rising (📈): 'Are you ready?' (Yes/No query)
  • Fall-Rise (📉📈): 'Well, I think so...' (Reservation/Politeness)
spanish: Contornos de Pitch (Entonación): Curvas tonales que aportan pragmática. Descendente (📉) para afirmaciones/WH-questions; Ascendente (📈) para Yes/No; Descendente-Ascendente (📉📈) para reservas.

explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.

text: In a business setting or with strangers, you should greet them formally with: '{{c1::Guten Morgen}}' (until 11 AM) or '{{c1::Guten Tag}}' (11 AM to 6 PM).
explanation: German distinguishes strictly between formal and informal greetings. Formal greetings are used with the pronoun 'Sie' and change based on the time of day.
usage:
Time-based formal greetings:
  • Guten Morgen (Good morning - until 11 AM)
  • Guten Tag (Good day - 11 AM to 6 PM)
  • Guten Abend (Good evening - after 6 PM)
spanish: En un ámbito empresarial o con extraños, debes saludarlos formalmente con: 'Guten Morgen' (hasta las 11 AM) o 'Guten Tag' (de 11 AM a 6 PM).

text: To ask a stranger 'How are you?' formally in German, you say: '{{c1::Wie geht es Ihnen?}}'
explanation: Ihnen is the dative case of the formal pronoun 'Sie'. For friends or peers, you would use the informal 'Wie geht es dir?' or simply 'Wie geht's?'.
usage:
Usage examples:
  • Formal: Wie geht es Ihnen, Herr Müller? (How are you, Mr. Müller?)
  • Informal: Wie geht es dir, Anna? (How are you, Anna?)
spanish: Para preguntar formalmente a un extraño '¿Cómo está usted?' en alemán, dices: 'Wie geht es Ihnen?'.

text: A common informal way to say goodbye to friends in German is '{{c1::Tschüss}}' or '{{c1::Bis bald}}' (See you soon).
explanation: Tschüss is the most common informal goodbye in Germany. Auf Wiedersehen is formal and used in shops, business meetings, or with strangers.
usage:
Goodbye options:
  • Informal: Tschüss! (Bye!) or Bis später! (See you later!)
  • Formal: Auf Wiedersehen! (Goodbye!)
spanish: Una forma informal común de despedirse de los amigos en alemán es 'Tschüss' o 'Bis bald' (Hasta pronto).

text: In German, how do you say 'Welcome'? {{c1::Willkommen}}
explanation: Used to welcome guests. Often prefixed as 'Herzlich willkommen' (A warm welcome).
usage: Term: Willkommen | Example: Herzlich willkommen in Berlin! (A warm welcome to Berlin!)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Welcome' en alemán? Willkommen

text: In German, how do you say 'See you tomorrow'? {{c1::Bis morgen}}
explanation: Literally 'until tomorrow'. Standard parting phrase for classmates, colleagues, or friends.
usage: Term: Bis morgen | Example: Schönen Feierabend, bis morgen! (Have a nice evening, see you tomorrow!)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'See you tomorrow' en alemán? Bis morgen

text: In German, how do you say 'See you later'? {{c1::Bis später}}
explanation: Literally 'until later'. Used when you expect to see the person again on the same day.
usage: Term: Bis später | Example: Ich muss los, bis später! (I have to go, see you later!)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'See you later' en alemán? Bis später

text: In German, the extremely versatile word for both 'Please' and 'You're welcome' is '{{c1::Bitte}}'.
explanation: Depending on context, 'Bitte' can mean please, you're welcome, here you go, or even 'pardon?' when you didn't hear something.
usage: Examples: Ein Bier, bitte. (A beer, please.) | - Danke! - Bitte! (- Thanks! - You're welcome!)
spanish: En alemán, la palabra 'Bitte' es muy versátil y significa tanto 'Por favor' como 'De nada'.

text: In German, how do you say 'Thank you very much'? {{c1::Danke schön}} or {{c1::Vielen Dank}}.
explanation: Both are polite ways to express strong gratitude. 'Vielen Dank' literally means 'many thanks'.
usage: Examples: Danke schön für das Geschenk. (Thank you very much for the gift.) | Vielen Dank für Ihre Hilfe. (Many thanks for your help.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Muchas gracias' en alemán? Danke schön / Vielen Dank.

text: In German, a polite and common way to say 'You're welcome / My pleasure' is '{{c1::Gern geschehen}}'.
explanation: Literally means 'happily occurred'. Used in response to a thank you.
usage: Dialogue: - Vielen Dank! - Gern geschehen!
spanish: En alemán, una respuesta cortés a 'gracias' es 'Gern geschehen' (Con gusto / De nada).

text: In German, the formal question to ask someone their name is '{{c1::Wie heißen Sie?}}' and the informal is '{{c2::Wie heißt du?}}'.
explanation: Uses the verb 'heißen' (to be named). Note the capital 'S' in formal 'Sie'.
usage: Formal: Guten Tag, wie heißen Sie? | Informal: Hallo, wie heißt du?
spanish: En alemán, la pregunta formal para preguntar el nombre es 'Wie heißen Sie?' y la informal es 'Wie heißt du?'.

text: In German, how do you say 'My name is [Name]'? {{c1::Ich heiße}} [Name] or {{c1::Mein Name ist}} [Name].
explanation: You can use the verb 'heißen' or the phrase 'Mein Name ist...'.
usage: Examples: Ich heiße Max. | Mein Name ist Max.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Me llamo...' o 'Mi nombre es...' en alemán? Ich heiße / Mein Name ist.

text: In German, how do you say 'Have a nice day' to wish someone well upon parting? {{c1::Schönen Tag noch}}
explanation: Commonly said by shopkeepers, cashiers, or friends when saying goodbye during the day.
usage: Term: Schönen Tag noch! | Dialogue: - Tschüss! - Schönen Tag noch!
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Que tenga un buen día' al despedirse en alemán? Schönen Tag noch.
