explanation: German verb conjugation of 'sein' is highly irregular and one of the most basic elements of A1 grammar.
usage: Term: sein | Example: glücklich sein (to be happy)
spanish: ¿Cuál es el infinitivo del verbo 'ser/estar' en alemán? sein

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: To negate the verb 'sein' in German, you place the negation particle {{c1::nicht}} after the verb. Example: I am not ready = {{c2::Ich bin nicht bereit}}.
explanation: 'nicht' is used to negate adjectives, adverbs, pronouns, and verbs in German.
usage: Example: Er ist nicht hier. (He is not here.)
spanish: Para negar el verbo 'sein' en alemán, se coloca 'nicht' después del verbo conjugado. 'Ich bin nicht' = Yo no soy/estoy.

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, after accusative prepositions like 'für' (for), the pronoun 'ich' becomes {{c1::mich}} (me) and 'du' becomes {{c2::dich}} (you).
explanation: 'für' is a preposition that always demands the accusative case.
usage: Examples: Das ist für mich. (This is for me.) | Ich habe etwas für dich. (I have something for you.)
spanish: En alemán, después de preposiciones acusativas como 'für', 'ich' se convierte en 'mich' y 'du' en 'dich'.

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, after dative prepositions like 'mit' (with), 'er' (he) becomes {{c1::ihm}} (him) and 'sie' (she) becomes {{c2::ihr}} (her).
explanation: 'mit' is a preposition that always demands the dative case.
usage: Examples: Ich gehe mit ihm. (I am going with him.) | Wir sprechen mit ihr. (We are speaking with her.)
spanish: En alemán, después de preposiciones dativas como 'mit', 'er' se convierte en 'ihm' y 'sie' en 'ihr'.

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, after dative prepositions like 'mit' (with), 'wir' (we) becomes {{c1::uns}} (us) and 'ihr' (you all) becomes {{c2::euch}} (you all).
explanation: Dative forms for first and second-person plural pronouns.
usage: Examples: Komm mit uns! (Come with us!) | Ich fahre mit euch. (I am driving with you all.)
spanish: En alemán, después de 'mit', 'wir' se convierte en 'uns' y 'ihr' en 'euch'.

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, after dative prepositions like 'mit' (with), 'sie' (they) becomes {{c1::ihnen}} (them) and formal 'Sie' (you) becomes {{c2::Ihnen}} (you formal).
explanation: Note that 'Ihnen' (formal you) is always capitalized, whereas 'ihnen' (them) is lowercase.
usage: Examples: Wie geht es Ihnen? (How are you? - formal) | Ich arbeite mit ihnen. (I work with them.)
spanish: En alemán, después de 'mit', el pronombre 'sie' (ellos/ellas) se convierte en 'ihnen' y 'Sie' (formal) se convierte en 'Ihnen'.

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: To form a question in German using the verb 'sein', you swap the subject and verb: 'Are you ready?' is '{{c1::Bist du bereit?}}' and 'Are you sure (formal)?' is '{{c2::Sind Sie sicher?}}'.
explanation: German questions are formed by placing the inflected verb at the first position (verb-initial).
usage: Examples: Bist du bereit? (Are you ready?) | Sind Sie sicher? (Are you sure? - formal)
spanish: Para formar preguntas en alemán con 'sein', se invierte el sujeto y el verbo. 'Bist du bereit?' = ¿Estás listo?

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, neuter nouns (like 'das Kind' or 'das Buch') are replaced by the personal pronoun {{c1::es}}. Example: Das Buch ist alt. {{c2::Es}} ist gut. (The book is old. It is good.)
explanation: Neuter nouns are referred to by 'es', masculine by 'er', and feminine by 'sie'.
usage: Example: Das Buch ist alt. Es ist gut. (The book is old. It is good.)
spanish: En alemán, los sustantivos neutros se reemplazan por el pronombre 'es'. Por ejemplo, 'das Buch' -> 'es'.

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, what is the gender-neutral demonstrative phrase used to say 'this is / that is / these are / those are'? {{c1::Das ist}} or {{c1::Das sind}}.
explanation: 'Das ist' is used for singular subjects, and 'Das sind' is used for plural subjects, regardless of gender.
usage: Examples: Das ist mein Freund. (This is my friend.) | Das sind meine Bücher. (These are my books.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Esto es / Estos son' en alemán de manera invariable? Das ist (singular) / Das sind (plural).

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, how do you say 'Who is that?'? {{c1::Wer ist das?}}
explanation: Wer is the question word for 'Who' in German.
usage: Dialogue: - Wer ist das? - Das ist meine Schwester. (- Who is that? - That is my sister.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Quién es ese/a?' en alemán? Wer ist das?

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, how do you say 'What is that?'? {{c1::Was ist das?}}
explanation: Was is the question word for 'What' in German.
usage: Example: Was ist das dort auf dem Tisch? (What is that there on the table?)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Qué es eso?' en alemán? Was ist das?

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, what is the singular masculine subject pronoun? {{c1::er}}
explanation: Used for male persons and any masculine grammatical noun (e.g. 'der Tisch' -> 'er ist').
usage: Example: Wo ist der Stuhl? Er ist hier. (Where is the chair? It is here.)
spanish: ¿Cuál es el pronombre de sujeto singular masculino en alemán? er

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, what is the singular feminine subject pronoun? {{c1::sie}}
explanation: Used for female persons and any feminine grammatical noun (e.g. 'die Tasche' -> 'sie ist').
usage: Example: Wo ist die Tasche? Sie ist weg. (Where is the bag? It is gone.)
spanish: ¿Cuál es el pronombre de sujeto singular femenino en alemán? sie

scenario: German A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In German, the formal 'you' is '{{c1::Sie}}' and the informal plural 'you all' is '{{c2::ihr}}'.
explanation: The formal 'Sie' is always capitalized and conjugates like the plural 'they' (sie). The informal 'ihr' is lowercase and has its own conjugation.
usage: Formal: Sind Sie müde? | Informal plural: Seid ihr müde?
spanish: En alemán, el 'usted' formal es 'Sie' (siempre en mayúsculas) y el 'vosotros/ustedes' informal es 'ihr'.

text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Tense vs. Lax}} is defined as: Long vowels are usually tense (e.g., /iː/), while short vowels are lax (e.g., /ɪ/).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
usage:
Rule: Tense vs. Lax
Description: Long vowels are usually tense (e.g., /iː/), while short vowels are lax (e.g., /ɪ/).
spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Tense vs. Lax' es: Long vowels are usually tense (e.g., /iː/), while short vowels are lax (e.g., /ɪ/).

text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Umlauts}} is defined as: The vowels /yː, ʏ, øː, œ, ɛː/ (written as ü, ö, ä) are central to German phonology.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
usage:
Rule: Umlauts
Description: The vowels /yː, ʏ, øː, œ, ɛː/ (written as ü, ö, ä) are central to German phonology.
spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Umlauts' es: The vowels /yː, ʏ, øː, œ, ɛː/ (written as ü, ö, ä) are central to German phonology.

text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Schwa and Vocalic R}} is defined as: /ə/ is common in unstressed prefixes/suffixes. Final '-er' is usually realized as a central vowel /ɐ/ (e.g., *Vater* [ˈfaːtɐ]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
usage:
Rule: The Schwa and Vocalic R
Description: /ə/ is common in unstressed prefixes/suffixes. Final '-er' is usually realized as a central vowel /ɐ/ (e.g., *Vater* [ˈfaːtɐ]).
spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'The Schwa and Vocalic R' es: /ə/ is common in unstressed prefixes/suffixes. Final '-er' is usually realized as a central vowel /ɐ/ (e.g., *Vater* [ˈfaːtɐ]).

text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Glottal Stop /ʔ/}} is defined as: Inserted before initial vowels of stressed syllables and word roots (e.g., *Beamter* [bəˈʔamtɐ]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
usage:
Rule: Glottal Stop /ʔ/
Description: Inserted before initial vowels of stressed syllables and word roots (e.g., *Beamter* [bəˈʔamtɐ]).
spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Glottal Stop /ʔ/' es: Inserted before initial vowels of stressed syllables and word roots (e.g., *Beamter* [bəˈʔamtɐ]).

text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Ich-Laut [ç] vs. Ach-Laut [x]}} is defined as: - [ç] follows front vowels and consonants (e.g., *ich*, *manchmal*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
usage:
Rule: Ich-Laut [ç] vs. Ach-Laut [x]
Description: - [ç] follows front vowels and consonants (e.g., *ich*, *manchmal*).
spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Ich-Laut [ç] vs. Ach-Laut [x]' es: - [ç] follows front vowels and consonants (e.g., *ich*, *manchmal*).

text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Final-Obstruent Devoicing}} is defined as: Voiced stops and fricatives become voiceless at the end of a syllable (e.g., *Hund* [hʊnt], *Tag* [taːk]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
usage:
Rule: Final-Obstruent Devoicing
Description: Voiced stops and fricatives become voiceless at the end of a syllable (e.g., *Hund* [hʊnt], *Tag* [taːk]).
spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Final-Obstruent Devoicing' es: Voiced stops and fricatives become voiceless at the end of a syllable (e.g., *Hund* [hʊnt], *Tag* [taːk]).

text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Aspiration}} is defined as: Voiceless stops /p, t, k/ are strongly aspirated ([pʰ, tʰ, kʰ]) when starting stressed syllables (e.g., *Tee* [tʰeː]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
usage:
Rule: Aspiration
Description: Voiceless stops /p, t, k/ are strongly aspirated ([pʰ, tʰ, kʰ]) when starting stressed syllables (e.g., *Tee* [tʰeː]).
spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Aspiration' es: Voiceless stops /p, t, k/ are strongly aspirated ([pʰ, tʰ, kʰ]) when starting stressed syllables (e.g., *Tee* [tʰeː]).

text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Vocalic "R"}} is defined as: /r/ is vocalized to [ɐ] in unstressed syllable codas and after long vowels (e.g., *Vater* -> [ˈfaːtɐ], *wir* -> [viːɐ]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
usage:
Rule: The Vocalic "R"
Description: /r/ is vocalized to [ɐ] in unstressed syllable codas and after long vowels (e.g., *Vater* -> [ˈfaːtɐ], *wir* -> [viːɐ]).
spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'The Vocalic "R"' es: /r/ is vocalized to [ɐ] in unstressed syllable codas and after long vowels (e.g., *Vater* -> [ˈfaːtɐ], *wir* -> [viːɐ]).

text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::'-en' Reduction (Verb/Noun endings)}} is defined as: In rapid speech, final unstressed '-en' reduces to a syllabic nasal matching the preceding consonant's place of articulation:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
usage:
Rule: '-en' Reduction (Verb/Noun endings)
Description: In rapid speech, final unstressed '-en' reduces to a syllabic nasal matching the preceding consonant's place of articulation:
spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de ''-en' Reduction (Verb/Noun endings)' es: In rapid speech, final unstressed '-en' reduces to a syllabic nasal matching the preceding consonant's place of articulation:

text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Final "e" Dropping}} is defined as: In casual spoken German, the final unstressed -e of the first-person singular verb conjugation is almost always dropped (e.g., *ich habe* -> "ich hab'" [ɪç hap], *ich gehe* -> "ich geh'" [ɪç ɡeː]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
usage:
Rule: Final "e" Dropping
Description: In casual spoken German, the final unstressed -e of the first-person singular verb conjugation is almost always dropped (e.g., *ich habe* -> "ich hab'" [ɪç hap], *ich gehe* -> "ich geh'" [ɪç ɡeː]).
spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Final "e" Dropping' es: In casual spoken German, the final unstressed -e of the first-person singular verb conjugation is almost always dropped (e.g., *ich habe* -> "ich hab'" [ɪç hap], *ich gehe* -> "ich geh'" [ɪç ɡeː]).

text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Article/Pronoun Reduction}} is defined as: - **Indefinite Articles**:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
usage:
Rule: Article/Pronoun Reduction

text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Pronouns}} is defined as: - *es* -> "'s" [s] (e.g., *Wie geht es dir?* -> "Wie geht's dir?" [viː ɡeːts diːɐ], *Ich habe es* -> "Ich hab's").
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
usage:
Rule: Pronouns
Description: - *es* -> "'s" [s] (e.g., *Wie geht es dir?* -> "Wie geht's dir?" [viː ɡeːts diːɐ], *Ich habe es* -> "Ich hab's").
spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Pronouns' es: - *es* -> "'s" [s] (e.g., *Wie geht es dir?* -> "Wie geht's dir?" [viː ɡeːts diːɐ], *Ich habe es* -> "Ich hab's").

text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Final -t Omission}} is defined as: In very high-speed speech, final /t/ is sometimes dropped from common words (e.g., *ist* -> "is" [ɪs], *nicht* -> "nich" [nɪç]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
usage:
Rule: Final -t Omission
Description: In very high-speed speech, final /t/ is sometimes dropped from common words (e.g., *ist* -> "is" [ɪs], *nicht* -> "nich" [nɪç]).
spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Final -t Omission' es: In very high-speed speech, final /t/ is sometimes dropped from common words (e.g., *ist* -> "is" [ɪs], *nicht* -> "nich" [nɪç]).

text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Word Stress}} is defined as: Usually falls on the first syllable of the root (e.g., **Spra**-che), but prefixes can shift this (e.g., be-**su**-chen).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
usage:
Rule: Word Stress
Description: Usually falls on the first syllable of the root (e.g., **Spra**-che), but prefixes can shift this (e.g., be-**su**-chen).
spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Word Stress' es: Usually falls on the first syllable of the root (e.g., **Spra**-che), but prefixes can shift this (e.g., be-**su**-chen).

text: In German phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Sentence Melody}} is defined as: Typically falling at the end of statements and rising for questions.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for German.
usage:
Rule: Sentence Melody
Description: Typically falling at the end of statements and rising for questions.
spanish: En la fonética de German, la regla de 'Sentence Melody' es: Typically falling at the end of statements and rising for questions.

text: What is the German pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The Nasal Ending Shortcut}}? Practice merging the '-en' ending with the previous consonant: "haben" shouldn't sound like "hah-ben," but "hahb-m". This single tip will instantly make your German sound much more native.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for German.
usage:
Tip: The Nasal Ending Shortcut
Practice merging the '-en' ending with the previous consonant: "haben" shouldn't sound like "hah-ben," but "hahb-m". This single tip will instantly make your German sound much more native.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de German para 'The Nasal Ending Shortcut'? Practice merging the '-en' ending with the previous consonant: "haben" shouldn't sound like "hah-ben," but "hahb-m". This single tip will instantly make your German sound much more native.

text: What is the German pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "'n" and "'ne" Reductions}}? Don't over-pronounce indefinite articles in casual talk. Use "'n" for masculine/neuter nominative, "'nen" for accusative, and "'ne" for feminine, just like English speakers say "a" instead of "an".
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for German.
usage:
Tip: The "'n" and "'ne" Reductions
Don't over-pronounce indefinite articles in casual talk. Use "'n" for masculine/neuter nominative, "'nen" for accusative, and "'ne" for feminine, just like English speakers say "a" instead of "an".
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de German para 'The "'n" and "'ne" Reductions'? Don't over-pronounce indefinite articles in casual talk. Use "'n" for masculine/neuter nominative, "'nen" for accusative, and "'ne" for feminine, just like English speakers say "a" instead of "an".

text: What is the German pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Tip}}? The glottal stop /ʔ/ is why German often sounds "staccato" or interrupted compared to the flow of French.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for German.
usage:
Tip: Tip
The glottal stop /ʔ/ is why German often sounds "staccato" or interrupted compared to the flow of French.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de German para 'Tip'? The glottal stop /ʔ/ is why German often sounds "staccato" or interrupted compared to the flow of French.

text: In German phonetics, the vowel {{c1::ö / oe}} is pronounced as a front rounded vowel, which can be long and tense (e.g., /øː/ as in *schön*) or short and lax (e.g., /œ/ as in *können*).
explanation: Formed by rounding the lips as if saying 'o' while pronouncing the front vowel 'e'.
usage:
Rule: ö pronunciation
Examples: schön (/ʃøːn/), können (/ˈkœnən/).
spanish: En la fonética del alemán, la vocal 'ö / oe' se pronuncia redondeando los labios para decir 'o' mientras se pronuncia la vocal 'e'.

text: In German phonetics, the consonant clusters 'sp' and 'st' at the beginning of a syllable or word are pronounced as {{c1::/ʃp/ (shp)}} and {{c1::/ʃt/ (sht)}} respectively (e.g., *sprechen* -> [ˈʃpʁɛçn̩], *stehen* -> [ˈʃteːən]).
explanation: German converts the 's' into a 'sch' sound when followed by 'p' or 't' at the start of a word/root.
usage:
Rule: sp/st pronunciation
Examples: Sport (sport), Stadt (city).
spanish: En la fonética del alemán, los grupos consonánticos 'sp' y 'st' al principio de una palabra se pronuncian como '/ ʃp /' (shp) y '/ ʃt /' (sht).

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Der Hund war den ganzen Tag aktiv."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Der Hunt war den ganzen Taak aktif.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Auslautverhärtung
Voiced consonants /d/ in 'Hund', /g/ in 'Tag', and /v/ in 'aktiv' are devoiced to [t], [k], and [f] at syllable endings.
usage: Formal: Der Hund war den ganzen Tag aktiv. → Connected: Der Hunt war den ganzen Taak aktif.
spanish: Spanish: El perro estuvo activo todo el día.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Ich esse einen Apfel."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Ich [ʔ]esse [ʔ]einen [ʔ]Apfel.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Knacklaut (Glottal Stop)
A glottal stop [ʔ] is inserted before each word-initial vowel, separating the words sharply.
usage: Formal: Ich esse einen Apfel. → Connected: Ich [ʔ]esse [ʔ]einen [ʔ]Apfel.
spanish: Spanish: Como una manzana.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Wir kommen morgen."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Wir komm'n morg'n.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Reduction of final -en
The final unstressed '-en' syllables lose their vowels, leaving a syllabic nasal [n̩].
usage: Formal: Wir kommen morgen. → Connected: Wir komm'n morg'n.
spanish: Spanish: Venimos mañana.

text: In Hindi phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Schwa Deletion (Syncope)}} is defined as: The inherent schwa vowel /ə/ in consonants is automatically deleted at the end of words or in unstressed intermediate syllables (e.g., *Khabara* खबर → "Khabr").
explanation: Hindi inherits a silent default schwa vowel in consonants from Sanskrit. In modern fast Hindi, this schwa is dropped in syllable codas, which dictates the correct pronunciation of verb and noun endings.
usage:
Rule: Schwa Deletion
Examples:
  • Khabara (news) → "Khabr"
  • Karanā (to do) → "Karnaa"
  • Bharata (India) → "Bhaarat"
spanish: Elisión de la Schwa (Síncope): Eliminación automática de la vocal neutra implícita al final de palabra o en sílabas internas átonas. Ejemplo: "Khabara" → "Khabr".

text: In Hindi phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Aspirated 'H' Elision}} is defined as: The voiced glottal fricative /ɦ/ is weakened or omitted in fast speech, modifying surrounding vowels (e.g., *bahut* बहुत → "baut", *kahana* कहना → "kana").
explanation: Because aspiration requires high vocal energy, the /h/ sound is often elided in fast conversation, which can lead to vowel blending.
usage:
Rule: Aspirated 'H' Elision
Examples:
  • bahut (very) → "baut"
  • kahana (to say) → "kana"
  • raha (stay) → "raa"
spanish: Elisión de la 'H' aspirada: Omisión del fonema glotal /ɦ/ en el habla rápida, provocando la fusión de las vocales colindantes. Ejemplo: "bahut" → "baut".

text: In Hindi phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Postposition Merger}} is defined as: Pronouns and their following postpositions fuse into a single word in writing and speech (e.g., *is me* इस में → "isme", *us ko* उस को → "usko").
explanation: Postpositions in Hindi behave similarly to prepositions in English. In speech, they form a single phonological word with the preceding pronoun, reducing transition time.
usage:
Rule: Postposition Merger
Examples:
  • is me (in this) → "isme"
  • us ko (to him/her) → "usko"
  • kis liye (why) → "kisliye"
spanish: Fusión de Posposiciones: Unión fonética de los pronombres con sus posposiciones para formar una sola unidad rítmica. Ejemplo: "is me" → "isme".

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "यह खबर है (Yeh khabara hai)."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Yeh khabr hai.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Schwa Deletion
The final implicit schwa vowel in 'khabara' is dropped, creating a consonant blend 'khabr'.
usage: Formal: यह खबर है (Yeh khabara hai). → Connected: Yeh khabr hai.
spanish: Spanish: Esta es la noticia.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "जल्दी कहना, बहुत अच्छा है (Jaldi kahana, bahut accha hai)."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Jaldi kana, baut accha hai.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Aspirated 'H' Elision
The soft /h/ in 'kahana' and 'bahut' is dropped in fast conversation, fusing surrounding vowels.
usage: Formal: जल्दी कहना, बहुत अच्छा है (Jaldi kahana, bahut accha hai). → Connected: Jaldi kana, baut accha hai.
spanish: Spanish: Dilo rápido, es muy bueno.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "इस में देखो (Is me dekho)."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Isme dekho.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Postposition Merger
The pronoun 'is' and postposition 'me' fuse into a single rhythmic word 'isme'.
usage: Formal: इस में देखो (Is me dekho). → Connected: Isme dekho.
spanish: Spanish: Mira dentro de esto.

explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.

spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hello / Goodbye - Informal' en Italian? Ciao

text: In Italian, how do you say 'How are you? - Formal'? {{c1::Come sta?}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Come sta? | Meaning: How are you? - Formal
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'How are you? - Formal' en Italian? Come sta?

text: In Italian, how do you say 'Goodbye - Formal'? {{c1::Arrivederci}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Arrivederci | Meaning: Goodbye - Formal
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Goodbye - Formal' en Italian? Arrivederci

text: In Italian, how do you say 'See you soon'? {{c1::A presto}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: A presto | Meaning: See you soon
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'See you soon' en Italian? A presto

text: In Italian, how do you say 'Good night'? {{c1::Buonanotte}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Buonanotte | Meaning: Good night
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good night' en Italian? Buonanotte

text: In Italian, 'ciao' is an informal greeting that means both {{c1::hello / hi}} and {{c2::bye / goodbye}}.
explanation: 'Ciao' is the most popular informal greeting in Italian. It should not be used in formal contexts.
usage: Term: Ciao | Dialogue: - Ciao Marco! - Ciao Sofia, come va?
spanish: En italiano, 'ciao' es un saludo informal que significa tanto 'hola' como 'adiós'.

text: In Italian, how do you say 'Good morning / Good day' formally? {{c1::Buongiorno}}
explanation: Used from morning until early afternoon as a polite greeting.
usage: Term: Buongiorno | Example: Buongiorno, Signor Rossi. (Good morning, Mr. Rossi.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenos días / Buen día' formalmente en italiano? Buongiorno.

text: In Italian, how do you say 'Good night' (when going to bed)? {{c1::Buonanotte}}
explanation: Used specifically when someone is going to sleep or parting late at night.
usage: Term: Buonanotte | Example: Buonanotte, sogni d'oro. (Good night, sweet dreams.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenas noches (al acostarse)' en italiano? Buonanotte.

text: In Italian, how do you say 'Goodbye' formally? {{c1::Arrivederci}}
explanation: Literally 'until we see each other again'. Used in shops, offices, or with strangers.
usage: Term: Arrivederci | Example: Grazie, arrivederci! (Thank you, goodbye!)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Adiós' formalmente en italiano? Arrivederci.

text: In Italian, how do you say 'Please'? {{c1::Per favore}} or {{c1::Per piacere}}.
explanation: Both phrases are extremely common and polite.
usage: Examples: Un caffè, per favore. (A coffee, please.) | Mi aiuti, per piacere. (Help me, please.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Por favor' en italiano? Per favore / Per piacere.

text: In Italian, how do you say 'Thank you very much'? {{c1::Grazie mille}}
explanation: Literally 'a thousand thanks'. The standard way to show strong appreciation.
usage: Term: Grazie mille | Example: Grazie mille per l'aiuto. (Thank you very much for the help.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Muchas gracias' en italiano? Grazie mille.

text: In Italian, the standard response to 'Grazie' is '{{c1::Prego}}'.
explanation: 'Prego' means 'You're welcome'. It is also used by waiters or hosts to say 'Please (after you)' or 'How can I help you?'.
usage: Dialogue: - Grazie! - Prego.
spanish: En italiano, la respuesta estándar a 'Grazie' es 'Prego' (De nada).

text: In Italian, the informal way to ask someone their name is '{{c1::Come ti chiami?}}' and the response is '{{c2::Mi chiamo}} [Name]'.
explanation: Uses the reflexive verb 'chiamarsi' (to call oneself). Literally: 'How do you call yourself?'.
usage: Dialogue: - Come ti chiami? - Mi chiamo Jesus.
spanish: En italiano, la pregunta informal para preguntar el nombre es 'Come ti chiami?' y la respuesta es 'Mi chiamo...'.

text: In Italian, how do you say 'Nice to meet you' when meeting someone for the first time? {{c1::Piacere}} or {{c1::Piacere di conoscerti}}.
explanation: 'Piacere' alone is very common. 'Piacere di conoscerti' is informal, and 'Piacere di conoscerla' is formal.
usage: Dialogue: - Mi chiamo Sofia. - Piacere, io sono Marco.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Mucho gusto / Encantado' en italiano? Piacere / Piacere di conoscerti.

text: In Italian, how do you ask 'How are you?' informally? {{c1::Come stai?}} or {{c1::Come va?}}.
explanation: 'Come stai?' is for peers/friends. 'Come va?' is generic (How's it going?). The formal version is 'Come sta?'.
usage: Dialogue: - Ciao, come stai? - Bene, grazie. E tu?
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cómo estás?' informalmente en italiano? Come stai? / Come va?.

text: In Italian, how do you say 'Have a nice day' upon parting? {{c1::Buona giornata}}
explanation: Similar to French 'Bonne journée', used when leaving during the day.
usage: Term: Buona giornata | Example: Grazie, buona giornata! (Thank you, have a nice day!)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Que tenga un buen día' al despedirse en italiano? Buona giornata.

text: In Italian, a very common and friendly way to say 'See you / See you later' is '{{c1::Ci vediamo}}'.
explanation: Literally means 'we see each other'. Used daily among friends and coworkers.
usage: Term: Ci vediamo | Example: Ciao ragazzi, ci vediamo domani! (Bye guys, see you tomorrow!)
spanish: En italiano, una forma muy común y amistosa de decir 'Nos vemos' es 'Ci vediamo'.

text: In Italian, how do you say 'Good day' as a wish when parting? {{c1::Buona giornata}}
explanation: Used when leaving during the day. Do not confuse with 'Buongiorno' which is used when arriving.
usage: Term: Buona giornata | Dialogue: - Arrivederci! - Buona giornata!
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Que tenga un buen día' al despedirse en italiano? Buona giornata.

scenario: Italian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️

explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Io sono | Meaning: I am
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'I am' en Italian? Io sono

scenario: Italian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Italian, how do you say 'You are'? {{c1::Tu sei}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Tu sei | Meaning: You are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'You are' en Italian? Tu sei

scenario: Italian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Italian, how do you say 'He/She is'? {{c1::Lui/Lei è}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Lui/Lei è | Meaning: He/She is
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'He/She is' en Italian? Lui/Lei è

scenario: Italian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Italian, how do you say 'We are'? {{c1::Noi siamo}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Noi siamo | Meaning: We are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'We are' en Italian? Noi siamo

scenario: Italian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Italian, how do you say 'You are'? {{c1::Voi siete}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Voi siete | Meaning: You are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'You are' en Italian? Voi siete

scenario: Italian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Italian, how do you say 'They are'? {{c1::Loro sono}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Loro sono | Meaning: They are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'They are' en Italian? Loro sono

text: In Italian, the subject pronouns for 'I' and 'you (singular informal)' are {{c1::io}} and {{c2::tu}}.
explanation: Italian pronouns are often omitted in sentences because the verb conjugation clearly indicates the subject.
usage: Examples: Io sono stanco. (I am tired.) | Tu sei pronto? (Are you ready?)
spanish: En italiano, los pronombres de sujeto para 'yo' y 'tú' son 'io' y 'tu'.

text: In Italian, the subject pronouns for 'he' and 'she' are {{c1::lui}} and {{c2::lei}}.
explanation: In grammatical descriptions, you might see 'egli' (he) and 'ella' (she), but in modern spoken Italian, 'lui' and 'lei' are exclusively used.
usage: Examples: Lui è bravo. (He is good.) | Lei è italiana. (She is Italian.)
spanish: En italiano, los pronombres de sujeto para 'él' y 'ella' son 'lui' y 'lei'.

text: In Italian, the subject pronoun for 'we' is {{c1::noi}}, and for 'you all (plural)' is {{c2::voi}}.
explanation: Unlike Spanish, Italian 'noi' and 'voi' do not change ending for gender (no 'nosotras' or 'vosotras').
usage: Examples: Noi siamo amici. (We are friends.) | Voi siete pronti? (Are you all ready?)
spanish: En italiano, los pronombres para 'nosotros' y 'vosotros/ustedes' son 'noi' y 'voi' (invariables en género).

text: In Italian, the subject pronoun for 'they' is {{c1::loro}}.
explanation: Used for both masculine, feminine, or mixed groups.
usage: Example: Loro sono stanchi. (They are tired.)
spanish: En italiano, el pronombre para 'ellos/ellas' es 'loro'.

text: In Italian, what is the infinitive form of the verb 'to be'? {{c1::essere}}
explanation: Essere is one of the two auxiliary verbs in Italian.
usage: Term: essere | Example: essere o non essere (to be or not to be)
spanish: ¿Cuál es el infinitivo del verbo 'ser/estar' en italiano? essere.

text: Conjugate 'essere' (to be) in the present tense for 'io' (I) and 'tu' (you): io {{c1::sono}}, tu {{c2::sei}}.
explanation: Essere present tense conjugations are highly irregular.
usage: Examples: Io sono felice. (I am happy.) | Tu sei qui. (You are here.)
spanish: Conjuga 'essere' en presente para 'yo' y 'tú': io sono, tu sei.

text: Conjugate 'essere' (to be) in the present tense for 'lui/lei' (he/she) and 'noi' (we): lui/lei {{c1::è}}, noi {{c2::siamo}}.
explanation: Note the grave accent on 'è' (is). Without the accent, 'e' means 'and'.
usage: Examples: Lui è stanco. (He is tired.) | Noi siamo a casa. (We are at home.)
spanish: Conjuga 'essere' en presente para 'él/ella' y 'nosotros': lui/lei è, noi siamo.

text: Conjugate 'essere' (to be) in the present tense for 'voi' (you all) and 'loro' (they): voi {{c1::siete}}, loro {{c2::sono}}.
explanation: Notice that 'sono' is used for both 'io' (I am) and 'loro' (they are). Context tells them apart.
usage: Examples: Voi siete italiani? (Are you all Italian?) | Loro sono stanchi. (They are tired.)
spanish: Conjuga 'essere' en presente para 'vosotros' y 'ellos': voi siete, loro sono.

text: In Italian, the polite/formal pronoun for 'you (singular)' is {{c1::Lei}}.
explanation: It is grammatically feminine (same as 'she') and conjugates with the third-person singular verb form (e.g. 'Lei è' = You are formal), but refers to both men and women.
usage: Example: Signore, Lei è molto gentile. (Sir, you are very kind.)
spanish: En italiano, el pronombre formal/cortés para 'usted' es 'Lei' (con mayúscula). Usa la tercera persona singular del verbo.

text: To make a sentence negative in Italian, you place the word {{c1::non}} directly before the verb. Example: I am not Italian = {{c2::Non sono italiano}}.
explanation: Very simple negation structure, placed right before the verb.
usage: Example: Lui non è stanco. (He is not tired.)
spanish: Para hacer una oración negativa en italiano, se coloca 'non' justo antes del verbo. 'Non sono italiano' = No soy italiano.

text: To ask a question in Italian, you do not need to change the word order or use auxiliary verbs; you simply raise your {{c1::intonation}} at the end of the sentence.
explanation: Word order remains identical to statements. Just change the pitch.
usage: Statement: Sei stanco. (You are tired.) | Question: Sei stanco? (Are you tired?)
spanish: En italiano, no es necesario cambiar el orden de las palabras para hacer preguntas; solo se eleva la entonación al final.

text: In Italian, possessive adjectives usually require a definite article. For example, 'my book' is '{{c1::il mio libro}}' and 'my house' is '{{c2::la mia casa}}'.
explanation: mio/mia are masculine/feminine. Unlike Spanish, you must include 'il/la' in front.
usage: Examples: Questo è il mio libro. (This is my book.) | La mia macchina è rossa. (My car is red.)
spanish: En italiano, los adjetivos posesivos suelen requerir un artículo definido. 'il mio libro' = mi libro.

text: In Italian, 'this' is '{{c1::questo}}' (masc) / '{{c1::questa}}' (fem) and 'that' is '{{c2::quello}}' / '{{c2::quella}}'.
explanation: These agree in gender and number with the noun they modify.
usage: Examples: questo tavolo (this table) | quella sedia (that chair)
spanish: En italiano, 'este' se dice 'questo/questa' y 'ese/aquel' se dice 'quello/quella'.

text: In Italian, adjectives must agree in gender and number. If 'He is happy' is 'Lui è felice', 'They (masc) are happy' is '{{c1::Loro sono felici}}'.
explanation: Adjectives ending in '-e' become '-i' in the plural for both masculine and feminine.
usage: Example: Le ragazze sono felici. (The girls are happy.)
spanish: En italiano, los adjetivos concuerdan en género y número. 'Lui è felice' -> 'Loro sono felici' (las palabras terminadas en -e cambian a -i).

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Pure Vowels}} is defined as: Italian vowels are never reduced to a schwa. They remain clear even in unstressed syllables.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Pure Vowels
Description: Italian vowels are never reduced to a schwa. They remain clear even in unstressed syllables.
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Pure Vowels' es: Italian vowels are never reduced to a schwa. They remain clear even in unstressed syllables.

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Mid-Vowel Contrast}} is defined as: There is a phonemic distinction between /e/ vs. /ɛ/ and /o/ vs. /ɔ/ (e.g., *pésche* "fishing" vs. *pèsche* "peaches"), though this varies by region.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Mid-Vowel Contrast
Description: There is a phonemic distinction between /e/ vs. /ɛ/ and /o/ vs. /ɔ/ (e.g., *pésche* "fishing" vs. *pèsche* "peaches"), though this varies by region.
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Mid-Vowel Contrast' es: There is a phonemic distinction between /e/ vs. /ɛ/ and /o/ vs. /ɔ/ (e.g., *pésche* "fishing" vs. *pèsche* "peaches"), though this varies by region.

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Diphthongs}} is defined as: Common combinations like /ai/, /au/, /ei/, /eu/.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Diphthongs
Description: Common combinations like /ai/, /au/, /ei/, /eu/.
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Diphthongs' es: Common combinations like /ai/, /au/, /ei/, /eu/.

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Gemination}} is defined as: Double consonants are significantly longer and change the word's meaning (e.g., *pala* "shovel" vs. *palla* "ball").
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Gemination
Description: Double consonants are significantly longer and change the word's meaning (e.g., *pala* "shovel" vs. *palla* "ball").
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Gemination' es: Double consonants are significantly longer and change the word's meaning (e.g., *pala* "shovel" vs. *palla* "ball").

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Palatal Sounds}} is defined as: Italian features common palatal sounds like /ɲ/ (written *gn*) and /ʎ/ (written *gli*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Palatal Sounds
Description: Italian features common palatal sounds like /ɲ/ (written *gn*) and /ʎ/ (written *gli*).
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Palatal Sounds' es: Italian features common palatal sounds like /ɲ/ (written *gn*) and /ʎ/ (written *gli*).

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Raddoppiamento Fonosintattico}} is defined as: A rule where the initial consonant of a word is doubled in pronunciation after certain words ending in a stressed vowel, monosyllables, or specific structural words:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Raddoppiamento Fonosintattico
Description: A rule where the initial consonant of a word is doubled in pronunciation after certain words ending in a stressed vowel, monosyllables, or specific structural words:
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Raddoppiamento Fonosintattico' es: A rule where the initial consonant of a word is doubled in pronunciation after certain words ending in a stressed vowel, monosyllables, or specific structural words:

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Elision}} is defined as: Dropping the final unstressed vowel of a word before another word starting with a vowel, marked with an apostrophe (e.g., *l'amico* instead of *lo amico*, *quest'anno* instead of *questo anno*, *un'ora* instead of *una ora*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Elision
Description: Dropping the final unstressed vowel of a word before another word starting with a vowel, marked with an apostrophe (e.g., *l'amico* instead of *lo amico*, *quest'anno* instead of *questo anno*, *un'ora* instead of *una ora*).
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Elision' es: Dropping the final unstressed vowel of a word before another word starting with a vowel, marked with an apostrophe (e.g., *l'amico* instead of *lo amico*, *quest'anno* instead of *questo anno*, *un'ora* instead of *una ora*).

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Apocope (Troncamento)}} is defined as: Dropping the final vowel or syllable before consonant-initial words under specific conditions, without an apostrophe (e.g., *buon giorno* instead of *buono giorno*, *parlar chiaro* instead of *parlare chiaro*, *dottor Rossi* instead of *dottore Rossi*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Apocope (Troncamento)
Description: Dropping the final vowel or syllable before consonant-initial words under specific conditions, without an apostrophe (e.g., *buon giorno* instead of *buono giorno*, *parlar chiaro* instead of *parlare chiaro*, *dottor Rossi* instead of *dottore Rossi*).
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Apocope (Troncamento)' es: Dropping the final vowel or syllable before consonant-initial words under specific conditions, without an apostrophe (e.g., *buon giorno* instead of *buono giorno*, *parlar chiaro* instead of *parlare chiaro*, *dottor Rossi* instead of *dottore Rossi*).

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Syllable-timed Rhythm}} is defined as: Maintain even tempo on each syllable. Do not reduce vowels to a schwa [ə]; even unstressed final vowels must remain pure and fully voiced.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Syllable-timed Rhythm
Description: Maintain even tempo on each syllable. Do not reduce vowels to a schwa [ə]; even unstressed final vowels must remain pure and fully voiced.
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Syllable-timed Rhythm' es: Maintain even tempo on each syllable. Do not reduce vowels to a schwa [ə]; even unstressed final vowels must remain pure and fully voiced.

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Word Blending}} is defined as: In rapid spoken speech, vowel sequences between adjacent words are glided together rather than separated by pauses (e.g., *come era* -> "com'era" [koˈmɛːra], *dove sei* -> "do' sei" [doˈsɛj] in informal regional accents).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Word Blending
Description: In rapid spoken speech, vowel sequences between adjacent words are glided together rather than separated by pauses (e.g., *come era* -> "com'era" [koˈmɛːra], *dove sei* -> "do' sei" [doˈsɛj] in informal regional accents).
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Word Blending' es: In rapid spoken speech, vowel sequences between adjacent words are glided together rather than separated by pauses (e.g., *come era* -> "com'era" [koˈmɛːra], *dove sei* -> "do' sei" [doˈsɛj] in informal regional accents).

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Word Stress}} is defined as: Usually falls on the penultimate syllable (**Paro**-la). Words stressed on the last syllable (*Città*) always have a written accent.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Word Stress
Description: Usually falls on the penultimate syllable (**Paro**-la). Words stressed on the last syllable (*Città*) always have a written accent.
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Word Stress' es: Usually falls on the penultimate syllable (**Paro**-la). Words stressed on the last syllable (*Città*) always have a written accent.

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Intonation}} is defined as: Italian has a melodic, undulating intonation pattern.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Intonation
Description: Italian has a melodic, undulating intonation pattern.
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Intonation' es: Italian has a melodic, undulating intonation pattern.

text: What is the Italian pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Raddoppiamento Pro-Tip}}? To sound natural and fluent, you must practice the "Raddoppiamento Fonosintattico". When saying "va bene", join the words and hold the 'b' sound: "vahb-behneh". This is the hallmark of correct phonetic phrasing in Italian.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Italian.
usage:
Tip: Raddoppiamento Pro-Tip
To sound natural and fluent, you must practice the "Raddoppiamento Fonosintattico". When saying "va bene", join the words and hold the 'b' sound: "vahb-behneh". This is the hallmark of correct phonetic phrasing in Italian.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Italian para 'Raddoppiamento Pro-Tip'? To sound natural and fluent, you must practice the "Raddoppiamento Fonosintattico". When saying "va bene", join the words and hold the 'b' sound: "vahb-behneh". This is the hallmark of correct phonetic phrasing in Italian.

text: What is the Italian pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Vowel Purity Rule}}? Never turn unstressed final vowels into the lazy English schwa sound. "Pizza" must end with a pure Spanish/Italian "ah" [a], never "pitz-uh" [ə].
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Italian.
usage:
Tip: Vowel Purity Rule
Never turn unstressed final vowels into the lazy English schwa sound. "Pizza" must end with a pure Spanish/Italian "ah" [a], never "pitz-uh" [ə].
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Italian para 'Vowel Purity Rule'? Never turn unstressed final vowels into the lazy English schwa sound. "Pizza" must end with a pure Spanish/Italian "ah" [a], never "pitz-uh" [ə].

text: What is the Italian pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Double Consonants}}? To pronounce double consonants correctly, hold the position of the consonant for a split second longer before releasing.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Italian.
usage:
Tip: Double Consonants
To pronounce double consonants correctly, hold the position of the consonant for a split second longer before releasing.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Italian para 'Double Consonants'? To pronounce double consonants correctly, hold the position of the consonant for a split second longer before releasing.

text: In Italian, the letter 'c' is pronounced as a soft 'ch' sound (/tʃ/) before {{c1::e and i}} (e.g., *ciao*, *cena*), and as a hard 'k' sound (/k/) before {{c2::a, o, and u}} (e.g., *casa*, *cosa*).
explanation: The pronunciation of 'c' depends strictly on the vowel that follows it.
usage: Examples: ciao (/tʃa.o/), cena (/tʃe.na/) | casa (/ka.sa/), colore (/ko.lo.re/)
spanish: En italiano, la letra 'c' suena como 'ch' antes de 'e, i', y como 'k' antes de 'a, o, u'.

text: In Italian, the letter 'g' is pronounced as a soft 'j' sound (/dʒ/) before {{c1::e and i}} (e.g., *gelato*, *giorno*), and as a hard 'g' sound (/ɡ/) before {{c2::a, o, and u}} (e.g., *gatto*, *gonna*).
explanation: Similar to 'c', 'g' alternates between soft and hard depending on the vowel.
usage: Examples: gelato (/dʒe.la.to/) | gatto (/ɡat.to/)
spanish: En italiano, la letra 'g' suena como la 'j' en inglés antes de 'e, i', y como 'g' suave antes de 'a, o, u'.

text: In Italian, to make a 'c' or 'g' sound hard before 'e' or 'i', the silent letter {{c1::h}} is inserted (e.g., *chiave* -> /k/, *margherita* -> /g/).
explanation: The 'h' serves as a phonetic modifier to keep the consonant sound hard.
usage: Examples: chiave (/kja.ve/ - key) | spaghetti (/spa.ɡet.ti/)
spanish: En italiano, para que la 'c' o 'g' tengan un sonido fuerte ante 'e, i', se introduce una 'h' muda (ej. 'chiave', 'spaghetti').

text: In Italian, the consonant cluster 'gli' is pronounced as a palatal lateral approximant {{c1::/ʎ/}}, which is similar to the 'lli' sound in English 'million' or the Spanish 'll' in 'calle'.
explanation: This sound is produced by placing the flat of the tongue against the hard palate.
usage: Examples: figlio (/fi.ʎo/ - son) | famiglia (/fa.mi.ʎa/ - family)
spanish: En italiano, la combinación 'gli' se pronuncia como una 'll' húmeda española (/ʎ/) (ej. 'figlio', 'famiglia').

text: In Italian, double consonants (like the 'tt' in *fatto* or 'nn' in *anno*) must be pronounced {{c1::longer / held longer}} than single consonants.
explanation: Italian is a geminate language; double consonants affect word meaning (e.g. *casa* /house/ vs *cassa* /cashbox/).
usage: Compare: capello (hair) vs cappello (hat). Hold the consonant closure longer for double letters.
spanish: En italiano, las consonantes dobles deben pronunciarse de forma sostenida o más larga que las simples. Afecta el significado de las palabras.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Andiamo a casa per un caffè espresso."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Andiamo a-ccasa per un caffè m-espresso.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Raddoppiamento Fonosintattico
The stressed vowels in 'a' and 'caffè' trigger double pronunciation of the next word's initial consonant.
usage: Formal: Andiamo a casa per un caffè espresso. → Connected: Andiamo a-ccasa per un caffè m-espresso.
spanish: Spanish: Vamos a casa por un café expreso.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Buon giorno, un poco di pane, per favore."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Buongiorno, un po' di pane, per favore.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Troncamento
Vowels at the end of 'buono' and 'poco' are dropped, creating shorter, more fluid phrases.
usage: Formal: Buon giorno, un poco di pane, per favore. → Connected: Buongiorno, un po' di pane, per favore.
spanish: Spanish: Buenos días, un poco de pan, por favor.

text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Good morning'? {{c1::おはようございます (Ohayō gozaimasu)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: おはようございます (Ohayō gozaimasu) | Meaning: Good morning
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good morning' en Japanese? おはようございます (Ohayō gozaimasu)

text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Hello / Good afternoon'? {{c1::こんにちは (Konnichiwa)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: こんにちは (Konnichiwa) | Meaning: Hello / Good afternoon
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hello / Good afternoon' en Japanese? こんにちは (Konnichiwa)

text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Good evening'? {{c1::こんばんは (Konbanwa)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: こんばんは (Konbanwa) | Meaning: Good evening
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good evening' en Japanese? こんばんは (Konbanwa)

text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Goodbye'? {{c1::さようなら (Sayōnara)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: さようなら (Sayōnara) | Meaning: Goodbye
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Goodbye' en Japanese? さようなら (Sayōnara)

text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Nice to meet you'? {{c1::はじめまして (Hajimemashite)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: はじめまして (Hajimemashite) | Meaning: Nice to meet you
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Nice to meet you' en Japanese? はじめまして (Hajimemashite)

text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Good night'? {{c1::おやすみなさい (Oyasumi nasai)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: おやすみなさい (Oyasumi nasai) | Meaning: Good night
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good night' en Japanese? おやすみなさい (Oyasumi nasai)

text: In Japanese, how do you say 'See you / Bye - Informal'? {{c1::じゃあね (Jā ne)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: じゃあね (Jā ne) | Meaning: See you / Bye - Informal
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'See you / Bye - Informal' en Japanese? じゃあね (Jā ne)

text: In Japanese, the general greeting 'Hello' used in the afternoon is '{{c1::こんにちは}}' (Konnichiwa).
explanation: Note that the final 'wa' sound is written with the hiragana 'ha' (は) because it historically starts a topic clause.
usage: Term: こんにちは (Konnichiwa) | Use: Day greeting.
spanish: En japonés, el saludo general 'Hola' en la tarde es 'こんにちは' (Konnichiwa).

text: In Japanese, the polite way to say 'Good morning' is '{{c1::おはようございます}}' (Ohayou gozaimasu).
explanation: Informally (with friends or family), you can simply say 'おはよう' (Ohayou).
usage: Polite: おはようございます | Informal: おはよう
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenos días' formalmente en japonés? おはようございます (Ohayou gozaimasu).

text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Good evening'? {{c1::こんばんは}} (Konbanwa)
explanation: Like Konnichiwa, the final 'wa' sound is written with the topic marker particle 'ha' (は).
usage: Term: こんばんは (Konbanwa)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenas noches (al llegar)' en japonés? こんばんは (Konbanwa).

text: In Japanese, the polite way to say 'Good night' (when going to bed) is '{{c1::おやすみなさい}}' (Oyasuminasai).
explanation: Informally, you can say 'おやすみ' (Oyasumi).
usage: Polite: おやすみなさい | Informal: おやすみ
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenas noches (al despedirse o ir a dormir)' en japonés? おやすみなさい (Oyasuminasai).

text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Goodbye' formally (implying a long separation)? {{c1::さようなら}} (Sayounara)
explanation: Often avoided with coworkers or daily contacts because it implies a final or long farewell. Classrooms and friends use shorter, informal words.
usage: Term: さようなら | Informal alternatives: またね (See you), じゃあね (Bye).
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Adiós' formalmente en japonés? さようなら (Sayounara).

text: In Japanese, when meeting someone for the first time, you start with the greeting '{{c1::はじめまして}}' (Hajimemashite), which means 'Nice to meet you'.
explanation: Derived from the verb 'hajimeru' (to begin). Used strictly at the very beginning of a self-introduction.
usage: Term: はじめまして (Hajimemashite)
spanish: Al presentarse en japonés, se empieza con 'はじめまして' (Hajimemashite), que significa 'Encantado de conocerle'.

text: In Japanese, at the end of a self-introduction, you say '{{c1::どうぞよろしくおねがいします}}' (Douzo yoroshiku onegaishimasu), which translates to 'Please be kind to me'.
explanation: An essential cultural phrase showing respect and requesting a good relationship.
usage: Term: どうぞよろしくおねがいします | Casual: よろしく
spanish: Al final de una presentación en japonés, se dice 'どうぞよろしくおねがいします' (Douzo yoroshiku onegaishimasu), que equivale a 'Mucho gusto / Cuento con usted'.

text: In Japanese, the polite way to say 'Please' (when asking for an action or object) is '{{c1::おねがいします}}' (Onegaishimasu) or '{{c2::ください}}' (Kudasai).
explanation: Onegaishimasu is more formal and can be used on its own. Kudasai is appended to items or verbs in the -te form.
usage: Examples: お水をお願いします。 (Water, please.) | これ、ください。 (This one, please.)
spanish: En japonés, para decir 'Por favor', se usa 'おねがいします' (Onegaishimasu) o 'ください' (Kudasai).

text: In Japanese, the polite way to say 'Thank you very much' is '{{c1::ありがとうございます}}' (Arigatou gozaimasu).
explanation: Informally (with close peers), you can say just 'ありがとう' (Arigatou) or even 'どうも' (Doumo).
usage: Polite: ありがとうございます | Informal: ありがとう
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Muchas gracias' en japonés? ありがとうございます (Arigatou gozaimasu).

text: In Japanese, how do you say 'You're welcome'? {{c1::どういたしまして}} (Douitashimashite)
explanation: The standard textbook response to 'Arigatou'. In natural conversation, speakers often say 'いいえ' (Iie - No problem).
usage: Term: どういたしまして
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'De nada' en japonés? どういたしまして (Douitashimashite).

text: In Japanese, how do you ask 'What is your name?' politely? {{c1::お名前は何ですか}} (O-namae wa nan desu ka)
explanation: The prefix 'o-' is honorific, expressing respect towards the other person.
usage: Dialogue: - お名前は何ですか。 - ジーザスです。
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cuál es su nombre?' en japonés? お名前は何ですか (O-namae wa nan desu ka).

text: In Japanese, how do you ask 'How are you? / Are you well?'? {{c1::お元気ですか}} (O-genki desu ka)
explanation: Used when you haven't seen someone for a while. Not used daily like 'how are you' in English.
usage: Dialogue: - お元気ですか。 - はい、元気です。
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cómo estás? / ¿Está usted bien?' en japonés? お元気ですか (O-genki desu ka).

text: In Japanese, '{{c1::すみません}}' (Sumimasen) is a highly useful word that means {{c2::Excuse me / Sorry / Thank you}}.
explanation: Used to get attention, apologize, or thank someone for going to trouble for you.
usage: Example: すみません、ビルはどこですか？ (Excuse me, where is the building?)
spanish: En japonés, 'すみません' (Sumimasen) significa 'Disculpe / Lo siento / Gracias' según el contexto.

scenario: Japanese A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Japanese, how do you say 'To be - Polite'? {{c1::です (desu)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: です (desu) | Meaning: To be - Polite
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'To be - Polite' en Japanese? です (desu)

scenario: Japanese A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Japanese, how do you say 'To not be - Polite'? {{c1::ではありません (dewa arimasen)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: ではありません (dewa arimasen) | Meaning: To not be - Polite
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'To not be - Polite' en Japanese? ではありません (dewa arimasen)

scenario: Japanese A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Is it?'? {{c1::ですか (desu ka?)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: ですか (desu ka?) | Meaning: Is it?
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Is it?' en Japanese? ですか (desu ka?)

scenario: Japanese A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Japanese, how do you say 'I am a student'? {{c1::学生です (gakusei desu)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: 学生です (gakusei desu) | Meaning: I am a student
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'I am a student' en Japanese? 学生です (gakusei desu)

text: In Japanese, the standard polite word for 'I' is '{{c1::わたし}}' (Watashi).
explanation: In casual speech, males often use 'ぼく' (Boku) or 'おれ' (Ore). Females often use 'わたし' or 'あたし'.
usage: Polite: わたしはエンジニアです。 (I am an engineer.)
spanish: En japonés, la palabra estándar y cortés para 'yo' es 'わたし' (Watashi).

text: In Japanese, the word for 'you (singular)' is '{{c1::あなた}}' (Anata).
explanation: In Japanese culture, it is polite to address people by their name + '-san' rather than saying 'Anata' (which can sound overly direct or mean 'dear' when said by a spouse).
usage: Rule: Prefer name + さん (san) over あなた. Example: マルコさんは学生ですか？ (Is Marco a student?)
spanish: En japonés, 'tú' es 'あなた' (Anata), pero suele preferirse el nombre de la persona + 'san'.

text: In Japanese, the pronouns for 'he' and 'she' are '{{c1::かれ}}' (Kare) and '{{c2::かのじょ}}' (Kanojo).
explanation: 'Kanojo' also means 'girlfriend' and 'Kare' can mean 'boyfriend' depending on context.
usage: Examples: 彼は先生です。 (He is a teacher.) | 彼女は元気です。 (She is well.)
spanish: En japonés, los pronombres para 'él' y 'ella' son 'かれ' (Kare) y 'かのじょ' (Kanojo).

text: In Japanese, to make a pronoun plural, you add the suffix '{{c1::たち}}' (-tachi). For example, 'we' is '{{c2::わたしたち}}' (Watashitachi).
explanation: -tachi can be appended to personal pronouns or nouns representing people.
usage: Example: わたしたちは学生です。 (We are students.) | あなたたち (You all)
spanish: En japonés, para hacer plural un pronombre, se añade '-tachi'. 'Watashi' (yo) -> 'Watashitachi' (nosotros).

text: In Japanese, the polite copula meaning 'is / am / are' is '{{c1::です}}' (desu). Example: I am a student = 学生{{c2::です}}.
explanation: 'desu' is placed at the end of the sentence and serves as the predicate of state or identity.
usage: Usage: Place at end of noun/adjective sentences. 本です。 (It is a book.)
spanish: En japonés, la cópula cortés que equivale a 'ser/estar' es 'です' (desu). Se ubica al final.

text: In Japanese, the polite negative form of 'desu' (am not / is not / are not) is '{{c1::ではありません}}' (de wa arimasen) or casually '{{c2::じゃないです}}' (ja nai desu).
explanation: 'de wa arimasen' is formal written/spoken. 'ja nai desu' is common spoken.
usage: Formal: 学生ではありません。 | Casual: 学生じゃないです。 (I am not a student.)
spanish: En japonés, la forma negativa cortés de 'desu' es 'ではありません' (de wa arimasen) o 'じゃないです' (ja nai desu).

text: To make a question in Japanese, you add the question particle '{{c1::か}}' (ka) at the very end of the sentence.
explanation: Acts like a spoken question mark. You do not invert subject/verb or add auxiliary verbs.
usage: Example: 学生ですか？ (Are you a student?) | そうですか。 (Is that so?)
spanish: En japonés, las preguntas se forman añadiendo la partícula 'か' (ka) al final de la oración.

text: In Japanese, the topic marker particle is '{{c1::は}}' (pronounced 'wa'). Example: わたし{{c2::は}}学生です (As for me, I am a student).
explanation: Written with the character for 'ha' but pronounced as 'wa' when used as a particle.
usage: Rule: Subject/Topic + は + Predicate. Example: これは何ですか？ (What is this?)
spanish: En japonés, la partícula marcadora de tema es 'は' (se pronuncia 'wa').

text: In Japanese, the direct object marker particle is '{{c1::を}}' (pronounced 'o').
explanation: Written with 'wo' (を) but normally pronounced 'o'. It marks the target of transitive actions.
usage: Example: 水を飲みます。 (I drink water. [mizu = water, nomimasu = drink])
spanish: En japonés, la partícula marcadora de objeto directo es 'を' (se pronuncia 'o').

text: In Japanese, the possessive particle that acts like English apostrophe 's or 'of' is '{{c1::の}}' (no). Example: My book = わたし{{c2::の}}本.
explanation: Connects nouns. Noun 1 + の + Noun 2 -> Noun 2 belongs to Noun 1.
usage: Example: 日本のカメラ (Japanese camera) | 先生の本 (The teacher's book)
spanish: En japonés, la partícula posesiva o modificadora de sustantivos es 'の' (no). わたしの本 = Mi libro.

text: In Japanese, the words for 'this', 'that' (near listener), and 'that over there' (far from both) are '{{c1::これ}}' (Kore), '{{c2::それ}}' (Sore), and '{{c3::あれ}}' (Are).
explanation: These are demonstrative pronouns. The corresponding demonstrative adjectives (followed by a noun) are kono, sono, ano.
usage: Pronoun: これはペンです。 (This is a pen.) | Adjective: このペンはわたしのです。 (This pen is mine.)
spanish: En japonés: 'これ' (esto), 'それ' (eso) y 'あれ' (aquello).

text: In Japanese, what is the polite phrase to say 'My name is [Name]' using the copula? '[Name] {{c1::です}}' or '[Name] {{c2::と申します}}' (more formal).
explanation: Simply putting 'desu' after your name is the most common self-introduction. 'To moushimasu' translates to 'I am called...'.
usage: Example: ジーザスです。 (I am Jesus.)
spanish: Para decir 'Mi nombre es...' en japonés de forma sencilla: '[Nombre] です' o '[Nombre] と申します' (humilde/formal).

text: In Japanese, the particle meaning 'also' or 'too' is '{{c1::も}}' (mo), which replaces 'wa' or 'ga'. Example: Me too = わたし{{c2::も}}.
explanation: Identical in meaning to Spanish 'también'.
usage: Example: 彼も学生です。 (He is also a student.)
spanish: En japonés, la partícula que significa 'también' es 'も' (mo), la cual reemplaza a 'wa'.

text: In Japanese, the question word for 'What' is '{{c1::何}}' (pronounced 'nani' or 'nan').
explanation: 'nan' is used before 'desu' or counter words. 'nani' is used in other cases.
usage: Examples: 何ですか？ (What is it?) | 何を食べますか？ (What will you eat?)
spanish: En japonés, la palabra interrogativa para '¿Qué?' es '何' (nani / nan).

text: In Japanese, the question word for 'Who' is '{{c1::だれ}}' (Dare) or politely '{{c2::どなた}}' (Donata).
explanation: 'Donata' is used in business or formal situations.
usage: Example: あの人は誰ですか？ (Who is that person?)
spanish: En japonés, la palabra interrogativa para '¿Quién?' es 'だれ' (Dare) o formalmente 'どなた' (Donata).

text: In Japanese, what is the polite phrase meaning 'Nice to meet you' when exchanging names? {{c1::よろしくおねがいします}} (Yoroshiku onegaishimasu).
explanation: Literally: 'Please treat me well.' It's an indispensable greeting in self-introductions.
usage: Dialogue: - ジーザスです。どうぞよろしく。 (- I am Jesus. Nice to meet you.)
spanish: En japonés, la frase clave para cerrar una presentación y decir 'Un placer' es 'よろしくおねがいします'.

text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Devoicing}} is defined as: /i/ and /u/ are often devoiced when between two voiceless consonants or at the end of a phrase (e.g., *desu* [desɯ̥]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
usage:
Rule: Vowel Devoicing
Description: /i/ and /u/ are often devoiced when between two voiceless consonants or at the end of a phrase (e.g., *desu* [desɯ̥]).
spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'Vowel Devoicing' es: /i/ and /u/ are often devoiced when between two voiceless consonants or at the end of a phrase (e.g., *desu* [desɯ̥]).

text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Length}} is defined as: Japanese distinguishes between short and long vowels (e.g., *ojisan* "uncle" vs *ojiisan* "grandfather").
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
usage:
Rule: Vowel Length
Description: Japanese distinguishes between short and long vowels (e.g., *ojisan* "uncle" vs *ojiisan* "grandfather").
spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'Vowel Length' es: Japanese distinguishes between short and long vowels (e.g., *ojisan* "uncle" vs *ojiisan* "grandfather").

text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::No Diphthongs}} is defined as: Sequences of vowels are usually treated as separate moras.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
usage:
Rule: No Diphthongs
Description: Sequences of vowels are usually treated as separate moras.
spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'No Diphthongs' es: Sequences of vowels are usually treated as separate moras.

text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Moraic Nasal (ん)}} is defined as: /ɴ/ changes its pronunciation depending on the following sound (e.g., [m] before /p, b, m/, [ŋ] before /k, ɡ/).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
usage:
Rule: The Moraic Nasal (ん)
Description: /ɴ/ changes its pronunciation depending on the following sound (e.g., [m] before /p, b, m/, [ŋ] before /k, ɡ/).
spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'The Moraic Nasal (ん)' es: /ɴ/ changes its pronunciation depending on the following sound (e.g., [m] before /p, b, m/, [ŋ] before /k, ɡ/).

text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Japanese 'R'}} is defined as: An alveolar tap /ɾ/, somewhere between an English 'd' and 'l'.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
usage:
Rule: The Japanese 'R'
Description: An alveolar tap /ɾ/, somewhere between an English 'd' and 'l'.
spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'The Japanese 'R'' es: An alveolar tap /ɾ/, somewhere between an English 'd' and 'l'.

text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Gemination (っ)}} is defined as: Double consonants (e.g., *matte*) count as a full mora and involve a distinct pause or holding of the consonant position.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
usage:
Rule: Gemination (っ)
Description: Double consonants (e.g., *matte*) count as a full mora and involve a distinct pause or holding of the consonant position.
spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'Gemination (っ)' es: Double consonants (e.g., *matte*) count as a full mora and involve a distinct pause or holding of the consonant position.

text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Mora-timed Rhythm}} is defined as: Keep a steady tempo where each sound unit (mora), including the double consonant っ (Q) and the nasal ん (N), receives exactly one beat of time (e.g., *kitte* [ki.t.te] has 3 moras; *shinkansen* has 5).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
usage:
Rule: Mora-timed Rhythm
Description: Keep a steady tempo where each sound unit (mora), including the double consonant っ (Q) and the nasal ん (N), receives exactly one beat of time (e.g., *kitte* [ki.t.te] has 3 moras; *shinkansen* has 5).
spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'Mora-timed Rhythm' es: Keep a steady tempo where each sound unit (mora), including the double consonant っ (Q) and the nasal ん (N), receives exactly one beat of time (e.g., *kitte* [ki.t.te] has 3 moras; *shinkansen* has 5).

text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Devoicing}} is defined as: Vowels /i/ and /u/ lose their voicing between voiceless consonants (/p, t, k, s, h, ch, sh/) and at the end of statements after a voiceless consonant (e.g., *desu* -> [desɯ̥], *sukiyaki* -> [sɯ̥kijaki], *shitsurei* -> [ɕi̥tsɯɾeː]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
usage:
Rule: Vowel Devoicing
Description: Vowels /i/ and /u/ lose their voicing between voiceless consonants (/p, t, k, s, h, ch, sh/) and at the end of statements after a voiceless consonant (e.g., *desu* -> [desɯ̥], *sukiyaki* -> [sɯ̥kijaki], *shitsurei* -> [ɕi̥tsɯɾeː]).
spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'Vowel Devoicing' es: Vowels /i/ and /u/ lose their voicing between voiceless consonants (/p, t, k, s, h, ch, sh/) and at the end of statements after a voiceless consonant (e.g., *desu* -> [desɯ̥], *sukiyaki* -> [sɯ̥kijaki], *shitsurei* -> [ɕi̥tsɯɾeː]).

text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Casual Contractions}} is defined as: Everyday spoken Japanese uses major contractions:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
usage:
Rule: Casual Contractions
Description: Everyday spoken Japanese uses major contractions:
spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'Casual Contractions' es: Everyday spoken Japanese uses major contractions:

text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::~de shimau}} is defined as: > **~jau** (e.g., *ninde shimau* -> "ninjau").
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
usage:
Rule: ~de shimau

text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Pitch Accent}} is defined as: Unlike English stress accent, Japanese uses changes in pitch (High vs. Low).

Rule: Pitch Accent
Description: Unlike English stress accent, Japanese uses changes in pitch (High vs. Low).

Description: - **Heiban** (Flat): Pitch rises and stays high.

text: What is the Japanese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "Desu" Whisper}}? When pronouncing "desu" or "masu", do not voice the final "u". Whisper it, or omit it completely: say "dess" and "mass".
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Japanese.
usage:
Tip: The "Desu" Whisper
When pronouncing "desu" or "masu", do not voice the final "u". Whisper it, or omit it completely: say "dess" and "mass".
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Japanese para 'The "Desu" Whisper'? When pronouncing "desu" or "masu", do not voice the final "u". Whisper it, or omit it completely: say "dess" and "mass".

text: What is the Japanese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Everyday Contraction Rule}}? If you are speaking casually, never use full form "te iru" or "te shimau". Use "shitteru" instead of "shitte iru" and "tabechau" instead of "tabete shimau" to sound completely native.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Japanese.
usage:
Tip: Everyday Contraction Rule
If you are speaking casually, never use full form "te iru" or "te shimau". Use "shitteru" instead of "shitte iru" and "tabechau" instead of "tabete shimau" to sound completely native.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Japanese para 'Everyday Contraction Rule'? If you are speaking casually, never use full form "te iru" or "te shimau". Use "shitteru" instead of "shitte iru" and "tabechau" instead of "tabete shimau" to sound completely native.

text: What is the Japanese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Tip}}? Think of Japanese as a drum beat; every character (mora) gets one beat of time.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Japanese.
usage:
Tip: Tip
Think of Japanese as a drum beat; every character (mora) gets one beat of time.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Japanese para 'Tip'? Think of Japanese as a drum beat; every character (mora) gets one beat of time.

text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Devoicing (無声化)}} is defined as: High vowels /i/ and /u/ lose their voicing between voiceless consonants or at the end of a sentence following a voiceless consonant (e.g., *desu* → "des'", *suki* → "s-ki").
explanation: Devoicing occurs because the vocal cords do not vibrate during the transition between two voiceless consonants (like /s/ and /k/), silencing the high vowels.
usage:
Rule: Vowel Devoicing (無声化)
Examples:
  • desu (to be) → "des'"
  • suki (like) → "s-ki"
  • shita (under) → "sh-ta"
spanish: Dessonorización de Vocales 'U' e 'I': Las vocales altas pierden su sonoridad cuando están entre consonantes sordas o al final de frase tras consonante sorda. Ejemplo: "desu" → "des'".

text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Sokuon (促音)}} is defined as: The small *tsu* (っ) represents a double consonant, causing a one-mora pause (air blockage) before pronouncing the next sorda consonant (e.g., *matte* → [maʔte]).
explanation: The Sokuon represents a geminate consonant that takes exactly one mora (unit of time) of silence, during which the mouth prepares the shape for the next consonant.
usage:
Rule: Sokuon (促音)
Examples:
  • matte (wait) → "ma [pause] te"
  • gakkou (school) → "ga [pause] kou"
spanish: Sokuon (っ): La pequeña tsu representa una pausa de una mora (detención de aire) antes de una consonante sorda. Ejemplo: "matte" → "ma-tte".

text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Colloquial Contractions}} is defined as: High-frequency verb endings compress in casual fast speech (e.g., *~te shimau* → "~tchau", *~de wa* → "~ja").
explanation: Common grammaticized auxiliaries contract heavily in casual speed: the glide /j/ fuses with preceding dental consonants.
usage:
Rule: Colloquial Contractions
Examples:
  • tabete shimau → "tabetchau"
  • de wa nai → "ja nai"
  • te oku → "toku"
spanish: Contracciones Coloquiales: Reducciones extremas de terminaciones de verbos en el habla rápida informal. Ejemplo: "~te shimau" → "~tchau".

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "好きです (Suki desu)."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::S-ki des'.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Vowel Devoicing
High vowels /u/ and /i/ lose their voicing between voiceless consonants /s/, /k/, /t/.
usage: Formal: 好きです (Suki desu). → Connected: S-ki des'.
spanish: Spanish: Me gusta / Es así.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "ちょっと待ってください (Chotto matte kudasai)."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Chotto ma[pause]te kudasai.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Sokuon (促音)
The small tsu represents a one-mora silent pause, holding the breath before the /t/ sound.
usage: Formal: ちょっと待ってください (Chotto matte kudasai). → Connected: Chotto ma[pause]te kudasai.
spanish: Spanish: Espere un momento, por favor.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "食べてしまった (Tabete shimatta)."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Tabetchatta.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Colloquial Contractions
The auxiliary verb ending '-te shimatta' fuses and contracts into '-tchatta' in casual speech.
usage: Formal: 食べてしまった (Tabete shimatta). → Connected: Tabetchatta.
spanish: Spanish: Terminé comiéndolo.

text: In Korean, how do you say 'Hello - Polite'? {{c1::안녕하세요 (Annyeong-haseyo)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: 안녕하세요 (Annyeong-haseyo) | Meaning: Hello - Polite
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hello - Polite' en Korean? 안녕하세요 (Annyeong-haseyo)

text: In Korean, how do you say 'Hi / Bye - Informal'? {{c1::안녕 (Annyeong)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: 안녕 (Annyeong) | Meaning: Hi / Bye - Informal
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hi / Bye - Informal' en Korean? 안녕 (Annyeong)

text: In Korean, how do you say 'Nice to meet you'? {{c1::만나서 반갑습니다 (Mannaseo bangapseumnida)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: 만나서 반갑습니다 (Mannaseo bangapseumnida) | Meaning: Nice to meet you
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Nice to meet you' en Korean? 만나서 반갑습니다 (Mannaseo bangapseumnida)

text: In Korean, how do you say 'Goodbye - To person staying'? {{c1::안녕히 계세요 (Annyeonghi gyeseyo)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: 안녕히 계세요 (Annyeonghi gyeseyo) | Meaning: Goodbye - To person staying

explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: 안녕히 가세요 (Annyeonghi gaseyo) | Meaning: Goodbye - To person leaving
