explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: 잘 자요 (Jal jayo) | Meaning: Good night - Polite
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good night - Polite' en Korean? 잘 자요 (Jal jayo)

text: In Korean, how do you say 'Did you eat? / Hello'? {{c1::밥 먹었어? (Bap meogeosseo?)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: 밥 먹었어? (Bap meogeosseo?) | Meaning: Did you eat? / Hello
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Did you eat? / Hello' en Korean? 밥 먹었어? (Bap meogeosseo?)

text: In Korean, the standard polite greeting 'Hello' is '{{c1::안녕하세요}}' (Annyeonghaseyo).
explanation: This greeting is in the polite 'haeyo' style. Informally, among close friends, you say '안녕' (Annyeong).
usage: Polite: 안녕하세요 | Informal: 안녕
spanish: En coreano, el saludo estándar 'Hola' en tono cortés es '안녕하세요' (Annyeonghaseyo).

text: In Korean, if you are LEAVING and the other person is STAYING, you say: '{{c1::안녕히 계세요}}' (Annyeonghi gyeseyo).
explanation: Literally: 'Please stay peacefully.' '계시다' (gyesida) is the honorific verb for 'to stay'.
usage: Term: 안녕히 계세요
spanish: En coreano, si te VAS y la otra persona se QUEDA, dices: '안녕히 계세요' (Annyeonghi gyeseyo).

text: In Korean, if you are STAYING (or both of you are leaving) and the other person is GOING, you say: '{{c1::안녕히 가세요}}' (Annyeonghi gaseyo).
explanation: Literally: 'Please go peacefully.' '가다' (gada) is the verb 'to go'.
usage: Term: 안녕히 가세요
spanish: En coreano, si te QUEDAS y la otra persona se VA, dices: '안녕히 가세요' (Annyeonghi gaseyo).

text: In Korean, the standard polite way to say 'Thank you' is '{{c1::감사합니다}}' (Gamsahabnida) or '{{c2::고맙습니다}}' (Gomapseubnida).
explanation: Both are polite formal (하십시오체) endings. 'Gamsahabnida' is Sino-Korean and slightly more formal.
usage: Polite: 감사합니다 | Casual: 고마워 (Thanks)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Gracias' formalmente en coreano? 감사합니다 (Gamsahabnida) / 고맙습니다 (Gomapseubnida).

text: In Korean, the polite way to say 'Sorry / I apologize' is '{{c1::죄송합니다}}' (Joesonghabnida) or '{{c2::미안합니다}}' (Mianhabnida).
explanation: 'Joesonghabnida' is higher respect and used with superiors/strangers.
usage: Polite: 죄송합니다 | Casual: 미안해 (Sorry)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Lo siento' formalmente en coreano? 죄송합니다 (Joesonghabnida) / 미안합니다 (Mianhabnida).

text: In Korean, how do you say 'Nice to meet you' at a first meeting? {{c1::반갑습니다}} (Bangapseubnida) or {{c2::처음 뵙겠습니다}} (Cheoeum boepgessseubnida).
explanation: 'Cheoeum boepgessseubnida' is very formal (lit. 'I meet you for the first time'). 'Bangapseubnida' is standard polite.
usage: Dialogue: - 반갑습니다. - 네, 반갑습니다.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Mucho gusto / Encantado' en coreano? 반갑습니다 (Bangapseubnida) / 처음 뵙겠습니다.

text: In Korean, how do you say 'My name is [Name]' politely? '제 이름은 [Name]입니다' ({{c1::Je ireumeun [Name]-ibnida}}).
explanation: '제' (je) is polite 'my', '이름' (ireum) is 'name', and '입니다' (ibnida) is 'is'.
usage: Example: 제 이름은 지저스입니다. (My name is Jesus.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Mi nombre es...' en coreano? 제 이름은 [Nombre]입니다 (Je ireumeun... ibnida).

text: In Korean, how do you ask 'What is your name?' politely? {{c1::이름이 무엇입니까?}} (Ireumi mueos-ibnikka?) or {{c2::성함이 어떻게 되세요?}} (Sunghami eotteohge doeseyo? - highly respectful).
explanation: '성함' (sungham) is the honorific word for name.
usage: Polite: 이름이 무엇입니까? | Respectful: 성함이 어떻게 되세요?
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cómo te llamas / cuál es su nombre?' en coreano? 이름이 무엇입니까? / 성함이 어떻게 되세요?.

text: In Korean, a common polite suffix added to names to show respect (like 'Mr./Ms.') is '{{c1::씨}}' (ssi). Example: Marco = 마르코{{c2::씨}}.
explanation: Added to the full name or just the given name. Never added to the family name alone.
usage: Example: 마르코 씨, 안녕하세요? (Hello, Mr. Marco.)
spanish: En coreano, el sufijo honorífico equivalente a 'Señor/Señorita' que se añade al nombre es '씨' (ssi).

text: In Korean, how do you say 'Please' (when requesting something)? {{c1::주세요}} (Juseyo) or {{c2::부탁드립니다}} (Butagdeuribnida).
explanation: '주세요' is literally 'please give me'. 'Butagdeuribnida' means 'I request of you'.
usage: Examples: 물 주세요. (Water, please.) | 도와주세요. (Help me, please.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Por favor / Deme' en coreano? 주세요 (Juseyo) / 부탁드립니다 (Butagdeuribnida).

text: In Korean, a polite way to say 'You're welcome' is '{{c1::천만에요}}' (Cheonmaneyo) or simply '{{c2::아니에요}}' (Anieyo - 'No problem / It's nothing').
explanation: 'Cheonmaneyo' is textbook but rarely used in speech. 'Anieyo' is much more natural.
usage: Dialogue: - 감사합니다! - 아니에요.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'De nada' en coreano? 천만에요 (Cheonmaneyo) / 아니에요 (Anieyo - más natural).

text: In Korean, how do you ask 'How have you been / How are you?' politely? {{c1::어떻게 지내세요?}} (Eotteohge jinaeseyo?)
explanation: Literally: 'How are you spending time?'.
usage: Dialogue: - 어떻게 지내세요? - 잘 지내요. (I'm doing well.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cómo estás / Cómo te va?' en coreano? 어떻게 지내세요? (Eotteohge jinaeseyo?).

text: In Korean, what is the polite phrase meaning 'Please look after me / I look forward to working with you'? {{c1::잘 부탁드립니다}} (Jal butagdeuribnida).
explanation: A crucial cultural phrase in self-introductions in business, school, or community.
usage: Term: 잘 부탁드립니다
spanish: En coreano, la frase típica de cortesía al presentarse que significa 'Por favor, cuide de mí' es '잘 부탁드립니다'.

explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: 이다 (ida) | Meaning: To be
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'To be' en Korean? 이다 (ida)

scenario: Korean A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Korean, how do you say 'To be - Formal'? {{c1::입니다 (imnida)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: 입니다 (imnida) | Meaning: To be - Formal
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'To be - Formal' en Korean? 입니다 (imnida)

scenario: Korean A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Korean, how do you say 'To be - Polite'? {{c1::예요 / 이에요 (yeyo / ieyo)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: 예요 / 이에요 (yeyo / ieyo) | Meaning: To be - Polite
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'To be - Polite' en Korean? 예요 / 이에요 (yeyo / ieyo)

scenario: Korean A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Korean, how do you say 'To not be - Polite'? {{c1::아니에요 (anieyo)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: 아니에요 (anieyo) | Meaning: To not be - Polite
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'To not be - Polite' en Korean? 아니에요 (anieyo)

text: In Korean, the polite/humble pronoun for 'I' is '{{c1::저}}' (Jeo) and the informal is '{{c2::나}}' (Na).
explanation: Use '저' when speaking to elders, strangers, or in professional settings. Use '나' with close friends or younger people.
usage: Polite: 저는 학생입니다. (I am a student.) | Informal: 나는 괜찮아. (I am okay.)
spanish: En coreano, el pronombre para 'yo' es '저' (Jeo - formal/humilde) y '나' (Na - informal).

text: In Korean, the pronoun for 'we / us' is '{{c1::우리}}' (Uri) or politely '{{c2::저희}}' (Jeohui).
explanation: Korean culture emphasizes collectivism, so '우리' is often used where English uses 'my' (e.g. 'our wife', 'our house', 'our country').
usage: Example: 우리 나라 (Our country / my country) | 우리 집 (Our house / my house)
spanish: En coreano, el pronombre para 'nosotros' es '우리' (Uri - común) y '저희' (Jeohui - formal/humilde).

text: In Korean, what is the word for 'you (singular)'? Grammatically it is '{{c1::당신}}' (Dangsin) or '{{c2::너}}' (Neo - informal), but in practice, speakers address others by name + title or omit it entirely.
explanation: 'Dangsin' can sound confrontational (used in arguments) or intimate (used between spouses). Avoid using it with strangers. 'Neo' is strictly for close friends/kids.
usage: Rule: Omit 'you' or use title/name + 씨 (ssi). Example: 지수 씨는 뭐 해요? (What are you doing, Jisoo?)
spanish: En coreano, 'tú' es '너' (Neo - muy informal). '당신' (Dangsin) se limita a esposos o peleas. Suele omitirse.

text: In Korean, the verb 'to be' (identify Noun A as Noun B) is the copula '{{c1::이다}}' (ida). In formal polite style, it is conjugated as '{{c2::입니다}}' (ibnida).
explanation: '입니다' is appended directly to the noun without a space.
usage: Example: 학생입니다. (I am a student. [학생 = student])
spanish: En coreano, el verbo 'ser' es la cópula '이다' (ida). En estilo formal cortés se conjuga como '입니다' (ibnida).

text: In Korean, in informal polite style (해요체), the copula '이다' is conjugated as '{{c1::이에요}}' after a consonant, and '{{c2::예요}}' after a vowel.
explanation: Matches phonetics. 예요 is faster to pronounce after a vowel.
usage: Consonant end: 학생이에요. (I am a student.) | Vowel end: 의사예요. (I am a doctor.)
spanish: En estilo informal cortés, 'ser' se conjuga como '이에요' (tras consonante) y '예요' (tras vocal).

text: In Korean, the negative copula 'not to be' is '{{c1::아니다}}' (anida). In formal polite style, it is conjugated as '{{c2::아닙니다}}' (anibnida).
explanation: Unlike '입니다', '아닙니다' is written with a space after the noun, which takes the subject marker '이/가'.
usage: Example: 저는 학생이 아닙니다. (I am not a student.)
spanish: El verbo 'no ser' en coreano es '아니다' (anida). En tono formal se conjuga como '아닙니다' (anibnida).

text: In Korean, in informal polite style, the negative copula '아니다' is conjugated as '{{c1::아니에요}}' (anieyo).
explanation: Appended after the noun + subject marker.
usage: Example: 저는 의사가 아니에요. (I am not a doctor.)
spanish: En tono informal cortés, el verbo 'no ser' se conjuga como '아니에요' (anieyo).

text: In Korean, the topic marker particles are '{{c1::은}}' (after consonant) and '{{c2::는}}' (after vowel). Example: I (topic) = 저{{c2::는}}.
explanation: Used to introduce a topic, create contrast, or state general facts.
usage: Consonant: 이름은 | Vowel: 저는
spanish: Las partículas marcadoras de tema en coreano son '은' (tras consonante) y '는' (tras vocal).

text: In Korean, the subject marker particles are '{{c1::이}}' (after consonant) and '{{c2::가}}' (after vowel). Example: Name (subject) = 이름{{c1::이}}.
explanation: Focuses on the subject of the verb, often answering 'who/which' specifically.
usage: Consonant: 이름이 | Vowel: 의사가 (doctor)
spanish: Las partículas marcadoras de sujeto en coreano son '이' (tras consonante) y '가' (tras vocal).

text: In Korean, the object marker particles are '{{c1::을}}' (after consonant) and '{{c2::를}}' (after vowel). Example: Water (object) = 물{{c1::을}}.
explanation: Marks the noun that receives the action of a transitive verb.
usage: Consonant: 물을 마시다 (drink water) | Vowel: 커피를 마시다 (drink coffee)
spanish: Las partículas marcadoras de objeto directo en coreano son '을' (tras consonante) y '를' (tras vocal).

text: In Korean, the possessive particle meaning 'of' or equivalent to 's is '{{c1::의}}' (pronounced 'ui' or usually 'e' in spoken language).
explanation: Connects Noun A (owner) to Noun B (owned). '저의' (my) is often contracted to '제' (je).
usage: Example: 선생님의 책 (The teacher's book) | 저의 -> 제
spanish: La partícula posesiva en coreano es '의' (se pronuncia 'e' en el habla común). '저의' (mío) se abrevia '제'.

text: In Korean, the demonstrative modifiers for 'this', 'that (near listener)', and 'that (far from both)' are '{{c1::이}}' (i), '{{c2::그}}' (geu), and '{{c3::저}}' (jeo).
explanation: These are always placed before a noun. To make them pronouns, combine with '것' (geot - thing): 이것 (this thing), 그것 (that thing), 저것 (that thing over there).
usage: Modifiers: 이 책 (this book) | Pronouns: 이것은 무엇입니까? (What is this?)
spanish: Modificadores demostrativos en coreano: '이' (este), '그' (ese), '저' (aquel).

text: In Korean, the particle that means 'also' or 'too' is '{{c1::도}}' (do). It replaces topic/subject/object particles.
explanation: Identical in meaning to Spanish 'también'.
usage: Example: 저도 학생이에요. (I am also a student.)
spanish: En coreano, la partícula que significa 'también' es '도' (do). Reemplaza a 'eun/neun' o 'i/ga'.

text: In Korean, the question word for 'What' is '{{c1::무엇}}' (mueot), which is often contracted to '{{c2::뭐}}' (mwo) in speech.
explanation: '뭐' is the standard conversational form.
usage: Example: 이것은 무엇입니까? (What is this? - formal) | 이게 뭐야? (What is this? - casual)
spanish: En coreano, la palabra interrogativa para '¿Qué?' es '무엇' (mueot), que se reduce a '뭐' (mwo) en el habla común.

text: In Korean, the question word for 'Who' is '{{c1::누구}}' (nugu), which becomes '{{c2::누가}}' (nuga) when followed by the subject particle 'ga'.
explanation: '누가' is a contraction of '누구' + '가'.
usage: Example: 누구예요? (Who is it?) | 누가 왔어요? (Who came?)
spanish: En coreano, la palabra interrogativa para '¿Quién?' es '누구' (nugu), que cambia a '누가' (nuga) al ser sujeto.

text: In formal polite style, questions are formed by changing the verb ending from '-da' (다) to '{{c1::까}}' (kka) or '{{c2::오}}' (o). For example: 입니다 -> {{c3::입니까?}}.
explanation: The formal style uses distinct declarative (-da) and interrogative (-kka) endings.
usage: Dialogue: - 학생입니까? - 네, 학생입니다. (- Are you a student? - Yes, I am a student.)
spanish: En estilo formal en coreano, las preguntas terminan en '-까' (-kka) en lugar de '-다' (-da). '입니다' -> '입니까?'.

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Length}} is defined as: Historically phonemic, but largely lost in modern Seoul dialect.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Korean.
usage:
Rule: Vowel Length
Description: Historically phonemic, but largely lost in modern Seoul dialect.
spanish: En la fonética de Korean, la regla de 'Vowel Length' es: Historically phonemic, but largely lost in modern Seoul dialect.

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Diphthongs}} is defined as: Korean has many y-glides (야, 여, 요, 유, 얘, 예) and w-glides (와, 워, 왜, 웨).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Korean.
usage:
Rule: Diphthongs
Description: Korean has many y-glides (야, 여, 요, 유, 얘, 예) and w-glides (와, 워, 왜, 웨).
spanish: En la fonética de Korean, la regla de 'Diphthongs' es: Korean has many y-glides (야, 여, 요, 유, 얘, 예) and w-glides (와, 워, 왜, 웨).

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Harmony}} is defined as: While less strict than in the past, it still influences onomatopoeia and some verb endings.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Korean.
usage:
Rule: Vowel Harmony
Description: While less strict than in the past, it still influences onomatopoeia and some verb endings.
spanish: En la fonética de Korean, la regla de 'Vowel Harmony' es: While less strict than in the past, it still influences onomatopoeia and some verb endings.

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Three-way Contrast}} is defined as: Stops and affricates are distinguished by phonation:

Description: Stops and affricates are distinguished by phonation:

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Plain (Laxis)}} is defined as: Slightly aspirated, voiced between vowels.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Korean.
usage:
Rule: Plain (Laxis)
Description: Slightly aspirated, voiced between vowels.
spanish: En la fonética de Korean, la regla de 'Plain (Laxis)' es: Slightly aspirated, voiced between vowels.

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Aspirated}} is defined as: Strongly aspirated.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Korean.
usage:
Rule: Aspirated
Description: Strongly aspirated.
spanish: En la fonética de Korean, la regla de 'Aspirated' es: Strongly aspirated.

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Tense (Fortis)}} is defined as: Produced with a "stiff" voice, never aspirated.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Korean.
usage:
Rule: Tense (Fortis)
Description: Produced with a "stiff" voice, never aspirated.
spanish: En la fonética de Korean, la regla de 'Tense (Fortis)' es: Produced with a "stiff" voice, never aspirated.

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Liquid /l/ (ㄹ)}} is defined as: Pronounced as a flap [ɾ] between vowels and a lateral [l] at the end of a syllable.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Korean.
usage:
Rule: The Liquid /l/ (ㄹ)
Description: Pronounced as a flap [ɾ] between vowels and a lateral [l] at the end of a syllable.
spanish: En la fonética de Korean, la regla de 'The Liquid /l/ (ㄹ)' es: Pronounced as a flap [ɾ] between vowels and a lateral [l] at the end of a syllable.

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Batchim (Syllable Finals)}} is defined as: Only 7 consonant sounds can be heard at the end of a syllable ([p, t, k, m, n, ŋ, l]). All other consonants are neutralized (e.g., 's' ㅅ, 't' ㅌ, 'ch' ㅊ, 'd' ㄷ all sound like [t] at the end of a block, e.g., *kkoch* 꽃 -> [kkot]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Korean.
usage:
Rule: Batchim (Syllable Finals)
Description: Only 7 consonant sounds can be heard at the end of a syllable ([p, t, k, m, n, ŋ, l]). All other consonants are neutralized (e.g., 's' ㅅ, 't' ㅌ, 'ch' ㅊ, 'd' ㄷ all sound like [t] at the end of a block, e.g., *kkoch* 꽃 -> [kkot]).
spanish: En la fonética de Korean, la regla de 'Batchim (Syllable Finals)' es: Only 7 consonant sounds can be heard at the end of a syllable ([p, t, k, m, n, ŋ, l]). All other consonants are neutralized (e.g., 's' ㅅ, 't' ㅌ, 'ch' ㅊ, 'd' ㄷ all sound like [t] at the end of a block, e.g., *kkoch* 꽃 -> [kkot]).

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Liaison (Linkage)}} is defined as: A Batchim consonant followed by a word starting with a vowel carries over to the next syllable (e.g., *bap-eul* 밥을 -> [ba.beul]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Korean.
usage:
Rule: Liaison (Linkage)
Description: A Batchim consonant followed by a word starting with a vowel carries over to the next syllable (e.g., *bap-eul* 밥을 -> [ba.beul]).
spanish: En la fonética de Korean, la regla de 'Liaison (Linkage)' es: A Batchim consonant followed by a word starting with a vowel carries over to the next syllable (e.g., *bap-eul* 밥을 -> [ba.beul]).

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Nasalization}} is defined as: Plain stop consonants (ㅂ, ㄷ, ㄱ) assimilate into nasals when followed by a nasal consonant (ㄴ, ㅁ) (e.g., *hap-ni-da* 합니다 -> [ham-ni-da], *kuk-mul* 국물 -> [kuŋ-mul]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Korean.
usage:
Rule: Nasalization
Description: Plain stop consonants (ㅂ, ㄷ, ㄱ) assimilate into nasals when followed by a nasal consonant (ㄴ, ㅁ) (e.g., *hap-ni-da* 합니다 -> [ham-ni-da], *kuk-mul* 국물 -> [kuŋ-mul]).
spanish: En la fonética de Korean, la regla de 'Nasalization' es: Plain stop consonants (ㅂ, ㄷ, ㄱ) assimilate into nasals when followed by a nasal consonant (ㄴ, ㅁ) (e.g., *hap-ni-da* 합니다 -> [ham-ni-da], *kuk-mul* 국물 -> [kuŋ-mul]).

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Tensification}} is defined as: Plain consonants (ㅂ, ㄷ, ㄱ, ㅅ, ㅈ) become tense/fortis (ㅃ, ㄸ, ㄲ, ㅆ, ㅉ) when following a Batchim stop (e.g., *hak-kyo* 학교 -> [hak-kkyo]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Korean.
usage:
Rule: Tensification
Description: Plain consonants (ㅂ, ㄷ, ㄱ, ㅅ, ㅈ) become tense/fortis (ㅃ, ㄸ, ㄲ, ㅆ, ㅉ) when following a Batchim stop (e.g., *hak-kyo* 학교 -> [hak-kkyo]).
spanish: En la fonética de Korean, la regla de 'Tensification' es: Plain consonants (ㅂ, ㄷ, ㄱ, ㅅ, ㅈ) become tense/fortis (ㅃ, ㄸ, ㄲ, ㅆ, ㅉ) when following a Batchim stop (e.g., *hak-kyo* 학교 -> [hak-kkyo]).

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Palatalization}} is defined as: Syllable final ㄷ (t) or ㅌ (tʰ) followed by 이 (i) is pronounced as [dʒ] (ㅈ) or [tʃ] (ㅊ) (e.g., *kat-i* 같이 -> [ga-tʃi] "gachi", *ha-tot-i* 해돋이 -> [hae-do-ji]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Korean.
usage:
Rule: Palatalization
Description: Syllable final ㄷ (t) or ㅌ (tʰ) followed by 이 (i) is pronounced as [dʒ] (ㅈ) or [tʃ] (ㅊ) (e.g., *kat-i* 같이 -> [ga-tʃi] "gachi", *ha-tot-i* 해돋이 -> [hae-do-ji]).
spanish: En la fonética de Korean, la regla de 'Palatalization' es: Syllable final ㄷ (t) or ㅌ (tʰ) followed by 이 (i) is pronounced as [dʒ] (ㅈ) or [tʃ] (ㅊ) (e.g., *kat-i* 같이 -> [ga-tʃi] "gachi", *ha-tot-i* 해돋이 -> [hae-do-ji]).

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Liquid Assimilation}} is defined as: - When ㄴ (n) and ㄹ (l) meet, the ㄴ is pronounced as [l] (e.g., *shin-ra* 신라 -> [shilla]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Korean.
usage:
Rule: Liquid Assimilation
Description: - When ㄴ (n) and ㄹ (l) meet, the ㄴ is pronounced as [l] (e.g., *shin-ra* 신라 -> [shilla]).
spanish: En la fonética de Korean, la regla de 'Liquid Assimilation' es: - When ㄴ (n) and ㄹ (l) meet, the ㄴ is pronounced as [l] (e.g., *shin-ra* 신라 -> [shilla]).

usage:

usage:
Rule: o + a
Description: > **wa** (e.g., *bo-a* -> "bwa" 봐).

explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Korean.
usage:
Rule: u + eo
Description: > **weo** (e.g., *ju-eo* -> "jweo" 줘).
spanish: En la fonética de Korean, la regla de 'u + eo' es: > **weo** (e.g., *ju-eo* -> "jweo" 줘).

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Syllable-timed}} is defined as: Korean is generally considered syllable-timed.

Rule: Syllable-timed
Description: Korean is generally considered syllable-timed.
spanish: En la fonética de Korean, la regla de 'Syllable-timed' es: Korean is generally considered syllable-timed.

text: In Korean phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Intonation}} is defined as: Pitch generally rises at the end of questions and remains relatively flat or falls in statements.

usage:
Rule: Intonation
Description: Pitch generally rises at the end of questions and remains relatively flat or falls in statements.

text: What is the Korean pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "Hapnida" Rule}}? Never say "hap-ni-da" for 합니다. The 'ㅂ' must turn into an 'ㅁ' due to nasalization: say "ham-ni-da". This is the most common beginner error.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Korean.
usage:
Tip: The "Hapnida" Rule
Never say "hap-ni-da" for 합니다. The 'ㅂ' must turn into an 'ㅁ' due to nasalization: say "ham-ni-da". This is the most common beginner error.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Korean para 'The "Hapnida" Rule'? Never say "hap-ni-da" for 합니다. The 'ㅂ' must turn into an 'ㅁ' due to nasalization: say "ham-ni-da". This is the most common beginner error.

text: What is the Korean pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The Palatalization Bridge}}? When 'ㅌ' (t) in 같이 (together) meets '이' (i), it naturally shifts to a 'ch' [tʃ] sound: "ga-chi". Do not try to pronounce it as "gat-ee".
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Korean.
usage:
Tip: The Palatalization Bridge
When 'ㅌ' (t) in 같이 (together) meets '이' (i), it naturally shifts to a 'ch' [tʃ] sound: "ga-chi". Do not try to pronounce it as "gat-ee".
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Korean para 'The Palatalization Bridge'? When 'ㅌ' (t) in 같이 (together) meets '이' (i), it naturally shifts to a 'ch' [tʃ] sound: "ga-chi". Do not try to pronounce it as "gat-ee".

usage: Term: Tudo bem? | Meaning: How are you? / Everything well?

usage: Term: Até logo | Meaning: See you later
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'See you later' en Portuguese? Até logo

text: In Portuguese, how do you say 'Hello / Hi' informally? {{c1::Olá}}
explanation: Standard friendly greeting used at any time of day.
usage: Term: Olá | Example: Olá, como vais? (Hi, how are you?)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hola' en portugués? Olá.

text: In Portuguese, how do you say 'Good evening / Good night'? {{c1::Boa noite}}
explanation: Used both upon arriving and departing after dark.
usage: Term: Boa noite | Example: Boa noite, dorme bem. (Good night, sleep well.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenas noches' en portugués? Boa noite.

text: In Portuguese, how do you say 'Goodbye' formally or for long separations? {{c1::Adeus}}
explanation: Literally 'to God'. For daily farewells, shorter terms like 'tchau' or 'até logo' are preferred.
usage: Term: Adeus | Dialogue: - Adeus! - Até logo!
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Adiós' en portugués? Adeus.

text: In Portuguese, how do you say 'See you later'? {{c1::Até logo}} or {{c1::Até mais}}.
explanation: 'Até logo' is very common when you expect to see the person later that day.
usage: Term: Até logo | Dialogue: - Vou ao supermercado. - Até logo!
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hasta luego' en portugués? Até logo.

text: In Portuguese, how do you say 'Please'? {{c1::Por favor}}
explanation: The universal and polite way to make requests.
usage: Term: Por favor | Example: Um café, por favor. (A coffee, please.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Por favor' en portugués? Por favor.

text: In Portuguese, if you are a male, you say 'Thank you' as '{{c1::Obrigado}}'. If you are a female, you say '{{c2::Obrigada}}'.
explanation: The word agrees with the gender of the speaker, not the listener.
usage: Male: Obrigado. | Female: Obrigada.
spanish: En portugués, las gracias concuerdan con el hablante: 'Obrigado' (hombre) o 'Obrigada' (mujer).

text: In Portuguese, how do you say 'You're welcome'? {{c1::De nada}}
explanation: Standard response to thank you. Can also hear 'não há de quê' (it's nothing).
usage: Dialogue: - Obrigado! - De nada.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'De nada' en portugués? De nada.

text: In Portuguese, how do you ask someone their name? {{c1::Como se chama?}} and the response is '{{c2::Chamo-me}} [Name]'.
explanation: Standard pronominal verb construction in European Portuguese. In Brazil, 'Como você se chama?' or 'Qual o seu nome?' is more common.
usage: Dialogue: - Como se chama? - Chamo-me Jesus.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cómo te llamas?' en portugués? 'Como se chama?' y la respuesta es 'Chamo-me...'.

text: In Portuguese, how do you say 'Nice to meet you' upon introductions? {{c1::Muito prazer}} or {{c1::Prazer}}.
explanation: 'Muito prazer' means 'much pleasure'.
usage: Dialogue: - Chamo-me Maria. - Muito prazer, eu sou o Carlos.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Mucho gusto' en portugués? Muito prazer.

text: In Portuguese, how do you ask 'How are you?' politely? {{c1::Como está?}} or informally {{c2::Tudo bem?}}.
explanation: 'Tudo bem?' (All good?) is extremely popular in all contexts. 'Como está?' is the standard verb structure.
usage: Dialogue: - Olá, tudo bem? - Tudo bem, obrigado.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cómo está / Todo bien?' en portugués? Como está? / Tudo bem?.

text: In Portuguese, how do you say 'See you tomorrow'? {{c1::Até amanhã}}
explanation: Common parting phrase for people who meet daily.
usage: Term: Até amanhã | Dialogue: - Adeus! - Até amanhã!
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hasta mañana' en portugués? Até amanhã.

text: In Portuguese, the most common informal way to say 'Bye / Goodbye' is '{{c1::Tchau}}'.
explanation: Derived from Italian 'Ciao'. Extremely common in daily conversational Portuguese.
usage: Term: Tchau | Example: Tchau, até logo! (Bye, see you later!)
spanish: En portugués, la forma informal más común de decir 'Adiós' es 'Tchau'.

text: In Portuguese, how do you ask 'How are you doing? / How's it going?' in a friendly way? {{c1::Como vai?}}
explanation: A very common alternative to 'Como está?' and 'Tudo bem?'.
usage: Term: Como vai? | Dialogue: - Olá, como vai? - Tudo ótimo, e contigo?
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cómo te va?' en portugués? Como vai?.

text: In Portuguese, the subject pronoun for 'I' is '{{c1::eu}}' and for 'you (singular informal)' is '{{c2::tu}}'.
explanation: European Portuguese regularly uses 'tu' for close friends/peers. Brazil mostly replaces 'tu' with 'você'.
usage: Examples: Eu sou espanhol. (I am Spanish.) | Tu és estudante? (Are you a student?)
spanish: En portugués, los pronombres para 'yo' y 'tú' son 'eu' y 'tu'.

text: In Portuguese, the third-person singular pronouns for 'he' and 'she' are {{c1::ele}} and {{c2::ela}}.
explanation: They correspond directly to 'él' and 'ella' in Spanish.
usage: Examples: Ele é médico. (He is a doctor.) | Ela é enfermeira. (She is a nurse.)
spanish: En portugués, los pronombres para 'él' y 'ella' son 'ele' y 'ela'.

text: In Portuguese, the subject pronoun for 'we' is {{c1::nós}}.
explanation: Conversational Portuguese (especially in Brazil) often replaces 'nós' with 'a gente' (conjugated in third-person singular, e.g., 'a gente é').
usage: Example: Nós somos amigos. (We are friends.) | Colloquial: A gente é amigo.
spanish: En portugués, 'nosotros' se dice 'nós'. Coloquialmente se usa 'a gente'.

text: In Portuguese, the plural pronouns for 'they' (masc/mixed) is {{c1::eles}} and 'they' (fem) is {{c2::elas}}.
explanation: They follow direct gender agreement rules.
usage: Examples: Eles são engenheiros. (They are engineers.) | Elas são professoras. (They are teachers - fem.)
spanish: En portugués, 'ellos' se dice 'eles' y 'ellas' se dice 'elas'.

text: In Portuguese, the verb for permanent identity 'to be' is '{{c1::ser}}'. Conjugate 'ser' for 'eu' and 'tu': eu {{c2::sou}}, tu {{c3::és}}.
explanation: Ser is used for characteristics like nationality, profession, permanent traits, and origin.
usage: Examples: Eu sou do Porto. (I am from Porto.) | Tu és simpático. (You are nice.)
spanish: El verbo 'ser' en portugués se conjuga para yo y tú como: eu sou, tu és.

text: Conjugate the verb 'ser' (to be) in the present tense for 'ele/ela' (he/she) and 'nós' (we): ele/ela {{c1::é}}, nós {{c2::somos}}.
explanation: Note the acute accent on 'é' (is). Without it, 'e' means 'and'.
usage: Examples: Ele é arquiteto. (He is an architect.) | Nós somos felizes. (We are happy.)
spanish: Conjuga 'ser' en presente para 'él/ella' y 'nosotros': ele/ela é, nós somos.

text: Conjugate the verb 'ser' (to be) in the present tense for 'eles/elas' (they): eles/elas {{c1::são}}.
explanation: Notice the nasal ending '-ão'.
usage: Example: Elas são estudantes. (They are students.)
spanish: Conjuga 'ser' en presente para 'ellos/ellas': eles/elas são.

text: In Portuguese, the verb for temporary states 'to be' is '{{c1::estar}}'. Conjugate 'estar' for 'eu' and 'tu': eu {{c2::estou}}, tu {{c3::estás}}.
explanation: Estar is used for location, health, temporary moods, or current weather.
usage: Examples: Eu estou cansado. (I am tired.) | Tu estás em Lisboa? (Are you in Lisbon?)
spanish: El verbo 'estar' en portugués se conjuga para yo y tú como: eu estou, tu estás.

text: Conjugate the verb 'estar' (to be) in the present tense for 'ele/ela' (he/she) and 'nós' (we): ele/ela {{c1::está}}, nós {{c2::estamos}}.
explanation: Estar conjugations carry accents on terminal vowels.
usage: Examples: Como está ele? (How is he?) | Nós estamos no hotel. (We are at the hotel.)
spanish: Conjuga 'estar' en presente para 'él/ella' y 'nosotros': ele/ela está, nós estamos.

text: Conjugate the verb 'estar' (to be) in the present tense for 'eles/elas' (they): eles/elas {{c1::estão}}.
explanation: Carries the nasal diphthong '-ão'.
usage: Example: Eles estão com pressa. (They are in a hurry.)
spanish: Conjuga 'estar' en presente para 'ellos/ellas': eles/elas estão.

text: In Portuguese, the pronoun '{{c1::você}}' is formal in Portugal but informal in Brazil, and it conjugates with the {{c2::third-person singular}} form of the verb.
explanation: In Portugal, 'você' is polite but some find it slightly cold; name + verb in 3rd person is preferred for polite address. In Brazil, 'você' is the default 'you'.
usage: Example: Você é de onde? (Where are you from?)
spanish: En portugués, 'você' equivale a 'tú' (Brasil) o 'usted' (Portugal). Se conjuga en tercera persona singular (e.g. você é).

text: To negate a sentence in Portuguese, you place the word '{{c1::não}}' before the verb. Example: I am not tired = {{c2::Não estou cansado}}.
explanation: Direct and simple negation identical to Spanish 'no'.
usage: Example: Ela não é daqui. (She is not from here.)
spanish: Para negar una oración en portugués, se pone 'não' antes del verbo. 'Não estou cansado' = No estoy cansado.

text: In Portuguese, prepositions regularly contract with personal pronouns. For example, 'de + ele' becomes '{{c1::dele}}' (his/of him) and 'de + ela' becomes '{{c2::dela}}' (her/of her).
explanation: Required in Portuguese grammar, unlike Spanish where 'de él' remains separated.
usage: Example: O livro é dele. (The book is his.) | A casa é dela. (The house is hers.)
spanish: En portugués, 'de + ele/ela' se contrae obligatoriamente como 'dele' (de él) y 'dela' (de ella).

text: In Portuguese, 'em + ele' contracts to '{{c1::nele}}' (in him/it) and 'em + ela' contracts to '{{c2::nela}}' (in her/it).
explanation: Combines preposition 'em' (in/on/at) with the pronoun.
usage: Example: Eu acredito nele. (I believe in him.)
spanish: La preposición 'em' se contrae con 'ele/ela' como 'nele' y 'nela'.

text: Just like Spanish, yes/no questions in Portuguese are formed purely through {{c1::intonation}} without changing the word order.
explanation: A statement becomes a question simply by raising the pitch of your voice at the end.
usage: Statement: Tu és o Jesus. | Question: Tu és o Jesus?
spanish: Al igual que el español, en portugués las preguntas de sí/no se indican únicamente mediante la entonación.

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Nasalization}} is defined as: Indicated by the tilde (e.g., *pão* /pɐ̃w/) or by a nasal consonant following the vowel in the same syllable (*cantar* /kɐ̃ˈtaɾ/).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Nasalization
Description: Indicated by the tilde (e.g., *pão* /pɐ̃w/) or by a nasal consonant following the vowel in the same syllable (*cantar* /kɐ̃ˈtaɾ/).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Nasalization' es: Indicated by the tilde (e.g., *pão* /pɐ̃w/) or by a nasal consonant following the vowel in the same syllable (*cantar* /kɐ̃ˈtaɾ/).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Reduction}} is defined as: In unstressed positions, vowels often reduce. In EP, this reduction is extreme, while in BP, /e/ often becomes /i/ and /o/ becomes /u/ at the end of words.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Vowel Reduction
Description: In unstressed positions, vowels often reduce. In EP, this reduction is extreme, while in BP, /e/ often becomes /i/ and /o/ becomes /u/ at the end of words.
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Vowel Reduction' es: In unstressed positions, vowels often reduce. In EP, this reduction is extreme, while in BP, /e/ often becomes /i/ and /o/ becomes /u/ at the end of words.

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Diphthongs}} is defined as: Portuguese is rich in both oral and nasal diphthongs (e.g., *mãe* /mɐ̃j/).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Diphthongs
Description: Portuguese is rich in both oral and nasal diphthongs (e.g., *mãe* /mɐ̃j/).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Diphthongs' es: Portuguese is rich in both oral and nasal diphthongs (e.g., *mãe* /mɐ̃j/).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Multiple 'R'}} is defined as: - **Weak R**: Alveolar tap /ɾ/ between vowels (*caro*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: The Multiple 'R'
Description: - **Weak R**: Alveolar tap /ɾ/ between vowels (*caro*).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'The Multiple 'R'' es: - **Weak R**: Alveolar tap /ɾ/ between vowels (*caro*).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Strong R}} is defined as: Variable (uvular fricative /ʁ/, glottal /h/, or trill /r/) at the start of words or when doubled (*carro*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Strong R
Description: Variable (uvular fricative /ʁ/, glottal /h/, or trill /r/) at the start of words or when doubled (*carro*).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Strong R' es: Variable (uvular fricative /ʁ/, glottal /h/, or trill /r/) at the start of words or when doubled (*carro*).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::L-Vocalization}} is defined as: In Brazil, final /l/ is usually pronounced as a glide [w] (*brasil* [bɾaˈziw]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: L-Vocalization
Description: In Brazil, final /l/ is usually pronounced as a glide [w] (*brasil* [bɾaˈziw]).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'L-Vocalization' es: In Brazil, final /l/ is usually pronounced as a glide [w] (*brasil* [bɾaˈziw]).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Palatalization of T/D}} is defined as: In BP, /t/ and /d/ become affricates [tʃ] and [dʒ] before the sound /i/ (*dia* [ˈdʒiɐ]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Palatalization of T/D
Description: In BP, /t/ and /d/ become affricates [tʃ] and [dʒ] before the sound /i/ (*dia* [ˈdʒiɐ]).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Palatalization of T/D' es: In BP, /t/ and /d/ become affricates [tʃ] and [dʒ] before the sound /i/ (*dia* [ˈdʒiɐ]).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Nasalization}} is defined as: Any vowel followed by 'm', 'n', or 'nh' is nasalized, even across syllable boundaries (e.g., *cama* -> [ˈkɐ̃mɐ]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Nasalization
Description: Any vowel followed by 'm', 'n', or 'nh' is nasalized, even across syllable boundaries (e.g., *cama* -> [ˈkɐ̃mɐ]).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Nasalization' es: Any vowel followed by 'm', 'n', or 'nh' is nasalized, even across syllable boundaries (e.g., *cama* -> [ˈkɐ̃mɐ]).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::EP Vowel Omission (Elision)}} is defined as: In European Portuguese, unstressed vowels are extremely reduced or dropped entirely in connected speech, creating complex consonant clusters (e.g., *excelente* -> [ʃˈlẽt], *padaria* -> [pðɐˈɾiɐ]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: EP Vowel Omission (Elision)
Description: In European Portuguese, unstressed vowels are extremely reduced or dropped entirely in connected speech, creating complex consonant clusters (e.g., *excelente* -> [ʃˈlẽt], *padaria* -> [pðɐˈɾiɐ]).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'EP Vowel Omission (Elision)' es: In European Portuguese, unstressed vowels are extremely reduced or dropped entirely in connected speech, creating complex consonant clusters (e.g., *excelente* -> [ʃˈlẽt], *padaria* -> [pðɐˈɾiɐ]).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::BP L-Vocalization}} is defined as: In Brazil, final syllable /l/ is vocalized to a semivowel [w] (e.g., *Brasil* -> [bɾaˈziw], *sol* -> [sɔw]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: BP L-Vocalization
Description: In Brazil, final syllable /l/ is vocalized to a semivowel [w] (e.g., *Brasil* -> [bɾaˈziw], *sol* -> [sɔw]).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'BP L-Vocalization' es: In Brazil, final syllable /l/ is vocalized to a semivowel [w] (e.g., *Brasil* -> [bɾaˈziw], *sol* -> [sɔw]).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::BP Plural Marker Dropping}} is defined as: In colloquial Brazilian speech, plural 's' is often dropped from nouns and adjectives if the preceding article already marks the plural (e.g., *as coisas* -> "as coisa", *os carros* -> "os carro").
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: BP Plural Marker Dropping
Description: In colloquial Brazilian speech, plural 's' is often dropped from nouns and adjectives if the preceding article already marks the plural (e.g., *as coisas* -> "as coisa", *os carros* -> "os carro").
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'BP Plural Marker Dropping' es: In colloquial Brazilian speech, plural 's' is often dropped from nouns and adjectives if the preceding article already marks the plural (e.g., *as coisas* -> "as coisa", *os carros* -> "os carro").

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Stress}} is defined as: Usually falls on the penultimate syllable if the word ends in a vowel, 'n', or 's'. Words ending in other consonants or nasal diphthongs are often stressed on the last syllable (*amor*, *maçã*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Stress
Description: Usually falls on the penultimate syllable if the word ends in a vowel, 'n', or 's'. Words ending in other consonants or nasal diphthongs are often stressed on the last syllable (*amor*, *maçã*).
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Stress' es: Usually falls on the penultimate syllable if the word ends in a vowel, 'n', or 's'. Words ending in other consonants or nasal diphthongs are often stressed on the last syllable (*amor*, *maçã*).

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::European Portuguese}} is defined as: More stress-timed with heavy vowel reduction.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: European Portuguese
Description: More stress-timed with heavy vowel reduction.
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'European Portuguese' es: More stress-timed with heavy vowel reduction.

text: In Portuguese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Brazilian Portuguese}} is defined as: More syllable-timed and musical.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Portuguese.
usage:
Rule: Brazilian Portuguese
Description: More syllable-timed and musical.
spanish: En la fonética de Portuguese, la regla de 'Brazilian Portuguese' es: More syllable-timed and musical.

text: What is the Portuguese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The Brazilian "Tá" Contraction}}? In Brazil, almost nobody says "está" in spoken conversation. Always shorten it to "tá" (e.g., "Tudo bem? Tá tudo bem!").
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Portuguese.
usage:
Tip: The Brazilian "Tá" Contraction
In Brazil, almost nobody says "está" in spoken conversation. Always shorten it to "tá" (e.g., "Tudo bem? Tá tudo bem!").
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Portuguese para 'The Brazilian "Tá" Contraction'? In Brazil, almost nobody says "está" in spoken conversation. Always shorten it to "tá" (e.g., "Tudo bem? Tá tudo bem!").

text: What is the Portuguese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "Pra" and "Cê" Shortcuts}}? To flow naturally, shorten "para" to "pra" and "você" to "cê". Saying "Cê vai pra casa?" sounds 100% natural, whereas "Você vai para casa?" sounds overly formal.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Portuguese.
usage:
Tip: The "Pra" and "Cê" Shortcuts
To flow naturally, shorten "para" to "pra" and "você" to "cê". Saying "Cê vai pra casa?" sounds 100% natural, whereas "Você vai para casa?" sounds overly formal.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Portuguese para 'The "Pra" and "Cê" Shortcuts'? To flow naturally, shorten "para" to "pra" and "você" to "cê". Saying "Cê vai pra casa?" sounds 100% natural, whereas "Você vai para casa?" sounds overly formal.

text: What is the Portuguese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Tip}}? The tilde `~` always indicates a nasal sound. Practice by saying the vowel while partially blocking your nose.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Portuguese.
usage:
Tip: Tip
The tilde `~` always indicates a nasal sound. Practice by saying the vowel while partially blocking your nose.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Portuguese para 'Tip'? The tilde `~` always indicates a nasal sound. Practice by saying the vowel while partially blocking your nose.

text: In Portuguese, the tilde accent (~) indicates {{c1::nasalization}} of the vowel. The common ending '-ão' is pronounced as a nasal diphthong {{c2::/ɐ̃w̃/}}.
explanation: The air goes through both the mouth and the nose when pronouncing these vowels. Essential for correct Portuguese accent.
usage: Examples: pão (/pɐ̃w̃/ - bread), não (/nɐ̃w̃/ - no), coração (heart).
spanish: En portugués, el acento de virgulilla (~) indica nasalización. La terminación '-ão' suena como el diptongo nasal '/ɐ̃w̃/'.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Bom dia, tia."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Bom djia, tchia.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Palatalization of T/D (BR)
In Brazilian Portuguese, /d/ and /t/ are palatalized into affricates [dʒ] and [tʃ] when preceding the vowel /i/.
usage: Formal: Bom dia, tia. → Connected: Bom djia, tchia.
spanish: Spanish: Buenos días, tía.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "O Brasil ganhou sob o sol."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::O Brasiw ganhou sob o sow.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: L-Vocalization (BR)
Syllable-final L is vocalized to the semivowel [w] in Brazilian dialects.
usage: Formal: O Brasil ganhou sob o sol. → Connected: O Brasiw ganhou sob o sow.
spanish: Spanish: Brasil ganó bajo el sol.

text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Isto é excelente."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Isto é shs-lẽt.}}
explanation:
Applied Rule: Vowel Reduction (PT)
In European Portuguese, unstressed vowels are almost completely deleted, leaving consonant clusters.
usage: Formal: Isto é excelente. → Connected: Isto é shs-lẽt.
spanish: Spanish: Esto es excelente.

text: In Russian, the polite and formal greeting for 'Hello' (to strangers, elders, or superiors) is '{{c1::Здравствуйте}}' (Zdravstvuyte).

usage: Term: Здравствуйте | Formal greeting.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hola' formalmente en ruso? Здравствуйте (Zdravstvuyte).

text: In Russian, how do you say 'Good afternoon'? {{c1::Добрый день}} (Dobryy den')

usage: Term: Добрый день
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenas tardes' en ruso? Добрый день (Dobryy den').

text: In Russian, how do you say 'Good evening'? {{c1::Добрый вечер}} (Dobryy vecher)
explanation: Greeting used after sunset or in the evening.
usage: Term: Добрый вечер
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenas noches (saludo)' en ruso? Добрый вечер (Dobryy vecher).

text: In Russian, how do you say 'Goodbye' formally? {{c1::До свидания}} (Do svidaniya)
explanation: Literally: 'Until meeting'. The universal formal farewell.
usage: Term: До свидания
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Adiós' formalmente en ruso? До свидания (Do svidaniya).

text: In Russian, how do you say 'Bye' informally? {{c1::Пока}} (Poka)
explanation: Used with friends, family, and peers.
usage: Term: Пока | Dialogue: - Увидимся, пока! - Пока!
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Adiós' informalmente en ruso? Пока (Poka).

text: In Russian, the versatile word for both 'Please' and 'You're welcome' is '{{c1::Пожалуйста}}' (Pozhaluysta).
explanation: Pronounced roughly: /pa-zha-luy-sta/. The first 'u' (у) and 'y' (й) are often blended in natural speech.
usage: Examples: Дайте, пожалуйста, воду. (Please give me water.) | - Спасибо! - Пожалуйста!
spanish: En ruso, la palabra 'Пожалуйста' (Pozhaluysta) significa tanto 'Por favor' como 'De nada'.

text: In Russian, how do you say 'Thank you'? {{c1::Спасибо}} (Spasibo)
explanation: The standard, most common way to show gratitude.
usage: Term: Спасибо | Example: Спасибо за помощь. (Thank you for the help.)
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Gracias' en ruso? Спасибо (Spasibo).

text: In Russian, how do you ask someone their name formally? '{{c1::Как вас зовут?}}' (Kak vas zovut?) and the response is '{{c2::Меня зовут}} [Name]' (Menya zovut...).
explanation: Literally: 'How do they call you?' / 'They call me...'. 'вас' is the formal/plural 'you' in accusative.
usage: Dialogue: - Как вас зовут? - Меня зовут Jesus.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cómo se llama?' en ruso? Как вас зовут? y la respuesta es Меня зовут...

text: In Russian, how do you say 'Nice to meet you'? {{c1::Очень приятно}} (Ochen' priyatno)
explanation: Literally: 'Very pleasant'. Short and standard phrase.
usage: Dialogue: - Меня зовут Анна. - Очень приятно, я Иван.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Mucho gusto' en ruso? Очень приятно (Ochen' priyatno).

text: In Russian, how do you ask 'How are things? / How are you?'? {{c1::Как дела?}} (Kak dela?)
explanation: The standard casual question. Response can be 'Хорошо' (Good), 'Нормально' (Normal), or 'Плохо' (Bad).
usage: Dialogue: - Как дела? - Хорошо, спасибо!
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cómo estás / Cómo te va?' en ruso? Как дела? (Kak dela?).

text: In Russian, how do you say 'Good night' (when going to bed)? {{c1::Спокойной ночи}} (Spokoynoy nochi)
explanation: Literally: 'Of peaceful night'. Said to someone going to sleep.
usage: Term: Спокойной ночи
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenas noches (al ir a dormir)' en ruso? Спокойной ночи.

text: In Russian, how do you say 'Not'? {{c1::не (ne)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: не (ne) | Meaning: Not
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Not' en Russian? не (ne)

text: In Russian, the subject pronouns for 'I' and 'you (singular informal)' are {{c1::я}} (ya) and {{c2::ты}} (ty).
explanation: Subject pronouns are used in front of verbs and nouns.
usage: Examples: Я студент. (I am a student.) | Ты дома? (Are you at home?)
spanish: En ruso, los pronombres para 'yo' y 'tú' son я y ты.

text: In Russian, the third-person singular pronouns for 'he', 'she', and 'it' are {{c1::он}} (on), {{c2::она}} (ona), and {{c3::оно}} (ono).
explanation: Russian nouns have three genders, and pronouns match the gender of the noun.
usage: Examples: Он врач. (He is a doctor.) | Она учитель. (She is a teacher.)
spanish: En ruso, los pronombres de tercera persona singular son он (él), она (ella) y оно (ello/neutro).

text: In Russian, the plural subject pronouns for 'we', 'you (formal/plural)', and 'they' are {{c1::мы}} (my), {{c2::вы}} (vy), and {{c3::они}} (oni).
explanation: 'вы' is capitalized as 'Вы' when writing to one person formally.
usage: Examples: Мы готовы. (We are ready.) | Они здесь. (They are here.)
spanish: En ruso, los pronombres de sujeto plurales son мы (nosotros), вы (vosotros/ustedes/formal) y они (ellos/ellas).

text: In present tense Russian, the verb 'to be' (am / is / are) is {{c1::omitted / not used}}. Example: 'I am a doctor' is translated simply as '{{c2::Я врач}}'.
explanation: The present copula (есть) exists but is almost never used in simple identity statements. A dash is sometimes used in writing (e.g. Я — врач).
usage: Formula: Subject + Noun. Example: Я инженер. (I am an engineer.)
spanish: En el presente del ruso, el verbo 'ser/estar' se omite. 'Я врач' = Yo soy médico.

text: To negate a sentence in Russian, you place the particle '{{c1::не}}' (nye) directly before the word being negated. Example: I am not a student = {{c2::Я не студент}}.
explanation: Simple negation by placing 'не' before the noun, verb, or adjective.
usage: Example: Он не здесь. (He is not here.)
spanish: Para negar en ruso, se coloca la partícula 'не' directamente antes de la palabra. 'Я не студент' = No soy estudiante.

text: In Russian, the possessive pronouns for 'my' and 'your (informal)' in masculine form are {{c1::мой}} (moy) and {{c2::твой}} (tvoy).
explanation: Possessives must agree in gender with the noun they modify. (мой - masc, моя - fem, моё - neuter).
usage: Examples: мой дом (my house [masc]) | моя книга (my book [fem])
spanish: En ruso, los posesivos masculinos para 'mi' y 'tu' son мой y твой. Concuerdan en género con el sustantivo.

text: In Russian, the possessive pronouns for 'our' and 'your (formal/plural)' in masculine form are {{c1::наш}} (nash) and {{c2::ваш}} (vash).
explanation: They modify nouns and agree in gender (e.g. наш дом, наша страна).
usage: Examples: наш город (our city) | ваш паспорт (your passport)
spanish: En ruso, los posesivos masculinos para 'nuestro' y 'vuestro/su' son наш y ваш.

text: In Russian, the possessive pronouns for 'his', 'her', and 'their' are {{c1::его}} (yevo), {{c2::её}} (yeyo), and {{c3::их}} (ikh).
explanation: These possessive pronouns are invariable — they do not change according to the gender or number of the noun they modify.
usage: Examples: его книга (his book) | её дом (her house) | их дети (their children)
spanish: En ruso, los posesivos invariables 'su' (de él), 'su' (de ella) y 'su' (de ellos) son его, её y их.

text: In Russian, the pronoun 'me' in the phrase 'Menya zovut' (My name is) is '{{c1::меня}}' (menya), which is the accusative case of 'я'.
explanation: Literally means 'They call me...'.
usage: Term: меня | Example: Меня зовут Иван.
spanish: El pronombre 'меня' (menya) en 'Menya zovut' es el acusativo de я (yo). Significa 'A mí me llaman'.

text: In Russian, the question word for 'Who' is '{{c1::Кто}}' (Kto) and for 'What' is '{{c2::Что}}' (pronounced 'shto').
explanation: Note that 'Что' is written with 'ч' but pronounced as 'ш' (sh).
usage: Examples: Кто это? (Who is this?) | Что это? (What is this?)
spanish: En ruso, '¿Quién?' es 'Кто' y '¿Qué?' es 'Что' (se pronuncia 'shto').

text: In Russian, the phrase used to say 'this is / that is / these are' pointing to things is '{{c1::это}}' (eto).
explanation: An invariable demonstrative pronoun used for identification.
usage: Example: Это мой телефон. (This is my phone.) | Это наши друзья. (These are our friends.)
spanish: En ruso, la palabra invariable usada para señalar y decir 'esto es / eso es' es 'это' (eto).

text: To state location in Russian, use 'где' (where) followed by the subject. Present verb is omitted. Example: Where is the hotel? = {{c1::Где гостиница?}}
explanation: No verb 'to be' is needed in the present tense for location either.
usage: Dialogue: - Где метро? - Метро там. (- Where is the metro? - The metro is there.)
spanish: Para indicar ubicación en ruso, se usa 'где' seguido de la palabra. 'Где гостиница?' = ¿Dónde está el hotel?

text: In Russian, the formal/respectful pronoun for 'you' is '{{c1::Вы}}' (with capital 'В' in writing) and the informal is '{{c2::ты}}'.
explanation: Always use 'Вы' with teachers, doctors, customers, and strangers.
usage: Formal: Как Вы поживаете? (How are you? - formal)
spanish: El pronombre formal 'usted' en ruso es 'Вы' (con V mayúscula) y el informal 'tú' es 'ты'.

text: In Russian, the pronoun 'you' in the formal question 'Kak vas zovut?' (What is your name?) is '{{c1::вас}}' (vas), which is the accusative case of 'вы'.
explanation: Literally: 'How do they call you?'
usage: Term: вас
spanish: El pronombre 'вас' (vas) en 'Как вас зовут?' es el acusativo de вы.

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Reduction}} is defined as: - **Akanje**: Unstressed /o/ and /a/ both sound like [ɐ] or [ə] (e.g., *молоко* [məlɐˈko]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Vowel Reduction
Description: - **Akanje**: Unstressed /o/ and /a/ both sound like [ɐ] or [ə] (e.g., *молоко* [məlɐˈko]).
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Vowel Reduction' es: - **Akanje**: Unstressed /o/ and /a/ both sound like [ɐ] or [ə] (e.g., *молоко* [məlɐˈko]).

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Ikanje}} is defined as: Unstressed /e/ and /i/ merge into [ɪ].
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Ikanje
Description: Unstressed /e/ and /i/ merge into [ɪ].
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Ikanje' es: Unstressed /e/ and /i/ merge into [ɪ].

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The vowel /ɨ/ (ы)}} is defined as: A central close vowel that sounds like a retracted 'i'.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: The vowel /ɨ/ (ы)
Description: A central close vowel that sounds like a retracted 'i'.
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'The vowel /ɨ/ (ы)' es: A central close vowel that sounds like a retracted 'i'.

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Hard vs. Soft}} is defined as: The most important feature. Soft consonants are produced with the tongue raised toward the hard palate.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Hard vs. Soft
Description: The most important feature. Soft consonants are produced with the tongue raised toward the hard palate.
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Hard vs. Soft' es: The most important feature. Soft consonants are produced with the tongue raised toward the hard palate.

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Final-Devoicing}} is defined as: Voiced consonants become voiceless at the end of a word (e.g., *друг* [druk]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Final-Devoicing
Description: Voiced consonants become voiceless at the end of a word (e.g., *друг* [druk]).
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Final-Devoicing' es: Voiced consonants become voiceless at the end of a word (e.g., *друг* [druk]).

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Regressive Assimilation}} is defined as: Consonants often match the voicing of the following consonant (e.g., *вход* [f xot]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Regressive Assimilation
Description: Consonants often match the voicing of the following consonant (e.g., *вход* [f xot]).
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Regressive Assimilation' es: Consonants often match the voicing of the following consonant (e.g., *вход* [f xot]).

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Reduction (Akanje & Ikanje)}} is defined as: Unstressed vowels undergo extreme reduction:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Vowel Reduction (Akanje & Ikanje)
Description: Unstressed vowels undergo extreme reduction:
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Vowel Reduction (Akanje & Ikanje)' es: Unstressed vowels undergo extreme reduction:

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Akanje}} is defined as: Unstressed /o/ and /a/ are pronounced as [ɐ] in the syllable immediately preceding stress, and as [ə] in other unstressed syllables (e.g., *молоко* -> [məlɐˈko], *хорошо* -> [xərɐˈʂo]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Akanje
Description: Unstressed /o/ and /a/ are pronounced as [ɐ] in the syllable immediately preceding stress, and as [ə] in other unstressed syllables (e.g., *молоко* -> [məlɐˈko], *хорошо* -> [xərɐˈʂo]).
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Akanje' es: Unstressed /o/ and /a/ are pronounced as [ɐ] in the syllable immediately preceding stress, and as [ə] in other unstressed syllables (e.g., *молоко* -> [məlɐˈko], *хорошо* -> [xərɐˈʂo]).

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Ikanje}} is defined as: Unstressed /e/ and /i/ (and /a/ after soft consonants) merge and reduce to a short [ɪ] sound (e.g., *часы* -> [tɕɪˈsɨ]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Ikanje
Description: Unstressed /e/ and /i/ (and /a/ after soft consonants) merge and reduce to a short [ɪ] sound (e.g., *часы* -> [tɕɪˈsɨ]).
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Ikanje' es: Unstressed /e/ and /i/ (and /a/ after soft consonants) merge and reduce to a short [ɪ] sound (e.g., *часы* -> [tɕɪˈsɨ]).

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Final-Obstruent Devoicing}} is defined as: Voiced stops and fricatives become completely voiceless at the end of a word (e.g., *друг* -> [druk] (g -> k), *глаз* -> [glas] (z -> s), *хлеб* -> [xlʲep] (b -> p)).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Final-Obstruent Devoicing
Description: Voiced stops and fricatives become completely voiceless at the end of a word (e.g., *друг* -> [druk] (g -> k), *глаз* -> [glas] (z -> s), *хлеб* -> [xlʲep] (b -> p)).
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Final-Obstruent Devoicing' es: Voiced stops and fricatives become completely voiceless at the end of a word (e.g., *друг* -> [druk] (g -> k), *глаз* -> [glas] (z -> s), *хлеб* -> [xlʲep] (b -> p)).

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Regressive Voicing Assimilation}} is defined as: A consonant matches the voicing of the consonant directly following it:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Regressive Voicing Assimilation
Description: A consonant matches the voicing of the consonant directly following it:
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Regressive Voicing Assimilation' es: A consonant matches the voicing of the consonant directly following it:

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Voicing}} is defined as: Voiceless becomes voiced before voiced consonants (except /v/) (e.g., *сделать* -> [ˈzʲdʲelətʲ] (s -> z)).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Voicing
Description: Voiceless becomes voiced before voiced consonants (except /v/) (e.g., *сделать* -> [ˈzʲdʲelətʲ] (s -> z)).
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Voicing' es: Voiceless becomes voiced before voiced consonants (except /v/) (e.g., *сделать* -> [ˈzʲdʲelətʲ] (s -> z)).

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Devoicing}} is defined as: Voiced becomes voiceless before voiceless consonants (e.g., *вход* -> [fxot] (v -> f), *водка* -> [ˈvotkə] (d -> t)).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Devoicing
Description: Voiced becomes voiceless before voiceless consonants (e.g., *вход* -> [fxot] (v -> f), *водка* -> [ˈvotkə] (d -> t)).
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Devoicing' es: Voiced becomes voiceless before voiceless consonants (e.g., *вход* -> [fxot] (v -> f), *водка* -> [ˈvotkə] (d -> t)).

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Consonant Cluster Simplification}} is defined as: Complex clusters drop sounds in rapid speech for ease of articulation:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Consonant Cluster Simplification
Description: Complex clusters drop sounds in rapid speech for ease of articulation:
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Consonant Cluster Simplification' es: Complex clusters drop sounds in rapid speech for ease of articulation:

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Word Stress}} is defined as: Highly unpredictable and mobile. Stress can shift when a word is declined or conjugated.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Word Stress
Description: Highly unpredictable and mobile. Stress can shift when a word is declined or conjugated.
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Word Stress' es: Highly unpredictable and mobile. Stress can shift when a word is declined or conjugated.

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Phonemic Stress}} is defined as: Stress can distinguish meaning (e.g., *за́мок* "castle" vs *замо́к* "lock").
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Phonemic Stress
Description: Stress can distinguish meaning (e.g., *за́мок* "castle" vs *замо́к* "lock").
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Phonemic Stress' es: Stress can distinguish meaning (e.g., *за́мок* "castle" vs *замо́к* "lock").

text: In Russian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Sentence Intonation}} is defined as: Russian has several distinct intonation constructions (IK) for different sentence types.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Russian.
usage:
Rule: Sentence Intonation
Description: Russian has several distinct intonation constructions (IK) for different sentence types.
spanish: En la fonética de Russian, la regla de 'Sentence Intonation' es: Russian has several distinct intonation constructions (IK) for different sentence types.

text: What is the Russian pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "Khorosho" Vowel Reduction}}? Never pronounce "хорошо" as "ho-ro-sho". Follow the Akanje rule: the first 'o' is a tiny grunt [ə], the second 'o' is a short 'a' [ɐ], and only the last 'o' is a full 'o' [o] because it is stressed: say "xə-ra-shó".
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Russian.
usage:
Tip: The "Khorosho" Vowel Reduction
Never pronounce "хорошо" as "ho-ro-sho". Follow the Akanje rule: the first 'o' is a tiny grunt [ə], the second 'o' is a short 'a' [ɐ], and only the last 'o' is a full 'o' [o] because it is stressed: say "xə-ra-shó".
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Russian para 'The "Khorosho" Vowel Reduction'? Never pronounce "хорошо" as "ho-ro-sho". Follow the Akanje rule: the first 'o' is a tiny grunt [ə], the second 'o' is a short 'a' [ɐ], and only the last 'o' is a full 'o' [o] because it is stressed: say "xə-ra-shó".

text: What is the Russian pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The Silent Letters}}? In long Russian words like "здравствуйте" or "солнце", don't try to pronounce every letter. Drop the silent consonants ('в' and 'л' respectively) to sound like a native.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Russian.
usage:
Tip: The Silent Letters
In long Russian words like "здравствуйте" or "солнце", don't try to pronounce every letter. Drop the silent consonants ('в' and 'л' respectively) to sound like a native.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Russian para 'The Silent Letters'? In long Russian words like "здравствуйте" or "солнце", don't try to pronounce every letter. Drop the silent consonants ('в' and 'л' respectively) to sound like a native.

text: In Russian phonetics, unstressed vowels undergo **reduction**. For example, the letter 'о' when unstressed is pronounced like {{c1::а}} (/a/ or /ə/). E.g. *хорошо* (well) is pronounced {{c2::/xərɐˈʂo/}} (khara-sho).
explanation: Vowel reduction is key to a native Russian accent. Only the stressed 'о' gets a full 'o' sound.
usage: Rule: Unstressed O reduction. Example: молоко (milk) -> pronounced /malakó/.
spanish: En la fonética rusa, las vocales átonas se reducen. La letra 'о' sin acento prosódico se pronuncia como una 'а'. Ej. 'хорошо' se pronuncia /xərɐˈʂo/ (jara-shó).

explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.

spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good morning' en Spanish? Buenos días

text: In Spanish, how do you say 'Good afternoon'? {{c1::Buenas tardes}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Buenas tardes | Meaning: Good afternoon
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good afternoon' en Spanish? Buenas tardes

text: In Spanish, how do you say 'Good evening / Good night'? {{c1::Buenas noches}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Buenas noches | Meaning: Good evening / Good night
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good evening / Good night' en Spanish? Buenas noches

explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Hola | Meaning: Hello
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hello' en Spanish? Hola

text: In Spanish, how do you say 'How are you? - Informal'? {{c1::¿Cómo estás?}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: ¿Cómo estás? | Meaning: How are you? - Informal
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'How are you? - Informal' en Spanish? ¿Cómo estás?

text: In Spanish, how do you say 'Goodbye'? {{c1::Adiós}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Adiós | Meaning: Goodbye
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Goodbye' en Spanish? Adiós

text: In Spanish, how do you say 'See you later'? {{c1::Hasta luego}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Hasta luego | Meaning: See you later
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'See you later' en Spanish? Hasta luego

text: In Spanish, how do you say 'Hello / Hi'? {{c1::Hola}}
explanation: The silent 'h' is never pronounced in Spanish.
usage: Term: Hola | Dialogue: - ¡Hola! ¿Qué tal?
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hello / Hi' en español? Hola.

text: In Spanish, how do you say 'Please'? {{c1::Por favor}}
explanation: Universal polite formula.
usage: Term: Por favor | Example: Un café, por favor.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Please' en español? Por favor.

text: In Spanish, how do you say 'Thank you very much'? {{c1::Muchas gracias}}
explanation: Textbook and conversational standard.
usage: Term: Muchas gracias
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Thank you very much' en español? Muchas gracias.

text: In Spanish, how do you say 'You're welcome'? {{c1::De nada}}
explanation: Response to gracias. Literally 'of nothing'.
usage: Dialogue: - ¡Gracias! - De nada.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'You're welcome' en español? De nada.

text: In Spanish, the question to ask someone their name is '{{c1::¿Cómo te llamas?}}' and the response is '{{c2::Me llamo}} [Name]'.
explanation: Reflexive verb construction (llamarse). Literally: 'How do you call yourself?'.
usage: Dialogue: - ¿Cómo te llamas? - Me llamo Jesus.
spanish: ¿Cómo se pregunta el nombre y se responde en español? ¿Cómo te llamas? / Me llamo...

text: In Spanish, how do you say 'Nice to meet you'? {{c1::Mucho gusto}} or {{c2::Encantado}}.
explanation: 'Encantado' changes to 'Encantada' depending on the speaker's gender.
usage: Dialogue: - Soy Marta. - Mucho gusto, soy Jorge.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Nice to meet you' en español? Mucho gusto / Encantado.

text: In Spanish, how do you ask 'How are you?' informally? {{c1::¿Cómo estás?}} or {{c2::¿Qué tal?}}.
explanation: '¿Qué tal?' is very common and casual.
usage: Dialogue: - Hola, ¿qué tal? - Bien, ¿y tú?
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'How are you?' en español? ¿Cómo estás? / ¿Qué tal?.

text: In Spanish, how do you say 'See you tomorrow'? {{c1::Hasta mañana}}
explanation: Used when parting from someone you will see the next day.
usage: Term: Hasta mañana
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'See you tomorrow' en español? Hasta mañana.

text: In Spanish, a very common informal greeting meaning 'How's it going? / What's up?' is '{{c1::¿Qué tal?}}'.
explanation: Can be used alone or combined with 'Hola'.
usage: Dialogue: - ¡Hola! ¿Qué tal? - Muy bien, gracias.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'How's it going? / What's up?' en español? ¿Qué tal?.

text: In Spanish, how do you say 'Nice to meet you' upon introductions? {{c1::Mucho gusto}}
explanation: Universal and gender-neutral introduction phrase.
usage: Dialogue: - Soy Jorge. - Mucho gusto.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Nice to meet you' en español? Mucho gusto.

text: In Spanish, how do you say 'See you / We'll see each other' casually? {{c1::Nos vemos}}
explanation: Extremely common informal parting phrase.
usage: Term: Nos vemos | Example: Chao, nos vemos más tarde.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Nos vemos' en español? Nos vemos.

text: In Spanish, how do you say 'Have a nice day!' upon parting? {{c1::¡Que tengas un buen día!}}
explanation: Subjunctive mood used to wish someone a good day.
usage: Term: ¡Que tengas un buen día!
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¡Que tengas un buen día!' en español? ¡Que tengas un buen día!.

text: In Spanish, how do you say 'What's new? / What's up?' casually? {{c1::¿Qué hay?}}
explanation: An informal way to ask for updates or greet a friend.
usage: Dialogue: - ¿Qué hay, amigo? - Nada nuevo, ¿y tú?
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Qué hay?' en español? ¿Qué hay?.

scenario: Spanish A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Spanish, how do you say 'I am'? {{c1::Yo soy / estoy}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Yo soy / estoy | Meaning: I am
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'I am' en Spanish? Yo soy / estoy

scenario: Spanish A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Spanish, how do you say 'You are'? {{c1::Tú eres / estás}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Tú eres / estás | Meaning: You are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'You are' en Spanish? Tú eres / estás

scenario: Spanish A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Spanish, how do you say 'He/She/You(formal) is/are'? {{c1::Él/Ella/Usted es / está}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Él/Ella/Usted es / está | Meaning: He/She/You(formal) is/are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'He/She/You(formal) is/are' en Spanish? Él/Ella/Usted es / está

scenario: Spanish A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Spanish, how do you say 'We are'? {{c1::Nosotros somos / estamos}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Nosotros somos / estamos | Meaning: We are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'We are' en Spanish? Nosotros somos / estamos

scenario: Spanish A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Spanish, how do you say 'They are'? {{c1::Ellos son / están}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: Ellos son / están | Meaning: They are
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'They are' en Spanish? Ellos son / están

text: In Spanish, the subject pronouns for 'I' and 'you (informal singular)' are {{c1::yo}} and {{c2::tú}}.
explanation: Subject pronouns are often omitted in Spanish because the verb conjugation clearly defines who is acting.
usage: Examples: Yo soy médico. | ¿Tú estás listo?
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'I' y 'you' en español? yo / tú.

text: In Spanish, the singular subject pronouns for 'he' and 'she' are {{c1::él}} and {{c2::ella}}.
explanation: Note the accent on 'él' to distinguish it from the masculine article 'el' (the).
usage: Examples: Él es de México. | Ella está en casa.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'he' y 'she' en español? él / ella.

text: In Spanish, the subject pronoun for 'we' is {{c1::nosotros}} (masculine/mixed) and {{c2::nosotras}} (all-female).
explanation: Spanish pronoun endings match the gender of the group.
usage: Examples: Nosotros somos ingenieros. | Nosotras estamos cansadas.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'we' en español? nosotros / nosotras.

text: In Spanish, the subject pronouns for 'they' are {{c1::ellos}} (masc/mixed) and {{c2::ellas}} (all-female).
explanation: Like 'nosotros', they agree in gender.
usage: Examples: Ellos son estudiantes. | Ellas están aquí.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'they' en español? ellos / ellas.

text: In Spanish, conjugate the permanent verb 'ser' (to be) in the present tense for 'yo' and 'tú': yo {{c1::soy}}, tú {{c2::eres}}.
explanation: Ser is used for identity, occupation, origin, time, and permanent traits.
usage: Examples: Yo soy Jesus. | Tú eres muy alto.
spanish: ¿Cómo se conjuga 'ser' para 'yo' y 'tú' en presente? soy / eres.

text: Conjugate the verb 'ser' (to be) in the present tense for 'él/ella' (he/she) and 'nosotros' (we): él/ella {{c1::es}}, nosotros {{c2::somos}}.
explanation: Simple present forms of ser.
usage: Examples: Ella es ingeniera. | Nosotros somos amigos.
spanish: ¿Cómo se conjuga 'ser' para 'él/ella' y 'nosotros' en presente? es / somos.

text: Conjugate the verb 'ser' (to be) in the present tense for 'ellos/ellas' (they): ellos/ellas {{c1::son}}.
explanation: Third-person plural form of ser.
usage: Example: Ellos son de Madrid.
spanish: ¿Cómo se conjuga 'ser' para 'ellos/ellas' en presente? son.

text: In Spanish, conjugate the temporary verb 'estar' (to be) in the present tense for 'yo' and 'tú': yo {{c1::estoy}}, tú {{c2::estás}}.
explanation: Estar is used for locations, temporary physical/mental states, and ongoing actions.
usage: Examples: Yo estoy cansado. | ¿Tú estás en la oficina?
spanish: ¿Cómo se conjuga 'estar' para 'yo' y 'tú' en presente? estoy / estás.

text: Conjugate the verb 'estar' (to be) in the present tense for 'él/ella' (he/she) and 'nosotros' (we): él/ella {{c1::está}}, nosotros {{c2::estamos}}.
explanation: Note the written accent mark on 'está'.
usage: Examples: Él está enfermo. | Nosotros estamos listos.
spanish: ¿Cómo se conjuga 'estar' para 'él/ella' y 'nosotros' en presente? está / estamos.

text: Conjugate the verb 'estar' (to be) in the present tense for 'ellos/ellas' (they): ellos/ellas {{c1::están}}.
explanation: Carries a written accent mark.
usage: Example: Ellos están contentos.
spanish: ¿Cómo se conjuga 'estar' para 'ellos/ellas' en presente? están.

text: In Spanish, the formal pronoun for 'you (singular)' is {{c1::usted}} and for 'you (plural)' is {{c2::ustedes}}.
explanation: Both conjugate with the third-person verb forms (e.g. usted es, ustedes son).
usage: Examples: ¿Cómo está usted? | Ustedes son bienvenidos.
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'you (formal)' y 'you (plural)' en español? usted / ustedes.

text: To negate a sentence in Spanish, you place the word '{{c1::no}}' directly before the verb. Example: I am not tired = {{c2::No estoy cansado}}.
explanation: The standard negative particle is 'no'.
usage: Example: Él no es profesor.
spanish: Para negar una frase en español, se coloca 'no' antes del verbo. 'No estoy cansado' = No estoy cansado.

text: In Spain, the informal plural pronoun for 'you all' is {{c1::vosotros}} (masc/mixed) or {{c2::vosotras}} (fem). In Latin America, it is replaced by {{c3::ustedes}}.
explanation: Vosotros has its own verb conjugations (e.g., vosotros sois, vosotros estáis).
usage: Spain: ¿Vosotros sois de aquí? | LatAm: ¿Ustedes son de aquí?
spanish: ¿Cuáles son los pronombres de segunda persona plural informal en España y América Latina? vosotros/as / ustedes.

text: In Spanish, the singular possessive adjectives for 'my', 'your', and 'his/her/your (formal)' are {{c1::mi}}, {{c2::tu}}, and {{c3::su}}.
explanation: They change to plural if the noun they modify is plural (e.g., mis libros, tus llaves, sus casas).
usage: Examples: mi perro | tu gato | su carro
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'my', 'your', 'his/her' en adjetivos posesivos en español? mi / tu / su.

text: In Spanish, the demonstrative adjectives for 'this' (masc/fem) are {{c1::este / esta}} and for 'that' (near listener) are {{c2::ese / esa}}.
explanation: They must agree in gender with the noun they modify.
usage: Examples: este libro | esta mesa | ese coche
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'this' y 'that' en adjetivos demostrativos en español? este/esta / ese/esa.

text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Monophthongs}} is defined as: Spanish vowels are short, crisp, and do not reduce to a schwa in unstressed positions.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
usage:
Rule: Monophthongs
Description: Spanish vowels are short, crisp, and do not reduce to a schwa in unstressed positions.
spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Monophthongs' es: Spanish vowels are short, crisp, and do not reduce to a schwa in unstressed positions.

text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Diphthongs}} is defined as: Created by combining a "strong" vowel (/a, e, o/) with a "weak" vowel (/i, u/), e.g., *aire* /ajɾe/, *huevo* /weβo/.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
usage:
Rule: Diphthongs
Description: Created by combining a "strong" vowel (/a, e, o/) with a "weak" vowel (/i, u/), e.g., *aire* /ajɾe/, *huevo* /weβo/.
spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Diphthongs' es: Created by combining a "strong" vowel (/a, e, o/) with a "weak" vowel (/i, u/), e.g., *aire* /ajɾe/, *huevo* /weβo/.

text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Hiatus}} is defined as: When two strong vowels meet, they form separate syllables (e.g., *teatro* /te.a.tɾo/).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
usage:
Rule: Hiatus
Description: When two strong vowels meet, they form separate syllables (e.g., *teatro* /te.a.tɾo/).
spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Hiatus' es: When two strong vowels meet, they form separate syllables (e.g., *teatro* /te.a.tɾo/).

text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Lenition of /b, d, ɡ/}} is defined as: Between vowels, these plosives become approximants [β, ð, ɣ] (e.g., *cada* [ˈkaða]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
usage:
Rule: Lenition of /b, d, ɡ/
Description: Between vowels, these plosives become approximants [β, ð, ɣ] (e.g., *cada* [ˈkaða]).
spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Lenition of /b, d, ɡ/' es: Between vowels, these plosives become approximants [β, ð, ɣ] (e.g., *cada* [ˈkaða]).

text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Rhotics}} is defined as: Distinction between the alveolar tap /ɾ/ (*pero*) and the alveolar trill /r/ (*perro*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
usage:
Rule: The Rhotics
Description: Distinction between the alveolar tap /ɾ/ (*pero*) and the alveolar trill /r/ (*perro*).
